topics.title: Familiarisation of various systems and subsystems of petrol engine / MPFI engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL609 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-2
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation of various systems and subsystems of petrol engine / MPFI engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Petrol Engine (SI Engine)?

A **Petrol Engine** is an internal combustion engine (ICE) that operates on the **Otto Cycle**, using petrol (gasoline) as the primary fuel. The combustion process is initiated by an external spark plug, classifying it as a **Spark Ignition (SI) engine**. The fundamental principle involves the conversion of chemical energy stored in the fuel-air mixture into mechanical work through controlled combustion within a cylinder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A spark-ignition (SI) engine is a positive-displacement internal combustion engine in which a compressed mixture of air and fuel is ignited by a timed electric spark delivered across a spark plug gap. The combustion products expand, producing useful work on a reciprocating piston.

### 1.2 What is an MPFI Engine?

**MPFI** stands for **Multi-Point Fuel Injection**. It is an advanced fuel delivery system for petrol engines in which fuel is injected by individual injectors directly into the **intake port of each cylinder**, just upstream of the intake valve. This is a significant upgrade over older systems like the **SPFI (Single-Point Fuel Injection)** or **Carburettor**, where fuel was centrally mixed with air before distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **MPFI vs Carburettor:** In a carburettor, air and fuel mix at a central point using the Venturi effect. In MPFI, injection occurs at multiple points, one per cylinder, ensuring **equal air-fuel ratio distribution** across all cylinders.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a petrol engine as a **team of synchronized athletes (cylinders) running a relay race**:
- The **fuel tank** is the energy reservoir (like the baton of energy).
- The **fuel pump** acts as the "assistant" who hands the fuel baton to each runner.
- The **injector (in MPFI)** is like a precision coach delivering the exact required amount of fuel to each athlete right before they start.
- The **spark plug** is the starter's pistol — it gives the precise signal to begin the combustion.
- The **piston** is the runner who converts this energy burst into forward motion.
- The **cooling system** is the support staff with water bottles preventing the runner from overheating.
- The **lubrication system** ensures all moving joints run smoothly without friction damage.

### 1.4 Primary Systems of a Petrol / MPFI Engine

A modern petrol engine is composed of several **interlocking subsystems**, each performing a dedicated function. For the KTU 2024 lab examination, students must be able to **identify, locate, and explain the function** of each subsystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven Major Subsystems of an MPFI Petrol Engine:**
> 1. **Fuel Supply System** (Tank → Pump → Filter → Injectors → Pressure Regulator)
> 2. **Air Intake / Induction System** (Air Filter → Throttle Body → Intake Manifold → Individual Runners)
> 3. **Ignition System** (Battery → Ignition Coil → Distributor/ECU → Spark Plugs)
> 4. **Lubrication System** (Sump → Oil Pump → Filter → Galleries → Bearings)
> 5. **Cooling System** (Water Jacket → Water Pump → Radiator → Thermostat → Fan)
> 6. **Exhaust System** (Manifold → Catalytic Converter → Silencer/Muffler)
> 7. **Engine Management System** (ECU, Sensors, Actuators)

### 1.5 Basic Operating Cycle Terminology

| Term | Definition | Stroke Range |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Intake Stroke** | Piston moves down, intake valve opens, fresh charge enters | TDC to BDC |
| **Compression Stroke** | Piston moves up, both valves close, charge is compressed | BDC to TDC |
| **Power / Expansion Stroke** | Combustion occurs, gases expand, piston pushed down | TDC to BDC |
| **Exhaust Stroke** | Piston moves up, exhaust valve opens, burnt gases expelled | BDC to TDC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Four-Stroke Engine:** A complete working cycle is completed in **two crankshaft revolutions (720°)** across four distinct piston strokes. This is the standard configuration in modern MPFI passenger car engines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Subsystem 1: Fuel Supply System (MPFI)

**Function:** To deliver precisely metered fuel at a controlled pressure to each cylinder's intake port at the correct timing.

**Major Components:**

1. **Fuel Tank** — Storage reservoir, typically mounted at the rear of the vehicle, made of high-density polyethylene (HDPE) or stamped steel.
2. **Fuel Pump** — Electrically driven (12 V DC), submerged inside the fuel tank. Generates pressure typically between **2.5 bar to 3.5 bar (250–350 kPa)**.
3. **Fuel Filter** — Removes particulates and water; recommended replacement every 20,000–40,000 km.
4. **Fuel Rail (Common Rail)** — A tubular manifold that distributes equal fuel pressure to all injectors. Contains a **fuel pressure regulator** maintaining constant pressure differential.
5. **Fuel Pressure Regulator** — Maintains constant fuel pressure relative to manifold vacuum. Excess fuel returns to the tank via a return line.
6. **Fuel Injectors** — Electromagnetic solenoids (or piezo-electric in advanced systems) that atomize fuel through multiple small orifices when energized by the ECU.
7. **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** — Provides feedback to ECU regarding driver demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle of MPFI Injector:** The ECU sends a precisely-timed current pulse to the injector's solenoid coil. This creates a magnetic field that lifts the needle valve off its seat, allowing pressurized fuel to spray into the intake port for a controlled **injection duration (in milliseconds)**. The quantity of fuel is proportional to this pulse width.

### 2.2 Subsystem 2: Air Intake / Induction System

**Function:** To filter, meter, and deliver clean ambient air into each cylinder in the correct proportion to fuel.

**Major Components:**
- **Air Filter** — Paper or cotton element removing dust > **5 µm**.
- **Mass Air Flow (MAF) Sensor** — Measures the mass (in kg/s) of incoming air.
- **Throttle Body** — Contains the throttle valve (butterfly valve) controlled by accelerator pedal via **Drive-by-Wire (DBW)** or mechanical linkage.
- **Intake Manifold** — Distributes air to individual cylinders; often **tuned-length** for resonance effects.
- **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)** — Bypasses throttle to control idle RPM.

### 2.3 Subsystem 3: Ignition System

**Function:** To generate and deliver a high-voltage spark (typically **20,000 to 40,000 V**) across the spark plug gap at precisely the right moment to ignite the compressed air-fuel mixture.

**Major Components:**
- **Battery (12 V)** — Source of low-voltage DC.
- **Ignition Coil** — Step-up transformer converting 12 V to ~30,000 V via electromagnetic induction.
- **Distributor (older systems)** — Mechanically routes high voltage to correct cylinder. *Note: Modern MPFI engines use **Distributorless Ignition System (DIS)** with **waste spark** or **coil-on-plug (COP)** technology.*
- **Spark Plugs** — Electrode gap typically **0.7 to 1.1 mm**; rated by heat range.
- **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP) & Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP)** — Provide timing reference to ECU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Distributorless Ignition?** Eliminating the mechanical distributor removes moving parts subject to wear, prevents voltage losses across the rotor gap, and allows **individual cylinder timing optimization** — improving efficiency by **3–5%** and reducing emissions.

### 2.4 Subsystem 4: Lubrication System

**Function:** To reduce friction between moving parts, dissipate heat, clean components, and prevent corrosion.

**Major Components:**
- **Oil Sump (Pan)** — Reservoir at engine base.
- **Oil Pump** — Usually gear-type or rotor-type, driven by crankshaft.
- **Oil Filter** — Full-flow type, replaceable cartridge.
- **Pressure Relief Valve** — Limits maximum pressure to ~**4 bar (400 kPa)**.
- **Oil Galleries** — Internal drilled passages.
- **Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** — Removes blow-by gases.

**Lubrication Methods:**
- **Splash Lubrication** — Connecting rod dips into oil.
- **Pressure Lubrication** — Pump-forced oil to bearings.
- **Splash + Pressure (Mist Lubrication)** — Combination used in most modern engines.

### 2.5 Subsystem 5: Cooling System

**Function:** To maintain optimal engine operating temperature between **85°C to 95°C**.

**Types:** Air-cooled (older/light applications) and **Liquid-cooled** (modern cars).

**Major Components:**
- **Water Jacket** — Hollow passages around cylinders cast into the block.
- **Water Pump** — Centrifugal, belt-driven.
- **Thermostat** — Wax-pellet type; opens at **82°C ± 2°C**.
- **Radiator** — Finned aluminum/ brass core for heat exchange.
- **Cooling Fan** — Electric (ECU-controlled) or mechanical (viscous clutch).
- **Expansion Tank** — Allows for thermal expansion of coolant.

### 2.6 Subsystem 6: Exhaust System

**Function:** To expel burnt gases, reduce noise, and treat pollutants before atmospheric discharge.

**Major Components:**
- **Exhaust Manifold** — Collects gases from multiple cylinders; often **log-type** or **tuned tubular**.
- **Catalytic Converter** — Three-way catalyst (TWC): converts **CO → CO₂**, **HC → CO₂ + H₂O**, **NOₓ → N₂ + O₂**.
- **Oxygen Sensor (Lambda Sensor)** — Pre- and post-catalyst; provides feedback for closed-loop air-fuel control.
- **Silencer / Muffler** — Attenuates exhaust noise.
- **Resonator** — Cancels specific frequency noise.

### 2.7 Subsystem 7: Engine Management System (EMS) / ECU

**Function:** Centralized electronic control over fuel injection, ignition timing, idle speed, and emissions based on sensor inputs.

**Key Sensors:**
- MAF, MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure), TPS, CKP, CMP, O₂, CTS (Coolant Temperature Sensor), IAT (Intake Air Temperature).

**Key Actuators:**
- Fuel Injectors, Ignition Coils, Throttle Body, IACV, EGR Valve, Fuel Pump Relay.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (SI Engine Fundamentals)

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $r_k = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | Compression Ratio | dimensionless |
| $\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_k^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Ideal Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency | dimensionless |
| $\eta_{th,actual} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | Actual Thermal Efficiency | dimensionless |
| $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} = 1 - \dfrac{FP}{IP}$ | Mechanical Efficiency | dimensionless |
| $BSFC = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{BP \cdot t}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | kg/kWh |
| $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{BP}{BSFC}$ | Fuel Mass Flow Rate | kg/h |
| $BSFC_{ideal} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{th} \cdot CV}$ | Theoretical minimum BSFC | kg/kWh |
| $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Relative Air-Fuel Ratio (Lambda) | dimensionless |
| $(A/F)_{stoich,petrol} \approx 14.7:1$ | Stoichiometric AFR for petrol | dimensionless |
| $P_{injector} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{N_{cyl} \cdot n_{inj/sec}}$ | Per-injector fuel demand | kg/s |
| $\Delta P_{rail} = P_{fuel} - P_{manifold}$ | Fuel Rail Pressure Differential | kPa |
| $E_{ignition} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Stored energy in ignition coil capacitor | Joules |
| $H_g = \dfrac{1}{2} (V_s - V_c)$ | Half-stroke (geometric) | m |
| $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L$ | Swept Volume per cylinder | m³ |
| $V_1 = V_c + V_s$ | Total Volume (BDC) | m³ |

Where: $V_1$ = volume at BDC, $V_2$ = volume at TDC, $\gamma$ ≈ **1.4** for air, $V_s$ = swept volume, $V_c$ = clearance volume, $D$ = bore, $L$ = stroke length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The compression ratio $r_k$ is the **most frequently asked** parameter in SI engine problems. Typical MPFI petrol engines operate at $r_k$ = **9:1 to 12:1**. Higher $r_k$ improves thermal efficiency but risks **knocking** (uncontrolled auto-ignition).

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive Industry:** MPFI is the default fuel system in 99% of petrol cars (BS-VI / Euro 6 compliant) due to precise emissions control.
- **Motorcycle Engines:** Single-cylinder or multi-cylinder MPFI systems (e.g., Hero, Bajaj, Honda).
- **Marine Outboards:** Some two-stroke outboards use variations of MPFI.
- **Aerospace:** Light-aircraft engines use MPFI derivatives.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency

The Otto cycle is the **idealized thermodynamic cycle** for a spark-ignition (SI) engine. It consists of four reversible processes:

1. **Process 1-2:** Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression
2. **Process 2-3:** Constant-volume heat addition
3. **Process 3-4:** Isentropic expansion (power stroke)
4. **Process 4-1:** Constant-volume heat rejection

**Starting Point — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics for the cycle:**

For a closed system undergoing a complete cycle, the **net work done equals net heat transfer**:

$$
W_{net} = Q_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}
$$

**Step 1: Determine heat added (Process 2-3, constant volume):**

The heat added occurs at constant volume, where the working fluid is the air-fuel mixture. Using the ideal gas relation $Q = m \cdot c_v \cdot \Delta T$:

$$
Q_{in} = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2)
$$

**Step 2: Determine heat rejected (Process 4-1, constant volume):**

Similarly, heat rejection at constant volume:

$$
Q_{out} = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_4 - T_1)
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the thermal efficiency expression:**

Thermal efficiency is defined as the ratio of net work output to heat input:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_4 - T_1)}{m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

**Step 4: Apply isentropic relations for processes 1-2 and 3-4:**

For an isentropic process in an ideal gas: $T \cdot V^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$.

For process 1-2 (isentropic compression):

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r_k^{\gamma-1}
$$

For process 3-4 (isentropic expansion):

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r_k^{\gamma-1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{T_3}{T_4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_k^{\gamma-1} \quad \text{and} \quad T_3 = T_4 \cdot r_k^{\gamma-1}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute to express temperature differences:**

From the above, we can write:

$$
T_3 - T_2 = T_4 \cdot r_k^{\gamma-1} - T_1 \cdot r_k^{\gamma-1} = (T_4 - T_1) \cdot r_k^{\gamma-1}
$$

**Step 6: Final substitution into efficiency expression:**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{(T_4 - T_1) \cdot r_k^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_k^{\gamma-1}}
$$

**Final Result — Otto Cycle Efficiency:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_k^{\gamma-1}}}
$$

**Numerical Verification Example:**

For a typical MPFI engine with $r_k = 10$ and $\gamma = 1.4$:

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{10^{1.4-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{10^{0.4}}
$$

$$
10^{0.4} = 10^{2/5} = \sqrt[5]{100} \approx 2.512
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.512} = 1 - 0.398 = 0.602 \quad \text{or} \quad 60.2\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Engine:** The actual brake thermal efficiency of an MPFI petrol engine is around **25–35%**, much lower than the Otto ideal value of 60.2%. The difference arises from: (i) mechanical friction, (ii) incomplete combustion, (iii) heat loss to cooling, (iv) finite-time processes, and (v) pumping losses.

### 3.2 Derivation: Indicated Power (IP) for a 4-Stroke Engine

For a 4-stroke MPFI engine, each cylinder fires **once every two revolutions**. Therefore, the indicated power is calculated per cylinder and summed.

**Step 1: Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP):**

$$
IMEP = \frac{W_{indicated}}{V_s} \quad [\text{units: kPa or bar}]
$$

**Step 2: Indicated Power per cylinder:**

$$
IP_{cyl} = \frac{IMEP \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N}{2 \cdot 60 \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{units: kW}]
$$

where:
- $L$ = stroke length (m)
- $A$ = piston area (m²) = $\frac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- $N$ = engine speed (RPM)
- The factor **2** accounts for the 4-stroke cycle (1 firing per 2 revolutions).
- The factor **1000** converts W to kW.

**Step 3: Total Indicated Power (multi-cylinder):**

$$
IP_{total} = \frac{IMEP \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n_{cyl}}{2 \cdot 60 \cdot 1000}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: BSFC Relation with Thermal Efficiency

**Step 1: Brakes Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) definition:**

$$
BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{BP} \quad [\text{kg/kWh}]
$$

**Step 2: Expressing BP using heat input:**

The power output equals the rate of heat input times thermal efficiency:

$$
BP = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot CV \cdot \eta_{brake\,th}
$$

where $CV$ (Calorific Value) of petrol ≈ **44,000 kJ/kg** or **44 MJ/kg**.

**Step 3: Substituting into BSFC:**

$$
BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot CV \cdot \eta_{brake\,th}} = \frac{1}{CV \cdot \eta_{brake\,th}}
$$

**Step 4: Ideal (minimum) BSFC for petrol:**

$$
BSFC_{ideal} = \frac{1}{44{,}000 \cdot 0.35} = 0.0000649 \text{ kg/kJ}
$$

Converting units (1 kJ/s = 1 kW, so per hour: multiply by 3600):

$$
BSFC_{ideal} = 0.0000649 \times 3600 = 0.2336 \text{ kg/kWh}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical BSFC range** for modern MPFI engines: **0.25 to 0.30 kg/kWh**. Lower BSFC = more efficient engine.

### 3.4 Derivation: Air-Fuel Ratio for Stoichiometric Combustion of Petrol

Assume petrol is approximated as **iso-octane (C₈H₁₈)**.

**Combustion Equation:**

$$
C_8H_{18} + a \left(O_2 + 3.76 N_2\right) \rightarrow b CO_2 + c H_2O + d N_2
$$

**Step 1: Carbon balance:** $b = 8$
**Step 2: Hydrogen balance:** $2c = 18 \Rightarrow c = 9$
**Step 3: Oxygen balance:** $2a = 2b + c = 16 + 9 = 25 \Rightarrow a = 12.5$
**Step 4: Nitrogen balance:** $d = 3.76 \times 12.5 = 47$

**Mass Analysis (per kg of fuel):**

Molar mass of C₈H₁₈ = $8(12) + 18(1) = 114$ kg/kmol.
Moles of O₂ required per mole of fuel = 12.5.
Molar mass of O₂ = 32 kg/kmol.
Moles of air = $12.5 \times (1 + 3.76) = 12.5 \times 4.76 = 59.5$ kmol air.
Molar mass of air = **28.97 kg/kmol**.

**Mass of air required:**

$$
m_{air} = 59.5 \times 28.97 = 1723.7 \text{ kg}
$$

**Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis):**

$$
(AFR)_{stoich} = \frac{1723.7}{114} = 15.12
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The standard textbook value of **14.7:1** is an empirical average for typical gasoline blends (not pure iso-octane). For KTU problems, **14.7 ± 0.2** is acceptable.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Component → Function → Failure Mode

| Subsystem | Key Component | Primary Function | Common Failure Mode |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Fuel System | Electric Fuel Pump | Pressurize fuel to 3 bar | Pump motor burnout, no-start |
| Fuel System | Injector | Atomize fuel | Clogging, drip/leakage |
| Fuel System | Pressure Regulator | Maintain ΔP constant | Diaphragm tear, rich/lean mix |
| Intake | MAF Sensor | Measure air mass | Contamination, false readings |
| Intake | Throttle Body | Meter airflow | Carbon buildup, stalling |
| Ignition | Ignition Coil | Step up 12V → 30kV | Insulation breakdown |
| Ignition | Spark Plug | Initiate combustion | Fouling, gap erosion |
| Ignition | CKP Sensor | Provide timing reference | Hall sensor failure |
| Lubrication | Oil Pump | Circulate oil | Pressure loss, seizure |
| Lubrication | Oil Filter | Remove contaminants | Clogging, bypass valve stuck |
| Cooling | Thermostat | Regulate coolant flow | Stuck open/closed |
| Cooling | Water Pump | Circulate coolant | Seal leak, impeller damage |
| Exhaust | Catalytic Converter | Treat emissions | Melting from misfires |
| Exhaust | O₂ Sensor | Air-fuel feedback | Slow response, contamination |
| EMS | ECU | Control actuators | Wiring faults, software error |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MPFI Engine System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Driver Input\nAccelerator Pedal] --> B[TPS Signal]
    B --> C[ECU Engine Control Unit]
    D[MAF Sensor\nMass Air Flow] --> C
    E[CKP and CMP Sensors\nCrank and Cam Position] --> C
    F[O2 Lambda Sensor\nExhaust Feedback] --> C
    G[CTS Coolant Temp\nSensor] --> C
    H[IAT Intake Air\nTemp Sensor] --> C
    C --> I[Injectors\nFuel Pulse Width Control]
    C --> J[Ignition Coils\nSpark Timing Control]
    C --> K[IACV Idle Air\nControl Valve]
    I --> L[Intake Port\nPer Cylinder]
    J --> M[Spark Plugs\nCombustion Initiation]
    L --> N[Engine Cylinders\n4 Stroke Otto Cycle]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Exhaust Manifold]
    O --> P[Catalytic Converter]
    P --> Q[Muffler and Tailpipe]
    Q --> F
    R[Fuel Tank] --> S[Electric Fuel Pump]
    S --> T[Fuel Filter]
    T --> U[Fuel Rail]
    U --> I
    U --> V[Pressure Regulator]
    V --> R
    N --> W[Oil Pump from Sump]
    W --> X[Oil Filter]
    X --> Y[Engine Bearings and Piston Walls]
    Y --> Z[Return to Sump]
    N --> AA[Water Jacket]
    AA --> AB[Thermostat]
    AB --> AC[Radiator]
    AC --> AD[Cooling Fan]
    AD --> AE[Expansion Tank]
    AE --> AA
```

### 4.2 MPFI Fuel System Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TANK[Fuel Tank] --> FP[Electric Fuel Pump]
    FP --> FF[Fuel Filter]
    FF --> FR[Fuel Rail]
    FR --> INJ1[Injector 1]
    FR --> INJ2[Injector 2]
    FR --> INJ3[Injector 3]
    FR --> INJ4[Injector 4]
    FR --> PR[Pressure Regulator]
    PR --> RET[Return Line]
    RET --> TANK
    INJ1 --> P1[Port Cyl 1]
    INJ2 --> P2[Port Cyl 2]
    INJ3 --> P3[Port Cyl 3]
    INJ4 --> P4[Port Cyl 4]
    ECU[ECU Pulse Control] -.-> INJ1
    ECU -.-> INJ2
    ECU -.-> INJ3
    ECU -.-> INJ4
```

### 4.3 Ignition System — Distributorless DIS with Coil-on-Plug

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BAT[12 V Battery] --> IGN[Ignition Switch]
    IGN --> ECU[ECU]
    CKP[Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> ECU
    CMP[Camshaft Position Sensor] --> ECU
    ECU --> CYL1[Coil Pack 1]
    ECU --> CYL2[Coil Pack 2]
    ECU --> CYL3[Coil Pack 3]
    ECU --> CYL4[Coil Pack 4]
    CYL1 --> SP1[Spark Plug 1]
    CYL2 --> SP2[Spark Plug 2]
    CYL3 --> SP3[Spark Plug 3]
    CYL4 --> SP4[Spark Plug 4]
    SP1 --> ENG[Engine Combustion Chamber]
    SP2 --> ENG
    SP3 --> ENG
    SP4 --> ENG
```

### 4.4 Lubrication System Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUMP[Oil Sump] --> OP[Oil Pump Driven by Crankshaft]
    OP --> RV[Relief Valve 4 bar]
    RV --> OF[Oil Filter Full Flow]
    OF --> OG[Oil Galleries in Block]
    OG --> MB[Main Bearings]
    OG --> CB[Connecting Rod Bearings]
    OG --> CS[Camshaft Bearings]
    OG --> VL[Valve Lifters]
    OG --> TC[Turbocharger if Equipped]
    MB --> SUMP
    CB --> SUMP
    CS --> SUMP
    VL --> SUMP
    TC --> SUMP
    PCV[PCV Ventilation] -.-> SUMP
```

### 4.5 Cooling System Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine Water Jacket] --> T[Thermostat 82 C]
    T --> RAD[Radiator]
    RAD --> FAN[Cooling Fan]
    FAN --> ET[Expansion Tank]
    ET --> WP[Water Pump]
    WP --> ENG
    T -. Cold Bypass .-> WP
    CHRG[Radiator Cap 1.1 bar] -.-> ET
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** These schematics are not physical drawings but **functional topology maps** showing how signals and fluids flow through the system. In a KTU lab viva, students are expected to trace a signal (e.g., accelerator pedal depression) from the input to the actuator (throttle opening) and explain the control loop.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCMEL609]**

**List any six major subsystems of an MPFI petrol engine and state the primary function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

An MPFI petrol engine comprises seven major subsystems:

| # | Subsystem | Primary Function |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Fuel Supply System** | Delivers atomized fuel at constant pressure to each intake port |
| 2 | **Air Intake System** | Filters, meters, and distributes fresh air to cylinders |
| 3 | **Ignition System** | Generates high-voltage spark at correct timing |
| 4 | **Lubrication System** | Reduces friction, cools, cleans moving parts |
| 5 | **Cooling System** | Maintains engine temperature at 85–95°C |
| 6 | **Exhaust System** | Expels burnt gases and reduces noise/pollution |
| 7 | **Engine Management System (ECU)** | Centralized control of all actuators based on sensor inputs |

**Marks Distribution (3 M):** Listing six subsystems: 2 marks + Function of one example subsystem: 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCMEL609]**

**Differentiate between a carburettor and a Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI) system. State two advantages of MPFI.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Carburettor | MPFI |
|-----------|-------------|------|
| **Injection Location** | Central (single venturi before manifold) | At each intake port, before intake valve |
| **Air-Fuel Mixing** | Uses Venturi suction | Direct injection atomization |
| **Distribution** | Unequal across cylinders (long runners cause variation) | Highly uniform |
| **Cold Start** | Choke mechanism required | ECU enriches pulse width automatically |
| **Altitude Compensation** | Manual jet change | Automatic via MAF sensor |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |

**Two Advantages of MPFI:**
1. **Improved fuel economy** (10–15% better) due to precise air-fuel control via closed-loop O₂ feedback.
2. **Lower emissions** (CO, HC, NOx reduced by ~30–40%) meeting BS-VI / Euro 6 norms.

**Marks Distribution (3 M):** Differentiation table: 2 marks + 2 advantages (½ M each): 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PCMEL609]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of a typical **fuel supply system** of a 4-cylinder MPFI petrol engine with a neat block diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a P-V and T-S diagram, derive an expression for the **air-standard Otto cycle efficiency** and compute it for $r_k = 9$ and $\gamma = 1.4$.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Construction and Working of MPFI Fuel Supply System [7 M]**

The MPFI fuel supply system has the following components:

**Construction:**
1. **Fuel Tank:** Located at vehicle rear, capacity ~35–55 L, with internal baffles and venting.
2. **In-Tank Electric Fuel Pump:** Submerged centrifugal pump, 12V DC, pressure output **2.5–3.5 bar**.
3. **Fuel Filter:** In-line, replaceable, 10 µm rating.
4. **Fuel Rail:** Hollow aluminum tube, common to all four injectors, maintains uniform pressure.
5. **Fuel Pressure Regulator:** Diaphragm-type, vacuum-referenced to intake manifold.
6. **Fuel Injectors:** Electromagnetic solenoid type, resistance **12–16 Ω** (high-impedance) or **2–5 Ω** (low-impedance with ballast).
7. **Fuel Return Line:** Bleeds excess fuel back to tank.

**Working Principle:**
- At ignition ON, ECU activates the fuel pump relay.
- Pump pressurizes fuel → Filter cleans → Rail distributes to all injectors.
- Pressure regulator maintains a constant **3 bar** differential (above manifold vacuum).
- ECU calculates **injection pulse width** based on: MAF reading, MAP, RPM, O₂ feedback, coolant temperature.
- Pulse width is typically **2–10 ms** at idle, increasing to **15–20 ms** at full load.
- When the solenoid is energized, the needle valve opens, fuel sprays through 8–12 orifices at **120° spray angle**, atomizing fuel into the intake port.
- Atomized fuel mixes with incoming air; the air-fuel mixture is drawn into the cylinder during the intake stroke.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.

**Marks Distribution (7 M):** Construction (list of components): 3 marks + Working with pulse width concept: 3 marks + Block diagram: 1 mark.

---

**Part (b) — Derivation of Otto Cycle Efficiency [7 M]**

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps:

1. Apply First Law to cycle: $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$
2. $Q_{in} = m c_v (T_3 - T_2)$ (constant volume)
3. $Q_{out} = m c_v (T_4 - T_1)$ (constant volume)
4. $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{(T_4 - T_1)}{(T_3 - T_2)}$
5. Apply isentropic relations: $\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{T_3}{T_4} = r_k^{\gamma-1}$
6. Substitute to obtain: $T_3 - T_2 = (T_4 - T_1) r_k^{\gamma-1}$
7. Final result: $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_k^{\gamma-1}}$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $r_k = 9$, $\gamma = 1.4$

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{9^{0.4}}
$$

Computing $9^{0.4}$:

$$
9^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \cdot \ln 9} = e^{0.4 \cdot 2.197} = e^{0.8789} = 2.408
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.408} = 1 - 0.4153 = 0.5847 \quad \text{or} \quad 58.47\%
$$

**Marks Distribution (7 M):** P-V and T-S diagram: 2 marks + Derivation: 3 marks + Numerical computation: 2 marks.

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – May 2023, PCMEL609]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **lubrication system** of a petrol engine with a neat flow diagram. Discuss **splash**, **pressure**, and **mist lubrication** methods.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-cylinder 4-stroke MPFI petrol engine has bore **80 mm**, stroke **90 mm**, compression ratio **9:1**, and runs at **3000 RPM**. The IMEP is **8 bar**. Calculate: (i) Indicated Power, (ii) Swept Volume per cylinder, (iii) Clearance Volume per cylinder.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Lubrication System [7 M]**

**Components:**
1. **Oil Sump (Pan):** Reservoir with capacity 3–5 L; baffles prevent oil surge during cornering.
2. **Oil Pump:** Gear-type (external/meshing gears) or rotor-type; flow rate ~30 L/min at 3000 RPM.
3. **Oil Filter:** Full-flow pleated paper element; bypass valve opens at 1.5 bar differential.
4. **Pressure Relief Valve:** Maintains system pressure at **3.5–4 bar**.
5. **Oil Galleries:** Drilled internal passages in block and head.
6. **Oil Cooler:** Heat exchanger (in some engines).
7. **Dipstick:** Oil level indicator.
8. **PCV System:** Routes blow-by back to intake.

**Working:** Oil sump → oil pump → filter → main gallery → main bearings → connecting rod bearings → camshaft bearings → valve train → returns to sump via gravity.

**Three Lubrication Methods:**

| Method | Description | Application |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Splash Lubrication** | Crank dips in oil and splashes it on cylinder walls and piston | Older small engines |
| **Pressure Lubrication** | Pump forces oil through drilled passages to critical bearings | Main/rod bearings |
| **Mist Lubrication (Splash + Pressure)** | Pressurized oil + splash from rod big-end creates oil mist | Modern engines — cylinder walls, pistons, small-end bearings |

**Marks Distribution (7 M):** Components list: 2 marks + Working + diagram: 2 marks + Three methods comparison: 3 marks.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 M]**

**Given Data:**
- $n_{cyl} = 4$ (4 cylinders)
- $D = 80$ mm = **0.08 m**
- $L = 90$ mm = **0.09 m**
- $r_k = 9$
- $N = 3000$ RPM
- $IMEP = 8$ bar = **800 kPa** = **$8 \times 10^5$ Pa**

**(i) Indicated Power Calculation:**

Swept volume per cylinder:

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^2 (0.09)
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \times 0.09 = 0.0004524 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_s = 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

Indicated power per cylinder (4-stroke, so divide by 2 for power strokes):

$$
IP_{cyl} = \frac{IMEP \cdot V_s \cdot N}{2 \times 60} = \frac{800 \times 10^3 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \times 3000}{120}
$$

$$
IP_{cyl} = \frac{800{,}000 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \times 3000}{120}
$$

Step-by-step:
- $800{,}000 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4} = 361.94$ W·min/cycle
- Multiply by $N/60$ = $3000/60 = 50$ cycles/s
- $IP_{cyl} = 361.94 \times 50 / 2 = 9048.5$ W = **9.05 kW**

Total IP (4 cylinders):

$$
IP_{total} = 4 \times 9.05 = 36.2 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Swept Volume per cylinder (already computed above):**

$$
V_s = 452.4 \text{ cm}^3 = 0.4524 \text{ litres}
$$

**(iii) Clearance Volume per cylinder:**

Total volume at BDC = $V_1 = V_s + V_c$
Compression ratio: $r_k = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = 9$

Solving for $V_c$:

$$
V_s + V_c = 9 V_c
$$

$$
V_s = 8 V_c
$$

$$
V_c = \frac{V_s}{8} = \frac{452.4}{8} = 56.55 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **IP_total = 36.2 kW**
- **V_s = 452.4 cm³ per cylinder**
- **V_c = 56.55 cm³ per cylinder**

**Marks Distribution (7 M):** Formulae: 2 marks + Substitutions: 2 marks + Step-by-step calculation (i): 1.5 marks + (ii) and (iii): 1.5 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing Marks in PCMEL609:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "2" in 4-stroke IP formula:** Many students write $IP = IMEP \cdot V_s \cdot N / 60$ (wrong). Correct: divide by $2 \times 60 = 120$ because firing occurs once every 2 revolutions.
> 2. **Confusing swept volume and clearance volume:** $V_s$ is the *displaced* volume; $V_c$ is the *residual* volume at TDC. Total $V_1 = V_s + V_c$.
> 3. **Ignoring unit conversion in BSFC:** BSFC must be in kg/kWh. Mixing kg/s and W gives wrong order of magnitude.
> 4. **Not mentioning ECU feedback in MPFI answers:** Examiners expect the closed-loop O₂ sensor control concept in any MPFI explanation.
> 5. **Wrong Otto cycle formula:** $\eta = 1 - \frac{1}{r_k^{\gamma-1}}$, **not** $1 - \frac{1}{r_k^\gamma}$. The exponent is $\gamma - 1$, not $\gamma$.
> 6. **Lab viva: "What is the function of a fuel pressure regulator?"** Many students say "to increase fuel pressure." Correct: "to maintain a *constant pressure differential* between the fuel rail and the intake manifold."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Familiarisation of Petrol / MPFI Engine**

- [ ] A petrol engine follows the **Otto Cycle** (4 reversible processes: 2 isentropic, 2 isochoric).
- [ ] **MPFI** = Multi-Point Fuel Injection; one injector per cylinder, mounted in the **intake port**.
- [ ] **7 Major Subsystems:** Fuel, Air Intake, Ignition, Lubrication, Cooling, Exhaust, EMS (ECU).
- [ ] **Compression ratio** $r_k$ for petrol: typically **9:1 to 12:1**; higher values risk **knocking**.
- [ ] **Stoichiometric AFR for petrol = 14.7:1** (mass basis).
- [ ] **Otto efficiency formula:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_k^{\gamma-1}}$; with $\gamma = 1.4$.
- [ ] **4-stroke IP formula:** $IP = \frac{IMEP \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n_{cyl}}{2 \times 60 \times 1000}$ (kW).
- [ ] **Fuel rail pressure:** 2.5–3.5 bar; **injection pulse width:** 2–20 ms.
- [ ] **Ignition voltage:** 20,000–40,000 V across spark plug gap of 0.7–1.1 mm.
- [ ] **BSFC for MPFI engines:** typically 0.25–0.30 kg/kWh.
- [ ] **Calorific value of petrol:** 44 MJ/kg.
- [ ] **Cooling system operating range:** 85–95°C; **thermostat opens:** 82±2°C.
- [ ] **Oil pressure:** 3.5–4 bar at operating temperature; **oil capacity:** 3–5 L.
- [ ] **Catalytic converter type:** Three-Way Catalyst (TWC); **lambda (λ) target:** 1.00 ± 0.01.
- [ ] **Modern ignition:** Distributorless Ignition System (DIS) with **coil-on-plug (COP)** arrangement.
- [ ] **ECU controls:** Injection timing, ignition timing, idle speed, EGR, fuel pump relay, cooling fan.
- [ ] **Sensors feeding ECU:** MAF, MAP, TPS, CKP, CMP, O₂, CTS, IAT — know the abbreviation and function of each.
- [ ] **Distinguish for viva:** 4-stroke vs 2-stroke (firing frequency), MPFI vs SPFI, distributor vs DIS, air-cooling vs liquid-cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Lab Exam:** Always trace a real-world signal (e.g., pressing the accelerator) through the entire system: **Pedal → TPS → ECU → Injector + Throttle → Engine → CKP feedback → ECU adjustment**. This systems-thinking approach scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarisation of various systems and subsystems of Diesel engine / Turbocharged engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL609 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-2
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation of various systems and subsystems of Diesel engine / Turbocharged engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Diesel Engine** is an internal combustion engine (ICE) that operates on the **diesel cycle** (a modified version of the **dual cycle** in modern high-speed applications), in which the ignition of fuel is achieved by the high temperature of the compressed air charge, without the need of a spark plug. The engine is also commonly referred to as a **Compression Ignition (CI) engine**.

A **Turbocharged Engine** is a variant of the internal combustion engine equipped with a **turbocharger** — a forced induction device that uses the engine's own exhaust gases to drive a turbine, which in turn drives a centrifugal compressor. This compressor forces more air (and hence more fuel) into the combustion chamber, thereby significantly increasing the **specific power output** and **brake thermal efficiency** of the base engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** Familiarisation experiments in Thermal Engineering Lab-2 expect the student to visually identify, functionally describe, and operationally explain each subsystem of a multi-cylinder diesel engine and its turbocharging unit, including all auxiliary accessories.

## 1.2 The Major Engine Systems — Overview

A modern multi-cylinder diesel engine is not a single monolithic machine; it is an **integrated assembly of seven interdependent sub-systems**:

| Sl. No. | System | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fuel Injection System** | Meter, pressurise, time, and atomise fuel into combustion chamber |
| 2 | **Air Intake & Boost System** | Supply clean, dense, metered air to cylinders |
| 3 | **Exhaust Gas System** | Expel burnt gases, drive turbocharger, reduce noise/emissions |
| 4 | **Cooling System** | Maintain optimum cylinder/head temperature |
| 5 | **Lubricating System** | Reduce friction, cool bearings, clean internal parts |
| 6 | **Starting & Electrical System** | Crank the engine, supply ignition-free start, monitor sensors |
| 7 | **Governing & Control System** | Regulate speed/load, protect from over-speed |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Hardworking Labourer + Oxygen Mask" Analogy**
> Imagine a labourer (the diesel engine) who is digging a ditch (producing work/horsepower). The more oxygen he breathes, the harder and longer he can work. A **naturally aspirated** diesel is like this labourer breathing normal air. A **turbocharged** diesel is the same labourer fitted with an **oxygen mask** (the turbocharger) that pumps in extra fresh air using the energy of the breath he just exhaled (the exhaust gases). The "oxygen mask" doesn't produce any energy of its own — it just *redirects wasted energy* (exhaust) to do *useful work* (force in more air). This is exactly how a turbocharger operates, recapturing **30–40%** of the exhaust energy that would otherwise be lost as heat and pressure.

**Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Bolded):**

- **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC):** **200–250 g/kWh** (typical for naturally aspirated diesel)
- **Compression Ratio (CR):** **14:1 to 22:1** (diesel)
- **Air-Fuel Ratio (λ):** **18:1 to 70:1** (much leaner than petrol's ~14.7:1)
- **Turbocharger Boost Pressure:** **1.2 bar to 3.5 bar** (absolute manifold pressure)
- **Diesel Cetane Number:** **45–55** (measure of ignition quality)
- **Engine Speed Range:** **800 rpm (idle) to 2500–3000 rpm (rated)** for medium-duty diesels

## 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters in the KTU Lab

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Objective:** Before performing any performance test (load test, Morse test, retardation test, heat balance), the student **must** be able to point to each component on the actual engine, name it, state its function, and explain its role in the overall working. This familiarisation forms the **preamble** of all subsequent experiments in PCMEL609.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System 1 — Fuel Injection System (FIS)

### 2.1.1 Components & Working

The fuel injection system in a modern diesel engine typically uses a **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)** or a **jerk pump (inline) system**. The major sub-components are:

- **Fuel Tank:** Stores diesel (volume typically 40–400 litres depending on application).
- **Fuel Lift Pump:** A diaphragm or plunger pump that transfers fuel from tank to the main injection pump.
- **Fuel Filter (Primary + Secondary):** Removes water and particulates (filtration rating **5–10 µm**).
- **Injection Pump (Inline / Distributor / Common Rail):** Pressurises fuel to injection pressures.
  - Inline pump: **400–600 bar**
  - Common Rail: **1500–2200 bar** (modern BS-VI engines)
- **High-Pressure Pipes:** Carry pressurised fuel to injectors.
- **Injector (Nozzle Holder Assembly / NHA):** Atomises fuel into fine spray pattern; in CRDi it is solenoid or piezo-electrically actuated.
- **Governor:** Mechanical / hydraulic / electronic — modulates fuel quantity as a function of load & speed.

### 2.1.2 Theory — Why High Pressure?

The diesel engine **relies on atomised fuel** mixing with high-temperature compressed air. The atomisation quality is governed by the **Weber Number (We)** and **Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD)** of the droplets:

$$SMD = 0.585 \cdot \frac{\left(\sigma \cdot \rho_{air}\right)^{0.5} \cdot d_{orifice}^{1.5}}{\Delta P^{0.5} \cdot \mu_{air}^{0.5}}$$

where higher $\Delta P$ (injection pressure differential) gives smaller droplet size, faster evaporation, and complete combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering relevance:** Failure of any single component in the FIS — clogged filter, worn plunger, leaky injector — can cause power loss (typically 5–20%), smoke, and rough running. This is why KTU lab exams often ask: *"What will happen if the injector is stuck open?"* (Answer: cylinder will hydraulically lock → engine damage.)

## 2.2 System 2 — Air Intake & Boost System

### 2.2.1 Components

- **Air Cleaner (Dry/Wet type):** Filters atmospheric air; dry-type uses paper element rated to **>99% efficiency at 5 µm**.
- **Intake Manifold:** Distributes air to individual cylinders; in multi-cylinder engines, the manifold is designed for **equal air distribution**.
- **Turbocharger Compressor Housing:** Centrifugal compressor that pressurises intake air.
- **Intercooler (Charge Air Cooler / CAC):** Cools the compressed air to increase density.
- **Throttle Valve (in modern electronic diesels):** Controls boost pressure via Variable Geometry Turbocharger (VGT) vanes.

### 2.2.2 Function

$$ \rho_{boosted} = \frac{P_{boost} \cdot M_{air}}{R \cdot T_{intake}} $$

Higher intake density $\rho_{boosted}$ means more oxygen per cycle, more fuel can be burned, and thus more power is produced from the same engine displacement.

## 2.3 System 3 — Exhaust Gas System

### 2.3.1 Components

- **Exhaust Manifold:** Collects exhaust gases from all cylinders.
- **Turbocharger Turbine Housing:** Converts exhaust gas enthalpy into rotational work.
- **Exhaust Pipe / Silencer (Muffler):** Reduces noise via **Helmholtz resonator** and **absorptive baffles**.
- **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC):** Oxidises CO and unburnt hydrocarbons.
- **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF):** Captures soot particles.
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) Valve:** Recirculates a small portion (~5–15%) of exhaust back to intake to reduce $NO_x$.

### 2.3.2 Theoretical Principle — Turbocharger Energy Recovery

The exhaust gas possesses significant **enthalpy** ($h = C_p T$) and **kinetic energy**. The turbocharger turbine extracts a portion of this energy:

$$ W_{turbine} = \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot C_{pg} \cdot (T_1 - T_2) \cdot \eta_{turbine} $$

This work drives the compressor:

$$ W_{compressor} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot C_{pa} \cdot (T_3 - T_2) / \eta_{compressor} $$

Equating both: $W_{turbine} = W_{compressor}$ (shaft is common).

## 2.4 System 4 — Cooling System

### 2.4.1 Two Types

- **Air-Cooled (rare in modern diesels):** Fins on cylinder head and block.
- **Water-Cooled (Liquid Cooling):** Most common in lab engines.

### 2.4.2 Components (Water-Cooled)

- **Water Jacket:** Cavities cast into the cylinder block and head.
- **Water Pump (Centrifugal):** Circulates coolant at typical flow of **40–80 L/min**.
- **Thermostat:** Wax-pellet type; opens at **82–95°C** to regulate flow to radiator.
- **Radiator:** Finned heat exchanger; rejects heat to atmosphere.
- **Coolant Reservoir / Expansion Tank:** Allows for thermal expansion.
- **Fan (Mechanical / Electric / Viscous):** Draws air through the radiator.

### 2.4.3 Heat Rejection

$$ Q_{coolant} = \dot{m}_{coolant} \cdot C_{p,coolant} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) $$

Typical heat distribution in a diesel engine:

| Heat Channel | % of Fuel Energy |
|---|---|
| Useful Work (Brake Power) | **30–40%** |
| Cooling Water | **25–30%** |
| Exhaust Gases | **30–35%** |
| Lubricating Oil | **2–5%** |
| Radiation & Misc. | **1–3%** |

## 2.5 System 5 — Lubricating System

### 2.5.1 Components

- **Sump (Oil Pan):** Reservoir; holds 4–12 litres of oil.
- **Oil Pump (Gear / Rotor / Vane type):** Pressurises oil to **2–5 bar** for the main gallery.
- **Oil Filter (Full-Flow / By-Pass):** Removes metallic particles and combustion byproducts.
- **Oil Cooler (Air-cooled or Water-cooled):** Maintains oil at **80–110°C**.
- **Pressure Relief Valve:** Opens at ~4 bar to protect gallery from over-pressure.
- **Oil Galleries and Passages:** Distribute oil to crankshaft, camshaft, valve train, turbocharger bearings.

### 2.5.2 Lubrication Methods

- **Splash Lubrication:** Connecting rod dips into oil (used in small 2-stroke engines).
- **Pressure Lubrication:** Used in all multi-cylinder diesels; oil is forced through drilled passages.
- **Mist / Fog Lubrication:** Used in 2-stroke large marine engines.

## 2.6 System 6 — Starting & Electrical System

- **Starter Motor:** DC motor rated **12V / 24V, 2–4 kW**.
- **Battery:** Lead-acid, capacity **60–200 Ah**.
- **Alternator (AC Generator):** Charges battery, supplies electrical accessories; output **14V / 28V**.
- **Glow Plugs:** In cold-start, heat the combustion chamber air to aid ignition (only for IDI diesels).
- **Sensors:** Oil pressure, coolant temperature, RPM, boost pressure, lambda sensor.
- **ECU (Engine Control Unit):** Brain of the engine; in CRDi systems it controls injection timing, duration, and EGR.

## 2.7 System 7 — Turbocharger Subsystem (Detailed)

The turbocharger is itself a **complete sub-system** of the engine, comprising:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Turbine Wheel** | Extracts energy from exhaust (typical inlet temp 600–900°C) |
| **Compressor Wheel** | Pressurises intake air (typical outlet temp 150–200°C) |
| **Bearing Housing (CHRA)** | Contains journal bearings; oil-cooled |
| **Wastegate** | Bypasses excess exhaust to limit boost pressure |
| **Variable Geometry Vanes (VNT/VGT)** | Adjusts turbine flow area for optimum boost across RPM range |
| **Boost Control Solenoid** | Electronic actuator for VGT / wastegate |

### 2.7.1 Turbocharger Performance Map

The performance is characterised by **compressor pressure ratio** $\Pi_c$ and **mass flow rate** $\dot{m}$:

$$ \Pi_c = \frac{P_{boost}}{P_{atm}} $$

$$ \eta_{adiabatic,compressor} = \frac{T_{boost,ideal} - T_{intake}}{T_{boost,actual} - T_{intake}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Turbocharger "surge" is a critical operating issue — it occurs when compressor operates to the left of its surge line (low flow, high pressure ratio). This causes violent airflow reversal and a characteristic "fluttering" sound. The wastegate prevents surge by bleeding off excess exhaust energy.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CR = \frac{V_1}{V_2}$ | Compression Ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Otto cycle (petrol) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (r_c - 1)}$ | Diesel cycle efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \left[ \frac{r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1)} \right]$ | Dual (Limited-pressure) cycle | dimensionless |
| 5 | $BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \cdot 10^6$ | Specific fuel consumption | g/kWh |
| 6 | $\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP}$ | Mechanical efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Pi_c = \frac{P_2}{P_1}$ | Compressor pressure ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_2 = T_1 \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | Isentropic compression temperature | K |
| 10 | $W_c = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | Compressor work per kg air | J/kg |
| 11 | $\rho = \frac{PM}{RT}$ | Ideal gas density (boost) | kg/m³ |
| 12 | $P_{mean,eff} = \frac{BP \cdot n_c \cdot 60}{L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot k}$ | Brake mean effective pressure | bar |

## 2.9 Real-world Engineering Utility

- **Heavy-duty diesel engines (trucks, locomotives, gensets):** Almost universally turbocharged.
- **Marine propulsion:** Large 2-stroke diesels often use **turbo-compounding** to recover additional exhaust energy via a power turbine.
- **Automotive:** Modern BS-VI/Euro-VI diesels use **VGT + EGR + DPF + DOC** combined.
- **Aircraft APUs (Auxiliary Power Units):** Small gas turbines are essentially turbochargers running standalone.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Air-Standard Diesel Cycle Efficiency

The Diesel cycle is the idealised air-standard cycle for a CI engine. It consists of:
- **Process 1-2:** Isentropic (adiabatic reversible) compression
- **Process 2-3:** Constant-pressure heat addition
- **Process 3-4:** Isentropic expansion
- **Process 4-1:** Constant-volume heat rejection

### 3.1.1 Statement of Efficiency

$$ \eta_{th,diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma \cdot r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r_c - 1} $$

where:
- $r = V_1 / V_2$ = Compression Ratio (CR)
- $r_c = V_3 / V_2$ = Cut-off Ratio
- $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ = Ratio of specific heats ≈ **1.4** for air

### 3.1.2 Full Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Heat Supplied (Q_s):** Occurs at constant pressure (process 2-3):

$$ Q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2) $$

**Step 2 — Heat Rejected (Q_r):** Occurs at constant volume (process 4-1):

$$ Q_r = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_4 - T_1) $$

**Step 3 — Definition of Efficiency:**

$$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_r}{Q_s} $$

**Step 4 — Express Temperatures in terms of Volumes using isentropic relations.**

For isentropic process 1-2:
$$ T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} $$

For constant-pressure process 2-3:
$$ \frac{T_3}{T_2} = \frac{V_3}{V_2} = r_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_3 = T_2 \cdot r_c = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \cdot r_c $$

For isentropic process 3-4:
$$ T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma-1} = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1} = T_1 \cdot r_c^{\gamma} $$

(Because $V_4 = V_1$, so $V_3 / V_4 = r_c / r$, and raising to $\gamma-1$ and multiplying by $T_3$ yields $T_1 \cdot r_c^{\gamma}$.)

**Step 5 — Substitute into the efficiency equation:**

$$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_1 (r_c^{\gamma} - 1)}{T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} (r_c - 1)} $$

**Step 6 — Simplify:**

$$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma \cdot r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r_c - 1} $$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th,diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma \cdot r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r_c - 1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The diesel cycle efficiency **decreases** as cut-off ratio $r_c$ increases. This is because longer fuel injection (higher $r_c$) means more heat is added at a lower average temperature, reducing Carnot-like efficiency. This is a foundational reason why **direct injection, short-cutoff** modern diesels outperform older indirect-injection designs.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Brake Thermal Efficiency of a Turbocharged Engine

### 3.2.1 Setup

The brake thermal efficiency is defined as the ratio of useful brake power output to the chemical energy input from fuel.

$$ \eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} $$

where:
- $BP$ = Brake Power (W or kW)
- $\dot{m}_f$ = Mass flow rate of fuel (kg/s or kg/h)
- $CV$ = Calorific Value of diesel ≈ **43,000 kJ/kg**

### 3.2.2 Re-arrangement to BSFC

The Brake Specific Fuel Consumption is the reciprocal, expressed in g/kWh:

$$ BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \cdot 10^6 \quad \text{(g/kWh)} $$

Thus:

$$ \eta_{bth} = \frac{3600 \cdot 10^3}{BSFC \cdot CV} $$

**Numerical Example:** A 4-cylinder turbocharged diesel engine develops 50 kW brake power at 2500 rpm and consumes 12 kg of fuel per hour. Calculate $\eta_{bth}$ and $BSFC$.

Given: $BP = 50$ kW, $\dot{m}_f = 12$ kg/h, $CV = 43{,}000$ kJ/kg

**Step 1 — Convert fuel flow to kg/s:**

$$ \dot{m}_f = \frac{12}{3600} = 3.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} $$

**Step 2 — Compute heat input rate:**

$$ \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV = 3.333 \times 10^{-3} \times 43{,}000 = 143.33 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 3 — Compute brake thermal efficiency:**

$$ \eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{50}{143.33} = 0.3488 = 34.88\% $$

**Step 4 — Compute BSFC:**

$$ BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \cdot 10^6 = \frac{12 \text{ kg/h}}{50 \text{ kW}} \cdot 10^3 = 240 \text{ g/kWh} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A $BSFC$ of **240 g/kWh** is excellent for a turbocharged diesel — indicating good fuel economy. A naturally aspirated diesel typically shows 250–280 g/kWh. The turbocharger improves $\eta_{bth}$ by allowing the engine to operate at higher loads (closer to its optimum efficiency point) for the same power output.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Turbocharger Compressor Work and Pressure Ratio

### 3.3.1 Statement

The compressor work (per kg of air) in an adiabatic (isentropic for ideal) compression process is:

$$ w_c = C_{pa} (T_2 - T_1) $$

For isentropic compression, the outlet temperature is:

$$ T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$

### 3.3.2 Derivation of Isentropic Relation

Starting from the isentropic relation for an ideal gas:

$$ \frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$

**Derivation using PV = mRT and PV^γ = constant:**

From $P V^{\gamma} = \text{const}$:

$$ P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma} $$

Using $V = mRT/P$:

$$ P_1 \left( \frac{mRT_1}{P_1} \right)^{\gamma} = P_2 \left( \frac{mRT_2}{P_2} \right)^{\gamma} $$

$$ P_1^{1-\gamma} \cdot T_1^{\gamma} = P_2^{1-\gamma} \cdot T_2^{\gamma} $$

Rearranging:

$$ \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$

$$\boxed{ T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} }$$

### 3.3.3 Work Calculation

The minimum (isentropic) work per kg of air:

$$ w_{c,s} = C_p (T_{2s} - T_1) = C_p T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] $$

The actual work, accounting for isentropic efficiency $\eta_c$:

$$ w_{c,actual} = \frac{w_{c,s}}{\eta_c} = \frac{C_p T_1}{\eta_c} \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] $$

### 3.3.4 Numerical Example

**Problem:** A turbocharger compressor takes in air at $T_1 = 35°C = 308$ K and $P_1 = 1.013$ bar. It delivers air at $P_2 = 2.5$ bar. Assuming isentropic compression with $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_p = 1005$ J/kg·K, find the isentropic outlet temperature and the work input per kg of air.

**Step 1 — Pressure ratio:**

$$ \Pi_c = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{2.5}{1.013} = 2.468 $$

**Step 2 — Isentropic outlet temperature:**

$$ T_{2s} = 308 \cdot (2.468)^{0.2857} $$

$$ (2.468)^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln(2.468)} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 0.9037} = e^{0.2583} = 1.2948 $$

$$ T_{2s} = 308 \times 1.2948 = 398.8 \text{ K} \quad (\approx 125.8°C) $$

**Step 3 — Isentropic work per kg:**

$$ w_{c,s} = 1005 \times (398.8 - 308) = 1005 \times 90.8 = 91{,}254 \text{ J/kg} \approx 91.25 \text{ kJ/kg} $$

**Step 4 — Actual work (assuming $\eta_c = 0.75$):**

$$ w_{c,actual} = \frac{91.25}{0.75} = 121.67 \text{ kJ/kg} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 91.25 kJ/kg of energy is **free** — it comes from the exhaust gases that would otherwise be wasted. This is why turbocharging is considered "free power" in thermodynamic terms, although mechanically it adds complexity and back-pressure to the engine.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Subsystem Failure Modes

| Subsystem | Component Failure | Observable Symptom | KTU Lab Test Affected |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel Injection | Injector leak | Smoke, power loss | BSFC test, smoke measurement |
| Turbocharger | Bearing wear | Whine, oil leak, low boost | Performance curve deviation |
| Cooling | Thermostat stuck | Overheating | Heat balance test |
| Lubrication | Oil pump failure | Low pressure warning | Morse test (friction HP changes) |
| Air Intake | Clogged filter | Black smoke, derating | Volumetric efficiency drops |
| Exhaust | DPF clogged | Excessive back-pressure | Heat balance, exhaust temp rises |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for Lab Viva:**
> - The **compressor-turbine** connection is **mechanical**, not flow-based (dotted line shows energy transfer).
> - The **wastegate** is a pressure-limiting device — it opens to bypass exhaust when boost exceeds the target (typically 1.5–2.5 bar absolute).
> - The **intercooler** is mandatory: compression raises the air temperature (we calculated ~126°C above); cooling it back to ~50°C dramatically increases the density and hence the mass of air trapped per cycle.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"List the major systems and subsystems of a multi-cylinder turbocharged diesel engine."**

**Model Answer:**

A modern multi-cylinder turbocharged diesel engine comprises the following systems:

1. **Fuel Injection System** — fuel tank, lift pump, filters, injection pump, high-pressure pipes, injectors, governor.
2. **Air Intake & Boost System** — air cleaner, turbocharger compressor, intercooler (CAC), intake manifold.
3. **Exhaust System** — exhaust manifold, turbocharger turbine, silencer, DPF/DOC.
4. **Cooling System** — water jacket, pump, thermostat, radiator, fan.
5. **Lubricating System** — sump, oil pump, filter, galleries, cooler, pressure relief valve.
6. **Starting & Electrical System** — battery, starter motor, alternator, glow plugs (if IDI), sensors, ECU.
7. **Turbocharger Subsystem** — turbine, compressor, wastegate, VGT, CHRA bearings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget the **governing system** and the **governor** as part of the fuel system, or omit the **intercooler**. Both are **favourite** viva questions — losing 1 mark each if missed.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**"What is the function of a turbocharger in a diesel engine? Why is an intercooler used after the turbocharger?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Function of turbocharger:** A turbocharger is a **forced-induction device** that uses energy from the exhaust gases to drive a turbine, which in turn drives a centrifugal compressor. The compressor forces additional air into the combustion chamber, increasing the **volumetric efficiency** and **specific power output** of the engine without increasing its displacement.

**Function of intercooler:** During compression in the turbocharger, the intake air temperature rises significantly (e.g., from 35°C to 125°C). This reduces the air density. The intercooler (Charge Air Cooler, CAC) **cools the compressed air**, thereby **increasing its density**. Higher density means more oxygen mass per cycle, enabling more fuel to be burned and more power to be produced. The intercooler also reduces the risk of engine knock and thermal stress on components.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**"With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the air intake and exhaust system of a turbocharged diesel engine. Also derive the expression for the isentropic work of compression in the turbocharger compressor."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Block Diagram and Explanation

**Model Solution:**

A turbocharged diesel engine's intake and exhaust systems work as an integrated energy-recovery loop:

**Intake Side:**
1. **Atmospheric air** is drawn through the **air cleaner** (dry paper element, filtration efficiency >99% for particles >5 µm).
2. Clean air enters the **compressor housing** of the turbocharger.
3. The centrifugal compressor wheel, driven by the common shaft with the turbine, compresses the air to a **boost pressure** typically 1.2 to 2.5 bar (gauge).
4. The hot, compressed air then passes through the **intercooler (CAC)**, where it is cooled by ambient air to approximately 50–80°C, increasing its density.
5. Cooled, dense air enters the **intake manifold**, which distributes it equally to each cylinder via individual runners.
6. The **throttle valve** (in modern electronically controlled engines) regulates the air mass flow rate.

**Exhaust Side:**
1. After combustion, hot exhaust gases at **600–900°C** are expelled into the **exhaust manifold**.
2. The high-energy exhaust gases enter the **turbine housing** of the turbocharger, where they expand across the turbine blades, doing work.
3. After the turbine, exhaust gases pass through the **diesel oxidation catalyst (DOC)** and **diesel particulate filter (DPF)** for emissions control.
4. Finally, gases exit via the **silencer/muffler** to the atmosphere.

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above):**
Air → Cleaner → Compressor → Intercooler → Manifold → Cylinder → Exhaust Manifold → Turbine → DPF/DOC → Silencer → Atmosphere

The mechanical link between turbine and compressor (via the common shaft) transfers the recovered exhaust energy to compress intake air.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Derivation of Isentropic Compressor Work

**Model Solution:**

Consider 1 kg of air as the working fluid entering the compressor at state 1 ($P_1$, $T_1$) and leaving at state 2 ($P_2$, $T_2$).

**Step 1 — Apply steady-flow energy equation** (neglecting KE and PE changes):

$$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 = C_p (T_2 - T_1) $$

**Step 2 — For an isentropic (reversible adiabatic) process:**

$$ \frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$

**Step 3 — Solving for $T_{2s}$:**

$$ T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the work equation:**

$$ w_{c,s} = C_p \left[ T_1 \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - T_1 \right] $$

**Step 5 — Factor out $T_1$:**

$$\boxed{w_{c,s} = C_p T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]}$$

This is the minimum (isentropic) work required per kg of air to compress it from $P_1$ to $P_2$. The actual work is obtained by dividing by the isentropic efficiency of the compressor.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**"Explain the fuel injection system and the lubricating system of a multi-cylinder diesel engine. Discuss the consequences of failure in any two critical components."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Fuel Injection System

**Model Solution:**

The fuel injection system in a modern diesel engine (typically CRDi) consists of:

1. **Fuel Tank** — stores diesel fuel (filtration begins here).
2. **Fuel Lift Pump** — transfers fuel from tank to the high-pressure pump.
3. **Fuel Filters (Primary + Secondary)** — remove water and particulates; the secondary filter has a finer rating (typically 5 µm).
4. **High-Pressure Pump (Common Rail Pump)** — pressurises fuel to **1500–2200 bar** in CRDi systems. The pump is driven by the camshaft.
5. **Common Rail (Accumulator)** — stores pressurised fuel and maintains constant rail pressure.
6. **High-Pressure Pipes** — carry fuel from the rail to each injector.
7. **Electronic Injectors (Solenoid / Piezo)** — atomise fuel into the combustion chamber at the precise moment and duration commanded by the ECU.
8. **ECU (Engine Control Unit)** — receives signals from sensors (RPM, load, temperature) and computes injection timing, duration, and pressure.
9. **Governor** — modulates fuel quantity to maintain constant speed under varying load.

**Working Principle:** The ECU determines the start of injection (SOI) and duration based on engine operating conditions. The injector is energised, the needle lifts, and fuel is sprayed at high pressure through multiple fine orifices (typically 5–9 holes of ~150 µm diameter), producing an atomised spray that mixes with the compressed hot air in the cylinder. Combustion initiates via auto-ignition due to the high temperature of the compressed air.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Lubricating System & Component Failure Consequences

**Model Solution:**

**Lubricating System Components:**
1. **Sump (Oil Pan)** — reservoir holding 4–12 litres of lubricating oil (typically SAE 15W-40).
2. **Oil Pump** — gear/rotor type; pressurises oil to **2–5 bar**.
3. **Oil Filter (Full-Flow)** — removes metallic debris and combustion byproducts.
4. **Oil Galleries** — drilled passages that distribute oil to main bearings, camshaft bearings, and turbocharger bearings.
5. **Oil Cooler** — maintains oil at **80–110°C** for optimum viscosity.
6. **Pressure Relief Valve** — opens at ~4 bar to prevent over-pressurisation.

**Consequences of Failure — Two Critical Components:**

**(i) Failure of Oil Pump:**
- Oil pressure drops below safe threshold.
- Crankshaft and camshaft bearings receive insufficient lubrication.
- Frictional heating rises, leading to **bearing seizure** (white-metal lining melts).
- Turbocharger bearings fail due to oil starvation — turbine/compressor shaft seizure, often preceded by a high-pitched whine.
- Engine seizure may occur within minutes, requiring complete engine overhaul.

**(ii) Failure of Oil Filter (clogged):**
- If the filter is clogged and the by-pass valve fails to open, oil flow to galleries is restricted.
- Oil pressure warning lamp illuminates.
- Insufficient lubrication of critical components.
- Metallic particles continue to circulate, accelerating wear.
- In severe cases, the filter element may **collapse**, releasing accumulated debris into the oil galleries and causing catastrophic abrasive wear.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Part B - 7 mark sub-parts):** A common mistake is to write the answer in **paragraph form without any headings or numbered list**. KTU examiners reward **structured presentation** — use bullet points, sub-headings, and a labelled diagram. Missing the diagram costs up to 2 marks. Also, students often forget to mention the **ECU** in the fuel system explanation, which alone can cost 1 mark.
> 
> **Pitfall 2 — Common Derivation Error:** In the compressor work derivation, students frequently forget to **state the assumption of KE and PE being negligible**. This is a 0.5 mark deduction. Also, using $\gamma = 1.4$ without stating it (or using $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K) loses another 0.5 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- [x] A **diesel engine** is a **compression ignition (CI) engine** with CR = **14:1 to 22:1**.
- [x] A **turbocharger** uses **exhaust gas energy** to drive a compressor — it does not consume engine work.
- [x] The **diesel cycle efficiency** is $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\gamma r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r_c - 1}$.
- [x] Higher **cut-off ratio $r_c$** → **lower** cycle efficiency.
- [x] The **isentropic compressor work** per kg of air is $w_{c,s} = C_p T_1 \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$.
- [x] **BSFC** for turbocharged diesel: typically **200–250 g/kWh**.
- [x] **Intercooler purpose:** cool the boost air to **increase density** (more oxygen per cycle).
- [x] **Wastegate function:** limit maximum boost pressure to prevent over-boost and surge.
- [x] **Fuel injection pressure** in CRDi: **1500–2200 bar** (much higher than older inline pump systems of 400–600 bar).
- [x] **Cooling system components:** water jacket → water pump → thermostat → radiator → fan. Thermostat opens at **82–95°C**.
- [x] **Lubricating oil pressure** in main gallery: **2–5 bar**.
- [x] **Turbocharger bearing lubrication** is **engine-pressure-fed** (failure of oil supply → turbo failure).
- [x] The **exhaust system** in modern engines includes **DOC + DPF** for emission control.
- [x] **VGT (Variable Geometry Turbocharger)** adjusts turbine flow area via moving vanes for optimum boost across the RPM range.
- [x] **Surge line** on the compressor map: operating left of it causes **compressor surge** (flow reversal).
- [x] **Brake thermal efficiency** of turbocharged diesel: typically **35–42%** (vs 30–35% for NA diesel).
- [x] **Air-fuel ratio** of diesel: **18:1 to 70:1** (very lean compared to petrol's 14.7:1).
- [x] Always remember to **state units** in all numerical problems; KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Viva Tip:** If asked *"Why is the diesel engine more efficient than the petrol engine?"*, the answer combines three reasons: **(1)** higher compression ratio (no knock limit), **(2)** lean burn operation, and **(3)** no throttling loss (governed by fuel quantity, not air). The turbocharger further enhances this by allowing higher specific power output without the penalty of increasing displacement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
