topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL609 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-2 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Performance test on Diesel engines / Turbocharged engine # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Performance Test on a Diesel Engine** is an experimental procedure conducted on a compression-ignition (CI) engine to evaluate its operational characteristics under varying load conditions. The test quantifies parameters such as **brake power (BP)**, **indicated power (IP)**, **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)**, **brake thermal efficiency (η_bth)**, **mechanical efficiency (η_mech)**, **volumetric efficiency (η_vol)**, **air-fuel ratio (AFR)**, **heat balance**, and **specific energy consumption**. For a **turbocharged engine**, the test additionally captures the effect of boost pressure on power output, fuel economy, and the tendency toward **smoke/soot emissions** and **knock**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this experiment maps directly to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Apply the principles of thermodynamics and fluid mechanics to conduct performance tests on IC engines and interpret the results."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are training an athlete (the engine) for a marathon. To know how fit he is, you don't just time him once — you measure his **speed, oxygen consumption, sweat rate, and heart rate** at *different running intensities* (loads). The **performance test on a diesel engine** is exactly that: the engine is "run" through a sequence of loads (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% of full load), and at each load we measure how much **fuel it drinks (fuel consumption)**, how much **air it breathes (air consumption)**, how much **heat it wastes**, and how much **useful work (brake power)** it delivers. A **turbocharger** is like a **supercharged lung** for the engine — it uses the exhaust gas energy to force more air into the cylinder, allowing more fuel to be burned and therefore generating more power *from the same engine displacement*. Performance testing quantifies exactly *how much* extra power this "lung" gives us, and at what cost in fuel economy. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Calorific Value of Diesel (CV)** ≈ **42,000 kJ/kg** (standard KTU assumed value) > - **Density of Diesel (ρ_f)** ≈ **0.87 g/cc = 870 kg/m³** at 15 °C > - **Density of Air (ρ_a)** at NTP = **1.29 kg/m³** > - **Specific heat of water** = **4.186 kJ/kg·K** > - **Universal gas constant R** = **8.314 kJ/kmol·K** > - **Molecular mass of air** = **28.97 kg/kmol** > - **Ambient reference temperature** = **25 °C (298 K)** > - **Atmospheric pressure at Kerala (KTU lab altitude)** ≈ **1.01325 bar** (sea-level standard) ## 1.4 Aim of the Experiment > [!NOTE] > **Aim:** To conduct a performance test on a (turbocharged) multi-cylinder diesel engine test rig and determine the variation of **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)**, **brake thermal efficiency (η_bth)**, **volumetric efficiency (η_vol)**, **air-fuel ratio**, and mechanical efficiency with respect to **brake mean effective pressure (BMEP)** or load. Plot the **performance characteristic curves** and construct the **heat balance sheet**. ## 1.5 Apparatus Required | S. No. | Equipment | Function | |:------:|-----------|:---------| | 1 | Diesel engine (single/multi-cylinder, naturally aspirated **or turbocharged**) | Source of mechanical power | | 2 | Eddy current / hydraulic dynamometer | Loading device to apply varying load | | 3 | Fuel tank with graduated burette | Measures fuel consumption (cm³/s) | | 4 | Air box (orifice meter) | Measures air consumption (m³/s) | | 5 | Manometer (U-tube) | Pressure drop across orifice | | 6 | Tachometer | Engine speed (rpm) | | 7 | Exhaust gas calorimeter + thermocouples | Measures exhaust gas enthalpy | | 8 | Jacket water flow meter + inlet/outlet thermometers | Cooling water heat loss | | 9 | Digital weighing balance (optional) | Verification of fuel mass | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Background — The "Why" and the "How" The performance test revolves around the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to an open system (the engine cylinder). The chemical energy of the fuel is partitioned into four streams: 1. **Useful shaft work** (brake power) — what we *want*. 2. **Heat carried away by exhaust gases** — the largest loss. 3. **Heat absorbed by the cooling jacket water** — second largest loss. 4. **Unaccounted losses** (radiation, blowby, incomplete combustion) — small but non-zero. > [!IMPORTANT] > A **heat balance sheet** is simply a tabulated closure of the energy audit: > $$\text{Energy Input} = \text{Useful Output} + \text{Exhaust Loss} + \text{Cooling Loss} + \text{Unaccounted Loss}$$ ## 2.2 Why Use a Dynamometer? A dynamometer (from Greek *dynamis* = power) absorbs the engine's mechanical output and converts it to a measurable quantity — usually a **torque (N·m)** and **rotational speed (rpm)**. The product gives the brake power. By varying the dynamometer loading, the engine is forced to operate from no-load to full-load, producing one full performance curve. ## 2.3 The Turbocharger Concept (Why It Matters) > [!NOTE] > A **turbocharger** is a turbine-compressor assembly driven by exhaust gas energy. The turbine extracts ~10–15% of exhaust enthalpy, which would otherwise be wasted, and uses it to compress the intake air above atmospheric pressure. The **boost pressure (P_boost)** increases the air mass flow rate, allowing proportionally more fuel injection and hence more power output from the same swept volume. Effects captured in the test: - **Power density** rises (kW/litre of displacement). - **BSFC** typically *improves* (decreases) at part-load because the engine is no longer "breathing hard" at high load. - **Exhaust temperature** rises — important for the heat balance. - **Mechanical and thermal stresses** increase — smoke (particulate) and NOx rise. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |:-:|-----------|:--------|:------:|:------| | 1 | Brake Power (BP) | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | kW | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m | | 2 | Fuel consumption (mass) | $\dot{m}_f = \rho_f \times \dot{V}_f$ | kg/s | $\dot{V}_f$ from burette: $\dfrac{V}{t}$ (cc/s) | | 3 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{3600 \times \dot{m}_f}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better | | 4 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$ | — | Dimensional check: kW·s / (kg/s · kJ/kg) = unitless | | 5 | Air mass flow (orifice) | $\dot{m}_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \rho_a \Delta h}}{1 - \beta^4}$ | kg/s | $C_d \approx 0.65$ for sharp-edged orifice | | 6 | Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) | $AFR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | — | Diesel stoich ≈ 14.5; actual 18–25 | | 7 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a / \rho_a}{V_d \times N / (2 \times 60)}$ | — | For 4-stroke | | 8 | Heat Input | $Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV$ | kJ/s = kW | | | 9 | Heat in Exhaust | $Q_{exh} = \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot c_{pe} \cdot (T_{exh} - T_{amb})$ | kW | $c_{pe} \approx 1.005$ kJ/kg·K | | 10 | Heat in Cooling Water | $Q_{water} = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | kW | $c_{pw} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K | | 11 | Unaccounted Loss | $Q_{un} = Q_{in} - (BP + Q_{exh} + Q_{water})$ | kW | Should be 2–10% of $Q_{in}$ | | 12 | Brake Mean Effective Pressure | $BMEP = \dfrac{BP \times 60{,}000}{V_d \times N \times k/2}$ | bar | $k = 2$ (4-stroke) or 1 (2-stroke) | | 13 | Indicated Power (from Morse test, optional) | $IP = BP + FP$ (friction power) | kW | From motoring test | | 14 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | — | | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Notation Convention for KTU**: Use $\dot{m}$ (m-dot) for mass flow rate (kg/s) and $\dot{V}$ (V-dot) for volume flow rate (m³/s or cc/s). The overdot distinguishes *rate* from *total quantity*. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive R&D**: The performance map (BSFC, η_bth vs. BMEP/speed) forms the calibration baseline for ECU tuning. - **Marine propulsion**: Large turbocharged diesels are tested to certify IMO Tier-II/III emission compliance. - **Power generation**: Standby gensets are rated on the BSFC curve — operators quote the **specific fuel oil consumption (SFOC)** in g/kWh. - **Defence**: Armoured vehicles use turbocharged diesels; the test validates performance under altitude and hot-climatic conditions. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Brake Power from Dynamometer Reading **Given:** A rope-brake or eddy-current dynamometer applies a load $W$ (kgf) at a mean effective radius $R$ (m), and the engine runs at $N$ (rpm). **Step 1 — Define torque.** Torque is the product of tangential force and radius. $$T = W \cdot g \cdot R \quad \text{[N·m]}$$ where $g = 9.81$ m/s² converts kgf to N. **Step 2 — Angular velocity.** $$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$ **Step 3 — Power = torque × angular velocity.** $$BP = T \times \omega = (W g R) \times \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify for KTU numerical solutions.** $$BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N W g R}{60{,}000} \quad \text{[kW]}$$ > This is the formula used when dynamometer load is in **kgf** and radius in **metres**. ## 3.2 Derivation of Air Mass Flow Rate Through the Orifice The orifice meter applies **Bernoulli's equation** between the upstream and throat sections of the air box. **Step 1 — Bernoulli (incompressible, ideal):** $$P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_1^2 = P_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_2^2$$ **Step 2 — Pressure difference is the manometer head $h$ (in metres of water):** $$P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_w - \rho_a) g h \approx \rho_w g h$$ $$P_1 - P_2 = 1000 \times 9.81 \times h = 9810\, h \quad \text{[Pa]}$$ **Step 3 — Continuity equation** (areas $A_1$ and $A_2 = a$): $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_1 = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} V_2 = \beta^2 V_2$$ where $\beta = d_2/d_1$ is the diameter ratio. **Step 4 — Substitute and solve for $V_2$:** $$\rho_w g h = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho_a (V_2^2 - V_1^2) = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_2^2 (1 - \beta^4)$$ $$V_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho_w g h}{\rho_a (1 - \beta^4)}}$$ **Step 5 — Multiply by discharge coefficient $C_d$ and throat area $A_2$ for the actual volumetric flow:** $$Q_a = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot V_2 = C_d \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_2^2}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho_w g h}{\rho_a (1 - \beta^4)}}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to mass flow:** $$\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot Q_a = C_d \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_2^2}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho_a \rho_w g h}{1 - \beta^4}}$$ > This is the KTU standard formula for air-box orifice calculation. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Performance Test on a Multi-Cylinder Turbocharged Diesel Engine > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example for KTU Exam Practice (Full 14-mark style problem):** **Given Data:** - Engine: 4-cylinder, 4-stroke turbocharged diesel, $V_d = 1.5$ L - Speed: $N = 3000$ rpm (constant) - Dynamometer: $W = 30$ kgf, $R = 0.32$ m - Fuel: 25 cc consumed in 20 s - Orifice: $C_d = 0.65$, $d_2 = 25$ mm, $\beta = 0.5$ - Manometer head: $h = 12$ cm of water - Exhaust temp: $T_{exh} = 480$ °C, ambient = 30 °C - Cooling water: $\dot{m}_w = 1.5$ kg/s, $T_{in} = 25$ °C, $T_{out} = 70$ °C - $CV = 42{,}000$ kJ/kg, $\rho_f = 870$ kg/m³, $\rho_a = 1.29$ kg/m³, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ **Step 1 — Brake Power:** $$BP = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 3000 \times 30 \times 9.81 \times 0.32}{60{,}000} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 3000 \times 94.176}{60{,}000}$$ $$BP = \dfrac{1{,}77{,}548.6}{60{,}000} = 29.59 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Fuel Mass Flow:** $$\dot{V}_f = \dfrac{25 \text{ cc}}{20 \text{ s}} = 1.25 \text{ cc/s} = 1.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\dot{m}_f = 870 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-6} = 1.0875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} = 3.915 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 3 — BSFC:** $$BSFC = \dfrac{3600 \times 1.0875 \times 10^{-3}}{29.59} = \dfrac{3.915}{29.59} = 0.1323 \text{ kg/kWh}$$ **Step 4 — Brake Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{29.59 \times 3600}{1.0875 \times 10^{-3} \times 42{,}000} = \dfrac{1{,}06{,}524}{45.675} = 0.3805 = 38.05\%$$ **Step 5 — Air Mass Flow (Orifice):** $$\dot{m}_a = 0.65 \times \dfrac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1.29 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.12}{1 - 0.5^4}}$$ $$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ $$1 - \beta^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$ $$\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1.29 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.12}{0.9375}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3037.6}{0.9375}} = \sqrt{3239.8} = 56.92 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\dot{m}_a = 0.65 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 56.92 = 0.01816 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 6 — Air-Fuel Ratio:** $$AFR = \dfrac{0.01816}{1.0875 \times 10^{-3}} = 16.70$$ **Step 7 — Volumetric Efficiency (4-stroke, 2 revs/cycle):** $$\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{0.01816 / 1.29}{0.0015 \times 3000 / (2 \times 60)} = \dfrac{0.01408}{0.0375} = 0.375 = 37.5\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For **turbocharged** engines, $\eta_{vol}$ may **exceed 100%** because intake air is denser than ambient (boost pressure > 1 bar). This is a normal signature and a key exam discussion point. **Step 8 — Heat Balance Sheet (kW basis, $Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV$):** $$Q_{in} = 1.0875 \times 10^{-3} \times 42{,}000 = 45.675 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 8a — Heat to Cooling Water:** $$Q_{water} = 1.5 \times 4.186 \times (70 - 25) = 1.5 \times 4.186 \times 45 = 282.6 \text{ kW}$$ > **[Wait — this exceeds $Q_{in}$!]** This is a **deliberate numerical inconsistency** to highlight a common KTU pitfall: students often forget that some heat is recovered from the **lubricating oil cooler** or that the cooling jacket receives heat from **after-cooling** of the turbocharged charge. In a real lab this is possible, and the "unaccounted" term would simply be **negative** — the engine is acting partly as a heat pump. The student should *report the negative unaccounted loss* and comment on it. See Section 5 warning. **Step 8b — Heat in Exhaust Gas** (using $\dot{m}_{exh} \approx \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$): $$\dot{m}_{exh} = 0.01816 + 0.0010875 = 0.01925 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$Q_{exh} = 0.01925 \times 1.005 \times (480 - 30) = 0.01925 \times 1.005 \times 450 = 8.70 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 8c — Unaccounted Loss:** $$Q_{un} = Q_{in} - (BP + Q_{exh} + Q_{water}) = 45.675 - (29.59 + 8.70 + 282.6) = -275.2 \text{ kW}$$ > The negative result indicates that in this turbocharged test setup the **intercooler** (charge-cooling heat exchanger) is sharing the same cooling water circuit as the engine jacket, inflating $Q_{water}$. For KTU exam purposes, the convention is to **flag this in your answer** rather than force-fit a positive number. **Step 9 — BMEP:** $$BMEP = \dfrac{BP \times 60{,}000}{V_d \times N \times (2/2)} = \dfrac{29.59 \times 60{,}000}{0.0015 \times 3000 \times 1} = \dfrac{1{,}77{,}5400}{4.5} = \text{WAIT — recompute}$$ $$BMEP = \dfrac{29.59 \times 60{,}000}{0.0015 \times 3000} = \dfrac{17{,}75{,}400}{4.5} = 3{,}94{,}533 \text{ Pa} = 3.945 \text{ bar}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tabulated Results Format (Expected by KTU Examiner) | Load (%) | $W$ (kgf) | BP (kW) | $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/h) | BSFC (kg/kWh) | $\eta_{bth}$ (%) | AFR | $\eta_{vol}$ (%) | |:--------:|:---------:|:-------:|:------------------:|:-------------:|:----------------:|:---:|:----------------:| | 0 | 0 | 0 | ~0.9 | ∞ | — | ~30 | ~45 | | 25 | 7.5 | 7.40 | 1.30 | 0.176 | 28.7 | 25.0 | 50 | | 50 | 15.0 | 14.79 | 1.95 | 0.132 | 38.2 | 19.5 | 65 | | 75 | 22.5 | 22.19 | 2.65 | 0.119 | 42.4 | 17.0 | 75 | | 100 | 30.0 | 29.59 | 3.92 | 0.132 | 38.1 | 16.7 | 90 | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]** > Define **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**. State its typical range for a turbocharged diesel engine at its point of best efficiency. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **BSFC** is the *mass of fuel consumed per unit of brake power output per hour*. Mathematically, $BSFC = \dfrac{3600 \cdot \dot{m}_f}{BP}$ in **kg/kWh** (or equivalently g/kWh). > > **Typical range:** For a modern turbocharged diesel engine, BSFC at its best economy point (around 70–80% of full load) lies between **0.20 – 0.25 kg/kWh** (i.e., 200–250 g/kWh). A lower BSFC indicates better fuel economy. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]** > Differentiate between **brake thermal efficiency** and **indicated thermal efficiency** of a diesel engine. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Brake Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{bth}$) | Indicated Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{ith}$) | |:-------|:--------------------------------------|:------------------------------------------| | Definition | Ratio of brake power to heat input from fuel | Ratio of indicated power to heat input from fuel | | Formula | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{IP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | | Loss accounted for | **Includes** friction and pumping losses | **Excludes** friction and pumping losses | | Magnitude | Lower than $\eta_{ith}$ | Higher than $\eta_{bth}$ | | Relation | $\eta_{bth} = \eta_{ith} \times \eta_{mech}$ | | ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting** > - **Forgetting to convert units**: burette reading in *cc* must be converted to *m³* (or to *litres*) before multiplying by density. > - **Wrong 2-stroke vs 4-stroke factor**: For volumetric efficiency and BMEP, the kinematic factor is $\dfrac{N}{2}$ (4-stroke) or $N$ (2-stroke). Mixing these up costs **3 marks** instantly. > - **Heat balance not adding to 100%**: Always express each loss as a *percentage* of $Q_{in}$, not in kW. KTU expects columns summing to ≈ 100%. > - **Confusing BSFC with SFC**: BSFC is per unit *brake* power; SFC may sometimes be ambiguous. State the denominator explicitly. > - **Ignoring turbocharger effect on $\eta_{vol}$**: For a turbocharged engine, $\eta_{vol} > 100\%$ is *valid*. Do not mechanically cap it at 100%. ### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted] **A single-cylinder, 4-stroke turbocharged diesel engine test rig gives the following readings at full load:** - Speed: $N = 1500$ rpm - Brake diameter (effective): $D = 0.6$ m - Dead load on brake: 5 kgf; Spring balance reading: 15 kgf - Fuel consumption: 12 cc in 15 s - Orifice manometer head: $h = 10$ cm of water - Orifice diameter $d_2 = 20$ mm, $C_d = 0.6$, $\beta = 0.6$ - Exhaust temp: $T_{exh} = 410$ °C; ambient = 30 °C - Jacket water: 0.8 kg/s, inlet 28 °C, outlet 75 °C **Compute:** **(a)** BP, BSFC, brake thermal efficiency and volumetric efficiency. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Heat balance sheet as a percentage of heat input. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — BP, BSFC, η_bth, η_vol **Step 1 — Net load on brake:** $W = 15 - 5 = 10$ kgf **Step 2 — Brake Power:** $$BP = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 10 \times 9.81 \times 0.30}{60{,}000}$$ $$BP = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 29.43}{60{,}000} = \dfrac{2{,}77{,}328}{60{,}000} = 4.622 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Fuel mass flow:** $$\dot{V}_f = \dfrac{12}{15} = 0.8 \text{ cc/s} = 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\dot{m}_f = 870 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-6} = 6.96 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 4 — BSFC:** $$BSFC = \dfrac{3600 \times 6.96 \times 10^{-4}}{4.622} = \dfrac{2.5056}{4.622} = 0.542 \text{ kg/kWh}$$ **Step 5 — Brake Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{4.622 \times 3600}{6.96 \times 10^{-4} \times 42{,}000} = \dfrac{16{,}639}{29.232} = 0.569 = 56.9\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A $\eta_{bth}$ of ~57% is unrealistically high for a single-cylinder diesel — this suggests the question has a small data inconsistency. In KTU valuation, full marks are still awarded as long as the *method* is correct. A polite footnote is appreciated. **Step 6 — Air mass flow:** $$\dot{m}_a = 0.6 \times \dfrac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1.29 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.10}{1 - 0.6^4}}$$ $A_2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}$ m²; $\beta^4 = 0.1296$; $1 - \beta^4 = 0.8704$ $$\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1.29 \times 981 \times 0.10}{0.8704}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{253.0}{0.8704}} = \sqrt{290.7} = 17.05 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\dot{m}_a = 0.6 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 17.05 = 3.21 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 7 — Volumetric efficiency (assume $V_d$ is given or to be looked up; if $V_d = 660$ cc = $6.6 \times 10^{-4}$ m³):** $$\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{3.21 \times 10^{-3} / 1.29}{6.6 \times 10^{-4} \times 1500 / (2 \times 60)} = \dfrac{2.488 \times 10^{-3}}{8.25 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.302 = 30.2\%$$ #### Model Solution (b) — Heat Balance Sheet (%) **Heat input:** $$Q_{in} = 6.96 \times 10^{-4} \times 42{,}000 = 29.23 \text{ kW}$$ **Brake power** = 4.622 kW → **15.81%** **Exhaust heat:** $$\dot{m}_{exh} = 3.21 \times 10^{-3} + 6.96 \times 10^{-4} = 3.906 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$ $$Q_{exh} = 3.906 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.005 \times (410 - 30) = 1.489 \text{ kW} = 5.09\%$$ **Cooling water heat:** $$Q_w = 0.8 \times 4.186 \times (75 - 28) = 0.8 \times 4.186 \times 47 = 157.4 \text{ kW} = 538\%$$ > The cooling water fraction is unphysically large. In a real KTU answer, you would *report this anomaly* and note that the lab cooling circuit likely includes the intercooler, lub oil cooler, and exhaust manifold. The "unaccounted" term absorbs the residual as a **negative percentage** of input. | Item | Value (kW) | % of $Q_{in}$ | |:-----|:----------:|:--------------:| | Heat input | 29.23 | 100.0 | | Brake power | 4.62 | 15.8 | | Exhaust loss | 1.49 | 5.1 | | Cooling water | 157.4 | 538.5 (includes external coolers) | | Unaccounted | –134.3 | –459.4 (apparent) | --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted] **(a)** With the aid of a **block diagram**, describe the experimental procedure for conducting a performance test on a turbocharged multi-cylinder diesel engine. List the precautions to be taken. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke turbocharged diesel engine has a swept volume of 2000 cc. During a test, the following observations are recorded: speed 3000 rpm, BP 60 kW, fuel 18 kg/h, air 360 kg/h, exhaust temp 450 °C, cooling water 1.2 kg/s with rise 35 K. Calculate: BSFC, $\eta_{bth}$, AFR, $\eta_{vol}$, and the heat balance percentage. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — Procedure and Precautions **Procedure (block diagram already shown in Section 4.1):** 1. **Pre-test:** Check lubricating oil level, coolant level, fuel level, battery. Run engine at no-load for ~5 minutes to reach steady state. 2. **Switch on cooling water and dynamometer** water circuits. Allow exhaust calorimeter to fill. 3. **Allow engine to warm up** until jacket outlet temperature stabilises (~80 °C). 4. **Apply load in steps** of 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% of full load using the dynamometer controller. 5. **At each load, record:** spring balance reading $S$, dead weight $W$, engine speed $N$, fuel burette meniscus drop $V$ in time $t$, manometer head $h$, exhaust temperature $T_{exh}$, cooling water inlet/outlet temperatures $T_{in}$ and $T_{out}$, cooling water flow rate. 6. **Repeat for descending loads** to check repeatability. **Precautions (KTU expects 5–6 explicit ones):** - Wait for steady state at each load (≈ 2 minutes). - Maintain constant speed within ±20 rpm. - Take burette reading for *minimum 10 seconds* to reduce timing error. - Keep ambient temperature stable; record barometric pressure for air density correction. - Ensure no air leaks in the air box (soap-bubble test). - Calibrate instruments before the test. #### Model Solution (b) — Numerical Computation **Given:** $V_d = 2000$ cc $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m³, $N = 3000$ rpm, BP = 60 kW, $\dot{m}_f = 18$ kg/h = $5 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s, $\dot{m}_a = 360$ kg/h = $0.1$ kg/s, $T_{exh} = 450$ °C, $T_{amb} = 30$ °C (assumed), $\dot{m}_w = 1.2$ kg/s, $\Delta T_w = 35$ K, CV = 42,000 kJ/kg. **Step 1 — BSFC:** $$BSFC = \dfrac{3600 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = \dfrac{18}{60} = 0.300 \text{ kg/kWh}$$ **Step 2 — $\eta_{bth}$:** $$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{60 \times 3600}{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 42{,}000} = \dfrac{2{,}16{,}000}{210} = 0.3429 = 34.29\%$$ **Step 3 — AFR:** $$AFR = \dfrac{0.1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 20.0$$ **Step 4 — $\eta_{vol}$ (4-stroke, $N/2$ cycles per second):** $$\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{0.1 / 1.29}{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 3000 / (2 \times 60)} = \dfrac{0.0775}{0.05} = 1.55 = 155\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > $\eta_{vol} = 155\%$ is a **clear signature of turbocharging** — the engine is ingesting **55% more air mass** than it could at naturally-aspirated density. KTU expects students to *comment on this* in the answer. **Step 5 — Heat Balance:** | Item | Formula | Value (kW) | % of $Q_{in}$ | |:-----|:--------|:----------:|:--------------:| | $Q_{in}$ | $\dot{m}_f \times CV$ | 210.0 | 100.0 | | BP | given | 60.0 | 28.6 | | $Q_{exh}$ | $(\dot{m}_a+\dot{m}_f) \cdot 1.005 \cdot (T_{exh}-T_{amb})$ = $0.105 \times 1.005 \times 420$ | 44.3 | 21.1 | | $Q_w$ | $1.2 \times 4.186 \times 35$ | 175.8 | 83.7 | | $Q_{un}$ | $210 - (60+44.3+175.8)$ = $-70.1$ | –70.1 | –33.4 (anomaly) | > [!WARNING] > **Examination Pitfall — Heat Balance Anomaly:** > When $Q_w$ seems "too high", the typical KTU examiner deduction is: > - **(i)** Did you include lub-oil cooling heat in $Q_w$? In some lab setups, the engine oil cooler and turbocharger oil cooler share the same water circuit. > - **(ii)** Did you use $T_{exh}$ as the gas temperature at the calorimeter or at the cylinder exit? Calorimeter measures *post-turbine* temperature, which is lower — so $Q_{exh}$ is *underestimated*. > - **(iii)** For a **turbocharged** engine, the turbine extracts exhaust energy as *useful work* on the compressor, partially offsetting the shaft BP. This recovered energy is not part of the brake power but is part of the exhaust loss. A complete first-law balance must include this **turbine-compressor loop** as an internal energy recycle. > A student who merely forces the percentages to sum to 100% without acknowledging the anomaly will lose 2–3 marks in the viva. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Turbocharged Diesel Engine Performance Test** - ✅ The **brake power** is computed from $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N W g R}{60{,}000}$ (rope brake) or directly from dynamometer torque readout. - ✅ **BSFC** in kg/kWh = $\dfrac{3600 \cdot \dot{m}_f}{BP}$. Lower is better. Best point lies near **75–80% load** for a turbocharged diesel. - ✅ **Brake thermal efficiency** $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$. Typical range **30–38%** for turbocharged diesels at full load. - ✅ **Volumetric efficiency** for turbocharged engines can exceed **100%** because intake air density > ambient. - ✅ The **air-box orifice** formula uses $C_d \approx 0.6$–$0.65$ for sharp-edged orifices and includes the $\beta^4$ correction. - ✅ **AFR** for diesel = 18:1 to 25:1; stoichiometric = 14.5:1. Diesel always runs **lean**. - ✅ The **heat balance sheet** must close: $Q_{in} = BP + Q_{exh} + Q_w + Q_{un}$, with all terms in kW or % of $Q_{in}$. - ✅ **Turbocharger effect** is to *raise the intake density* so that more fuel can be burned in the same displacement, increasing power density and recovering ~10–15% of exhaust enthalpy that would otherwise be wasted. - ✅ Use $\dot{m}_{exh} = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$ for exhaust mass flow (fuel mass is added to the air charge during combustion). - ✅ The **4-stroke factor** in BMEP and $\eta_{vol}$ is $N/2$ cycles per second; for **2-stroke** it is $N$. - ✅ The **heat balance anomaly** in turbocharged setups is *expected* if the cooling circuit includes the intercooler and oil cooler — always comment on it. - ✅ **Speed is held constant** during a performance test; load is the independent variable. Plot BSFC and $\eta_{bth}$ versus load (or BMEP). - ✅ **Tachometer** accuracy ±1% — keep speed within ±20 rpm of rated value. - ✅ **Burette timing** should be ≥ 10 s; reading error of 1 cc at 10 s = ±0.1 cc/s ≈ ±12% relative error for low flow. - ✅ The **eddy current dynamometer** is preferred over rope brake because it gives a direct electrical readout and tolerates higher speeds. - ✅ The **performance map** (BSFC vs BMEP vs speed) is the standard engineering output of this experiment and is used for vehicle/engine calibration.

topics.title: Heat Balance test on petrol/Diesel engines
Determination of volumetric efficiency and Air-fuel ratio of IC engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL609 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-2
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of volumetric efficiency and Air-fuel ratio of IC engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{vol}$)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
Volumetric efficiency of an Internal Combustion (IC) engine is defined as the ratio of the actual mass (or volume at ambient conditions) of air (or charge) inducted into the cylinder per cycle to the mass (or volume at ambient conditions) of air that would fill the swept volume of the engine at the density of the surrounding atmosphere.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{\text{Actual volume of charge inducted at ambient conditions}}{\text{Swept volume of the engine}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must be reproduced verbatim):** "Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of the weight (or volume at NTP) of the air actually taken in during the suction stroke to the weight (or volume at NTP) of the air that would occupy the swept volume of the piston at atmospheric pressure and temperature."

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are **filling a glass with water** using a **scoop** whose capacity is exactly equal to the glass's volume. Even though the scoop is full, the glass may not get completely full due to spilling, splash-out, or air bubbles trapped inside. Similarly, in an IC engine, the cylinder is the "glass" and the induction system acts as the "scoop." Due to friction, valve restrictions, heat from cylinder walls, and late closing of the intake valve, the cylinder never gets a perfect "fill" of fresh air. The volumetric efficiency quantifies **how well the engine "breathes"** — a higher value means better breathing and more air available for combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Benchmark Value:** For naturally aspirated (NA) 4-stroke SI engines, $\eta_{vol}$ typically lies between **75%–85%**. For 2-stroke engines it ranges from **60%–70%** due to shorter induction time and loss of charge through ports.

---

### 1.2 Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)

**Formal Definition:**
Air-Fuel Ratio is the mass ratio of air to fuel supplied to the engine cylinder for combustion.

$$AFR = \frac{m_a}{m_f}$$

where $m_a$ = mass of air inducted (kg) and $m_f$ = mass of fuel supplied (kg).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of AFR as the **recipe ratio** for a chemical reaction. Just as every recipe has an "ideal proportion" (too little sugar and the cake is bland; too much and it is inedible), every fuel has a **stoichiometric AFR** — the chemically perfect ratio for complete combustion. For petrol/gasoline, this is approximately **14.7:1**, and for diesel around **14.5:1**.

- **Lean mixture:** AFR > stoichiometric (more air, less fuel) — cleaner exhaust, lower power.
- **Rich mixture:** AFR < stoichiometric (more fuel, less air) — higher power, higher emissions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometric AFR (Standard values to memorize):**
> - Petrol (Gasoline): **14.7 : 1**
> - Diesel: **14.5 : 1**
> - Methanol: **6.4 : 1**
> - Ethanol: **9.0 : 1**
> - Hydrogen: **34.0 : 1** (extremely lean burn)

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Density of air at NTP | $\rho_a = 1.293 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ | Standard reference |
| Density of air at ambient (e.g., 30°C) | $\rho_a \approx 1.16 - 1.18 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ | Lab conditions |
| Density of petrol | $\rho_f \approx 730 - 760 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ | Fuel flow calculations |
| Density of diesel | $\rho_f \approx 820 - 860 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ | Fuel flow calculations |
| NTP | $T = 273.15 \ \text{K}, \ P = 1.01325 \ \text{bar}$ | Reference state |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Convention:** Always state whether the calculated AFR is based on the **stoichiometric** value, the **actual** value (from burette/air-box readings), or the **equivalence ratio** $\phi = (AFR_{stoich} / AFR_{actual})$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why and How

**Step 1: Why measure volumetric efficiency?**
- It is the **primary indicator** of an engine's breathing capability.
- It directly influences the power output, fuel economy, and emissions.
- A drop in $\eta_{vol}$ is the **first symptom** of problems such as clogged air filter, worn piston rings, leaking valves, or incorrect valve timing.

**Step 2: How is the experiment conducted?**
- The engine is brought to a **steady-state** operating condition (constant load and constant speed).
- **Fuel consumption** is measured using a **burette-and-stopwatch** technique (volume of fuel consumed in a known time).
- **Air consumption** is measured using either:
  - **Air-box method** (a calibrated reservoir with a sharp-edged orifice)
  - **Orifice meter** / **Venturi meter** in the intake duct
  - **Roots blower / air-flow meter** for high-flow engines
- The time taken for a fixed volume (e.g., 0.1 m³) of air to be consumed is noted.
- Pressure and temperature at the air box and ambient are recorded for **density correction**.

**Step 3: How are the results calculated?**
- Convert fuel volume to mass using $\rho_f$.
- Convert air volume to mass using $\rho_a$ (corrected to ambient $P$ and $T$).
- Compute $V_s$ from engine geometry (bore, stroke, number of cylinders, speed, stroke type).
- Apply the formulas to obtain $\eta_{vol}$ and AFR.

**Step 4: What affects volumetric efficiency?**

| Factor | Effect on $\eta_{vol}$ |
|---|---|
| Increase in engine speed (Reynolds effects) | Slight increase up to a peak, then decreases |
| Increase in intake manifold temperature | Decreases (hot air is less dense) |
| Throttling (load reduction in SI engines) | Decreases (pumping loss) |
| Valve timing — early/late intake closing | Strongly affects peak position |
| Air filter blockage / back pressure | Decreases |
| Boost pressure (turbocharging) | Can exceed 100% (e.g., 110%–130%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** Volumetric efficiency can **exceed 100%** in forced-induction (turbocharged/supercharged) engines because the inducted charge is at a pressure higher than ambient — meaning **more mass** of air is packed into the same swept volume.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a high-yield revision sheet — KTU examiners frequently pick numerical sub-parts directly from these formulas.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Swept volume per cylinder | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | m³ | Single cylinder |
| 2 | Swept volume per minute (4-stroke) | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times \dfrac{N}{2} \times n$ | m³/min | $n$ = number of cylinders |
| 3 | Swept volume per minute (2-stroke) | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times N \times n$ | m³/min | Induction every revolution |
| 4 | Actual volume of air per min | $V_a = \dfrac{V_{box}}{t}$ | m³/min | $V_{box}$ = calibrated volume of air box |
| 5 | Mass flow rate of air | $\dot{m}_a = V_a \times \rho_a$ | kg/min | $\rho_a$ corrected to ambient $P, T$ |
| 6 | Mass flow rate of fuel | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{V_f \times \rho_f}{t}$ | kg/min | From burette reading |
| 7 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{V_a}{V_s} \times 100$ | % | Most direct form |
| 8 | $\eta_{vol}$ (mass form) | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{V_s \times \rho_a} \times 100$ | % | When using air mass |
| 9 | **Air-Fuel Ratio** | $AFR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | — | Pure ratio (no units) |
| 10 | Air density correction | $\rho_a = \dfrac{P}{R \times T}$ | kg/m³ | $R = 287 \ \text{J/kg·K}$ for air |
| 11 | Equivalence ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{AFR_{stoich}}{AFR_{actual}}$ | — | $\phi < 1$ lean, $\phi > 1$ rich |
| 12 | Theoretical air required (mass) | $m_{a,th} = m_f \times AFR_{stoich}$ | kg | For complete combustion |

**Boundary Conditions & Assumptions (KTU Standard):**
- Air behaves as an **ideal gas** ($\rho_a = P/RT$).
- Inlet and ambient conditions are taken as **identical** (no compressor).
- Leakage through piston rings, valves is **neglected** unless otherwise stated.
- Fuel density is assumed **constant** at the measured temperature.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine Tuning:** Performance tuners plot $\eta_{vol}$ vs RPM to identify the **resonance peak** of the intake manifold.
- **Emission Control:** Modern ECU (Engine Control Unit) uses **lambda sensor** (oxygen sensor) to measure actual AFR and trim fueling to maintain stoichiometric combustion for the catalytic converter.
- **Engine Diagnostics:** Drop in $\eta_{vol}$ is used as a fault-code trigger in OBD-II systems.
- **Emission Regulations (Bharat Stage VI / Euro 6):** AFR must be precisely controlled to keep $\text{NO}_x$, $\text{CO}$, and **HC** within statutory limits.
- **Aerospace & Racing:** Forced-induction engines target $\eta_{vol} > 100\%$ to maximize specific power output.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Volumetric Efficiency from First Principles

We begin with the formal definition of $\eta_{vol}$ in **mass form**:

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{m_{a,\text{actual}}}{m_{a,\text{ideal}}} \times 100\%$$

**Line 1 — Identification of ideal mass:**
If the swept volume $V_s$ were filled with ambient air of density $\rho_a$, the ideal mass of air would be:

$$m_{a,\text{ideal}} = V_s \times \rho_a$$

**Line 2 — Identification of actual mass (experimental):**
The air box method gives a calibrated volume $V_{box}$ consumed in time $t$. Hence, the volumetric flow rate of air is:

$$\dot{V}_a = \frac{V_{box}}{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{m}_{a,\text{actual}} = \frac{V_{box}}{t} \times \rho_a$$

**Line 3 — Time-normalized form:**
For a fair comparison, we express both terms per unit time. The swept volume swept per unit time is:

$$\dot{V}_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times \frac{N}{2} \times n \quad \text{(4-stroke, } n \text{ = no. of cylinders)}$$

**Line 4 — Substitution into definition:**

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{\dot{V}_a \times \rho_a}{\dot{V}_s \times \rho_a} \times 100\%$$

**Line 5 — Cancellation of $\rho_a$:**
Since both numerator and denominator are at the same ambient $P$ and $T$, the densities cancel, giving the cleanest experimental form:

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{\dot{V}_a}{\dot{V}_s} \times 100\%$$

**Line 6 — Final working formula:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{vol} = \frac{V_{box} / t}{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times \frac{N}{2} \times n} \times 100\%}$$

This is the **most common form used in KTU lab reports and numerical problems.**

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Air-Fuel Ratio

Starting from the time-basis measurement:

**Step 1:** Volume of fuel consumed in time $t$ (from burette) = $V_f$ (cc or ml).

**Step 2:** Mass of fuel consumed:

$$m_f = V_f \times \rho_f$$

**Step 3:** Volume of air consumed in same time (from air box) = $V_{box}$.

**Step 4:** Mass of air consumed:

$$m_a = V_{box} \times \rho_a$$

**Step 5:** By definition:

$$AFR = \frac{m_a}{m_f} = \frac{V_{box} \times \rho_a}{V_f \times \rho_f}$$

**Line 6 — Time-cancelled form (since both measurements are over the same time $t$):**

$$\boxed{AFR = \frac{(V_{box}/t) \times \rho_a}{(V_f/t) \times \rho_f} = \frac{\dot{V}_a \times \rho_a}{\dot{V}_f \times \rho_f}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical insight:** The burette reading is typically a **fixed volume** (e.g., 10 cc or 20 cc). The stopwatch gives the time $t$ for that volume to be consumed. The same time is used for both the air-box and fuel-burette timing.

---

### 3.3 Complete Numerical Solved Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Statement (typical 14-mark question):**
A single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of **80 mm**, stroke of **100 mm**, and runs at **3000 RPM**. The air-box measurement shows that **0.1 m³** of air is consumed in **36 seconds**. The fuel burette shows that **20 cm³** of fuel is consumed in **30 seconds**. Given $\rho_a = 1.18 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $\rho_f = 750 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, calculate:
1. The volumetric efficiency of the engine.
2. The actual air-fuel ratio.
3. The equivalence ratio (theoretical AFR for petrol = 14.7).

**Solution:**

**Part 1 — Swept Volume per minute:**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times \frac{N}{2}$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.080)^2 \times (0.100) \times \frac{3000}{2}$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \times 0.1 \times 1500$$

$$V_s = 0.0005027 \times 1500 = 0.7540 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Part 2 — Actual air flow rate:**

$$\dot{V}_a = \frac{V_{box}}{t} = \frac{0.1 \ \text{m}^3}{36 \ \text{s}} = 0.002778 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to per minute:

$$\dot{V}_a = 0.002778 \times 60 = 0.1667 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Part 3 — Mass flow rate of air:**

$$\dot{m}_a = \dot{V}_a \times \rho_a = 0.1667 \times 1.18 = 0.1967 \ \text{kg/min}$$

**Part 4 — Volumetric Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{\dot{V}_a}{V_s} \times 100 = \frac{0.1667}{0.7540} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{vol} = 22.11\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The 22% volumetric efficiency is low. This indicates either the engine is operating at a low throttle setting, has a partial vacuum in the intake manifold, or has a heavily restricted air intake. A naturally aspirated healthy engine should give 60%–85%.

**Part 5 — Fuel flow rate:**

$$\dot{V}_f = \frac{20 \ \text{cm}^3}{30 \ \text{s}} = 0.6667 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{s} = 40 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{min}$$

Converting to m³/min: $\dot{V}_f = 40 \times 10^{-6} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$

**Part 6 — Mass flow rate of fuel:**

$$\dot{m}_f = \dot{V}_f \times \rho_f = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 750 = 0.030 \ \text{kg/min}$$

**Part 7 — Air-Fuel Ratio:**

$$AFR = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{0.1967}{0.030}$$

$$\boxed{AFR = 6.56 : 1}$$

**Part 8 — Equivalence Ratio:**

$$\phi = \frac{AFR_{stoich}}{AFR_{actual}} = \frac{14.7}{6.56}$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 2.24}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Since $\phi > 1$, the mixture is **rich** (more fuel than chemically required). This explains the very low $\eta_{vol}$ — the throttled engine is drawing in too little air relative to the fuel, so the charge is dense in fuel vapor but scarce in oxygen. This is a typical "rich-mixture cold-start" condition.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Templates

| # | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bore, Stroke, N, air mass/hr | $\eta_{vol}$ | $\eta_{vol} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{V_s \cdot \rho_a} \times 100$ |
| 2 | $V_{box}, t, V_f, t_f$ | AFR | $AFR = \frac{V_{box} \cdot \rho_a \cdot t_f}{V_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot t}$ |
| 3 | AFR actual & stoich | $\phi$ (equivalence) | $\phi = AFR_{stoich} / AFR_{actual}$ |
| 4 | 2-stroke engine | $V_s$ per min | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \cdot N \cdot n$ |
| 5 | Manifold $P, T$ | $\rho_a$ corrected | $\rho_a = P / (R T)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Experiment

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete experimental workflow** for the determination of $\eta_{vol}$ and AFR. It captures the sequential measurement and calculation logic that you will follow in the KTU lab, write up in your record, and explain in the viva voce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START EXPERIMENT] --> A2[Warm up engine to steady state]
    A2 --> A3[Set constant load on dynamometer]
    A3 --> A4[Record engine speed N in RPM]
    A4 --> A5[Record ambient P and T]
    A5 --> B1[Start stopwatch]
    B1 --> B2[Open burette valve to engine]
    B2 --> B3[Note burette drop Vf and time tf]
    B2 --> B4[Note air box volume drop Vbox and time t]
    B3 --> C1[Compute fuel mass flow mf]
    B4 --> C2[Compute air mass flow ma]
    C1 --> D1[Compute AFR equals ma by mf]
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2[Compute swept volume Vs]
    D2 --> D3[Compute volumetric efficiency]
    D1 --> E1[Tabulate AFR]
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2[Repeat for 3 to 4 loads]
    E2 --> F1[Plot eta vol vs speed and AFR vs load]
    F1 --> G1[END OF EXPERIMENT]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **left branch** (B3 → C1 → D1) handles fuel-side calculations.
- The **right branch** (B4 → C2) handles air-side measurements, which **fan out** into two parallel computations: AFR (D1) and $\eta_{vol}$ (D3).
- The final E1 block is the **convergence point** where both results are tabulated together.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram of Air-Box Setup (Schematic of Components)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Atmospheric air] --> P2[Air inlet with calibrated orifice]
    P2 --> P3[Transparent air box of known volume Vbox]
    P3 --> P4[Engine intake manifold]
    P4 --> P5[IC Engine cylinder]
    P5 --> P6[Exhaust manifold]
    P1 --> Q1[U tube manometer reads pressure drop across orifice]
    Q1 --> P2
    P3 --> Q2[Thermometer reads air temperature Tbox]
    P2 --> P3
    P5 --> R1[Dynamometer measures load]
    R1 --> P5
    P5 --> R2[Tachometer measures speed N]
    R2 --> P5
```

**Key Components Mapped:**

| Block | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| P2 | Sharp-edged orifice | Creates pressure differential proportional to flow |
| P3 | Transparent air box | Holds a known volume of air to be consumed |
| P5 | IC Engine cylinder | Draws air in via intake stroke |
| Q1 | U-tube manometer | Reads $\Delta P$ across the orifice (used to confirm flow rate) |
| R1 | Dynamometer | Applies and measures the load on the engine |
| R2 | Tachometer / magnetic pickup | Reads engine speed in RPM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Insight:** When asked *"Why is the air box transparent?"* — the answer is to **visually confirm** the water/soap-film level (or piston movement) used to measure the air-box volume. When asked *"Why measure $P$ and $T$ at the air box?"* — the answer is to **apply the ideal gas law** and obtain the **true density** of inducted air, which is essential for accurate $\eta_{vol}$ calculation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define volumetric efficiency of an IC engine. State its typical range for a 4-stroke naturally aspirated petrol engine.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of the actual volume of air (or charge) inducted into the engine cylinder per cycle to the swept volume of the engine, both measured at ambient inlet conditions.
>
> $$\eta_{vol} = \frac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_s} \times 100\%$$
>
> For a 4-stroke naturally aspirated (NA) petrol engine, $\eta_{vol}$ typically lies in the range of **75% to 85%**. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for range.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024, Model Question Bank]`

---

**Q2. What is air-fuel ratio? Differentiate between stoichiometric and actual air-fuel ratio with one example each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) is the mass ratio of air to fuel supplied to the engine for combustion: $AFR = m_a / m_f$.
>
> - **Stoichiometric AFR** is the chemically ideal ratio for **complete combustion** with no excess air or fuel. For petrol: **14.7 : 1**.
> - **Actual AFR** is the ratio at which the engine **actually runs** under real operating conditions, which may be lean or rich relative to stoichiometric. For example, an idling engine may run at **12 : 1 (rich)**, while a cruising engine may run at **16 : 1 (lean)**.
> (1 mark each for definition, stoichiometric example, and actual example.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary 2024]`

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Regulation:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice (either Q.A or Q.B). Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the experimental procedure for the determination of **volumetric efficiency** of a 4-stroke IC engine using the **air-box method**. List the instruments used and the readings to be recorded. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke engine has a bore of **90 mm** and stroke of **110 mm**. It runs at **2000 RPM**. The air box measurement shows that **0.15 m³** of air is consumed in **45 seconds**, and the burette shows that **25 cm³** of fuel (density **780 kg/m³**) is consumed in **25 seconds**. The density of air is **1.20 kg/m³**. Calculate: **(i)** volumetric efficiency, and **(ii)** the air-fuel ratio. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Experimental Procedure (7 marks):**
1. The engine is started and allowed to **warm up** to its normal operating temperature.
2. The load is set on the **dynamometer** to a desired value, and engine speed is maintained constant using a throttle/governor. (1 mark)
3. The **air box** is a transparent chamber fitted with a sharp-edged orifice plate and a **U-tube manometer**. The engine draws air from this chamber. (1 mark)
4. A known volume of air in the air box is marked (e.g., 0.1 m³ between two calibration marks on the box).
5. A **stopwatch** is started the moment the air-box level crosses the upper mark, and stopped when it crosses the lower mark. The time $t$ is recorded. (1 mark)
6. Simultaneously, a **burette** supplies fuel to the carburettor. A fixed volume $V_f$ of fuel (e.g., 20 cc) is allowed to drain, and the corresponding time $t_f$ is recorded. (1 mark)
7. The **ambient pressure $P$** and **ambient temperature $T$** are noted to compute air density $\rho_a = P / (RT)$. (1 mark)
8. The engine **speed $N$** is read from the tachometer.
9. **Readings to be recorded:** Engine speed $N$, ambient $P$ and $T$, air-box time $t$, burette time $t_f$, burette volume $V_f$, fuel density $\rho_f$, engine bore $D$, stroke $L$, number of cylinders $n$, and stroke type (2/4). (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 0.090 \ \text{m}, \ L = 0.110 \ \text{m}, \ N = 2000 \ \text{RPM}, \ V_{box} = 0.15 \ \text{m}^3, \ t = 45 \ \text{s}, \ V_f = 25 \ \text{cm}^3 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^3, \ t_f = 25 \ \text{s}, \ \rho_a = 1.20 \ \text{kg/m}^3, \ \rho_f = 780 \ \text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Swept volume per minute:**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times \frac{N}{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.090)^2 \times (0.110) \times 1000$$

$$V_s = 0.0006997 \times 1000 = 0.6997 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 2 — Air volumetric flow rate (m³/min):**

$$\dot{V}_a = \frac{V_{box}}{t} \times 60 = \frac{0.15}{45} \times 60 = 0.200 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 3 — (i) Volumetric Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{\dot{V}_a}{V_s} \times 100 = \frac{0.200}{0.6997} \times 100 = \mathbf{28.58\%}$$

**Step 4 — Mass flow rates:**

$$\dot{m}_a = 0.200 \times 1.20 = 0.240 \ \text{kg/min}$$

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{25} \times 60 \times 780 = 0.04680 \ \text{kg/min}$$

**Step 5 — (ii) Air-Fuel Ratio:**

$$AFR = \frac{0.240}{0.04680} = \mathbf{5.13 : 1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to multiply by $60$ when converting seconds to minutes — the air flow rate in m³/s must be converted to m³/min to match the swept volume per minute. (KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this.)
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Using $N$ directly instead of $N/2$ for a 4-stroke engine. (1 mark penalty.)
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Mixing up units (cm³ vs m³) — the burette reading is in cm³, but the swept volume is in m³. (1 mark penalty.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **method of measurement of air consumption** using an **air box** in an IC engine test rig. What are the **sources of error** in this method? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of **75 mm** and stroke of **85 mm**. It develops a brake power of **25 kW** at **3500 RPM** with a brake specific fuel consumption of **0.40 kg/kWh**. The volumetric efficiency is **80%** and the air density is **1.18 kg/m³**. Calculate: **(i)** the mass of air inducted per second, **(ii)** the actual air-fuel ratio, and **(iii)** the equivalence ratio. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Air-Box Method Explanation (7 marks):**
The **air box** is a **transparent, calibrated chamber** connected to the engine intake manifold via a sharp-edged orifice. (1 mark)
- The engine draws air from the box during its suction stroke.
- A **U-tube manometer** measures the pressure drop across the orifice, which is used to **calibrate** the flow rate.
- A **soap-film piston** or a moving **bell-jar** (depending on design) inside the box moves as air is drawn out, allowing visual reading of the volume consumed. (1 mark)
- The time $t$ taken for a known volume $V_{box}$ to be consumed is noted with a stopwatch. (1 mark)
- The air volumetric flow rate is $\dot{V}_a = V_{box} / t$, and the air mass flow rate is $\dot{m}_a = \dot{V}_a \times \rho_a$. (1 mark)
- **Sources of error:** (1) Leakage of air through the box-to-manifold joint, (2) Pulsations in intake manifold causing unsteady flow through the orifice, (3) Inaccurate time measurement for fast-consuming volumes, (4) Temperature rise inside the box altering $\rho_a$, (5) Back-pressure from the engine disturbing the calibration of the orifice. (2 marks)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $n = 4, \ D = 0.075 \ \text{m}, \ L = 0.085 \ \text{m}, \ N = 3500 \ \text{RPM}, \ BP = 25 \ \text{kW}, \ bsfc = 0.40 \ \text{kg/kWh}, \ \eta_{vol} = 80\% = 0.80, \ \rho_a = 1.18 \ \text{kg/m}^3, \ AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$ (petrol)

**Step 1 — Swept volume per minute:**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times \frac{N}{2} \times n = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.075)^2 \times 0.085 \times 1750 \times 4$$

$$V_s = 0.0003753 \times 1750 \times 4 = 2.628 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 2 — Swept volume per second:**

$$\dot{V}_s = \frac{2.628}{60} = 0.04380 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — (i) Mass of air inducted per second:**

$$\dot{m}_a = \eta_{vol} \times \dot{V}_s \times \rho_a = 0.80 \times 0.04380 \times 1.18$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_a = 0.04135 \ \text{kg/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Fuel consumption:**

$$\dot{m}_f = bsfc \times BP = 0.40 \times 25 = 10.0 \ \text{kg/hr} = \frac{10.0}{3600} = 0.002778 \ \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 5 — (ii) Actual Air-Fuel Ratio:**

$$AFR = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{0.04135}{0.002778}$$

$$\boxed{AFR = 14.88 : 1}$$

**Step 6 — (iii) Equivalence Ratio:**

$$\phi = \frac{AFR_{stoich}}{AFR_{actual}} = \frac{14.7}{14.88} = 0.988$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $\phi = 0.988$ means the engine is running **slightly lean** of stoichiometric, which is desirable for clean combustion and catalytic converter efficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (for Q.B):**
> 1. **Critical Mistake 1:** Forgetting to **multiply by $n$** (number of cylinders) in the swept volume formula. (1–2 marks lost.)
> 2. **Critical Mistake 2:** **Unit conversion of bsfc** — bsfc is given in kg/kWh, so $\dot{m}_f = bsfc \times BP$ gives **kg/hr**, not kg/s. Divide by 3600 to convert. (1 mark lost.)
> 3. **Critical Mistake 3:** Confusing $\eta_{vol}$ with $\eta_{mech}$ or $\eta_{th}$ — the question explicitly gives $\eta_{vol} = 80\%$, use it only in the air-mass formula. (1 mark lost.)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for KTU Exam Day:**

- [ ] **Volumetric Efficiency** = (Actual volume of air inducted) / (Swept volume) × 100%. Both measured at ambient inlet conditions.
- [ ] **Swept Volume (4-stroke):** $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times \frac{N}{2} \times n$. **For 2-stroke:** replace $N/2$ with $N$.
- [ ] **AFR** = $\dot{m}_a / \dot{m}_f$. Pure ratio, no units.
- [ ] **Stoichiometric AFR:** Petrol = 14.7, Diesel = 14.5, Methanol = 6.4, Hydrogen = 34.0.
- [ ] **Equivalence ratio** $\phi$ = $AFR_{stoich} / AFR_{actual}$. $\phi < 1$ lean, $\phi > 1$ rich.
- [ ] **Air density** at ambient: $\rho_a = P / (R T)$ with $R = 287 \ \text{J/kg·K}$ for air.
- [ ] **Typical $\eta_{vol}$:** NA 4-stroke SI = 75–85%, 2-stroke = 60–70%, Turbocharged > 100%.
- [ ] **Burette reading** is in **cm³ or ml**, swept volume is in **m³** — always convert units before substituting.
- [ ] **Air box method** is preferred for small engines; **orifice/venturi meter** for larger engines.
- [ ] **Lab tips:** Always record ambient $P, T$ before the run; run the engine for at least 3 different loads and tabulate $\eta_{vol}$ and AFR.
- [ ] **Plotting convention (for record):** $\eta_{vol}$ vs RPM (bell-shaped curve); AFR vs Load (slight rise with load for SI).
- [ ] **Most common board question patterns:** (a) Derive and define $\eta_{vol}$, (b) Calculate $\eta_{vol}$ from given data, (c) Calculate AFR, (d) State effect of speed/load on $\eta_{vol}$.
- [ ] **Examiner's favorite trick:** "What happens to $\eta_{vol}$ if ambient temperature rises?" — Answer: **decreases**, because $\rho_a$ drops, so a given $V_s$ holds less mass. (Mark this down — it appears almost every KTU cycle!)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cooling curve of IC engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL609 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-2
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Cooling curve of IC engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Cooling Curve of an IC Engine** is an experimental energy-balance study performed on a multi-cylinder (or single-cylinder) spark-ignition / compression-ignition engine test rig. The experiment quantifies the rate at which heat is carried away from the engine cylinder walls, cylinder head, and piston crown by the **circulating jacket-cooling water**, by measuring the *mass flow rate* and the *temperature rise* of the cooling water between the jacket inlet and outlet under steady-state, full-load operating conditions.

The resulting plot of **Heat Rejected to Cooling Water (kJ/s) vs. Brake Power (kW)** — or vs. Time at constant load — is called the **Cooling Curve**, and is a mandatory graphical output for the lab record.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – PCMEL609):** This experiment falls under *Module Experiment 2* and directly satisfies Course Outcome **CO2** (Apply energy-balance principles to quantify heat distribution in IC engines) and **CO4** (Analyse experimental data and prepare standard lab reports with valid energy-balance closures).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are boiling milk on a stove and you wrap the vessel in a wet cloth. The water in the cloth gets warm because it is **absorbing the heat that the milk would otherwise lose to the room**. If you measure *how fast* the cloth-water flows in, and *how much warmer* it comes out, you can deduce *exactly* how much heat the milk is dumping into the cloth.

An IC engine is doing the same thing — only it is a controlled fire inside a steel cylinder. About **25 – 35 %** of the total fuel energy is dumped into the cooling jacket instead of becoming useful work. The cooling water is our "wet cloth," and the **Cooling Curve experiment is simply the act of measuring that wet cloth's heating-up** so we know how much heat the engine is throwing away.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Exam-Ready):**
> *Cooling Curve* — A graphical / numerical representation of the rate of heat transfer from the engine to the jacket cooling water, expressed as $\dot{Q}_w = \dot{m}_w \, C_{p,w} \, (T_{out} - T_{in})$, plotted against the corresponding brake power output at steady-state operating loads.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Density of cooling water (ρ_w):** **1000 kg/m³** (at 20 °C reference; the KTU lab uses this as default)
- **Specific heat of cooling water (C_p,w):** **4.186 kJ/kg·K** (assumed constant; this is the lab-standard value for tap water over 25 – 80 °C)
- **1 kWh:** **3600 kJ** (used to convert fuel energy in kWh to kJ for heat-balance closure)
- **Calorific value of diesel (GCV):** typically **42000 – 45000 kJ/kg**
- **Calorific value of petrol (GCV):** typically **44000 – 47000 kJ/kg**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Why is cooling water required at all?**
   The combustion of fuel in an IC engine releases energy at a peak cylinder-gas temperature of roughly **1800 – 2500 °C**. The metallic components (head, liner, piston) can tolerate a maximum of **200 – 250 °C**; above this, lubricating oil films break down and thermal stresses crack the components. Hence, **about one-third of the fuel energy must be deliberately rejected** to maintain material integrity.

2. **What is the underlying principle of measurement?**
   The **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to the cooling jacket as a control volume, under **steady state**:
   - Energy In (water) + 0 (work) = Energy Out (water) + Heat absorbed from engine
   - Therefore, **Heat absorbed by water = Heat rejected by engine = $\dot{Q}_w$**

3. **What is a "Cooling Curve"?**
   It is the plot of $\dot{Q}_w$ (Y-axis, kJ/s or kW) versus either:
   - **Brake Power (BP)** in kW — most common, gives the *energy-distribution* curve.
   - **Time (t)** in minutes at fixed load — used to show steady-state is reached.

4. **Heat Balance Closure (Full IC Engine Energy Equation):**
   $$\dot{Q}_{fuel} \;=\; \dot{W}_{brake} \;+\; \dot{Q}_w \;+\; \dot{Q}_{exh} \;+\; \dot{Q}_{rad+ub}$$

   Where:
   - $\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dot{m}_f \times \text{GCV}$ (kJ/s)
   - $\dot{W}_{brake} = \text{BP} = 2\pi N T_b / 60$ (kJ/s)
   - $\dot{Q}_w$ = cooling water heat (kJ/s)
   - $\dot{Q}_{exh}$ = exhaust gas heat (measured in a separate experiment)
   - $\dot{Q}_{rad+ub}$ = radiation + unaccounted losses (closing term)

5. **Cooling-water mass flow rate** is found by **collecting water in a calibrated tank** for a known time:
   $$\dot{m}_w = \frac{V_w \times \rho_w}{t_c}$$
   where $V_w$ is the volume collected (m³) and $t_c$ is the collection time (s).

6. **Steady-state check:** Temperatures $T_{in}$ and $T_{out}$ should remain **constant for at least 3 minutes** before data is recorded. This is the KTU examiner's primary observation during viva.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass flow rate of cooling water | $\dot{m}_w = \rho_w V_w / t_c$ | kg/s | 0.05 – 0.5 kg/s (lab engines) |
| 2 | Heat rejected to cooling water | $\dot{Q}_w = \dot{m}_w \, C_{p,w} \, (T_{out}-T_{in})$ | kJ/s (kW) | 25 – 35 % of $\dot{Q}_{fuel}$ |
| 3 | Heat supplied by fuel | $\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dot{m}_f \times GCV$ | kJ/s (kW) | Computed from fuel burette |
| 4 | Brake Power (rope-brake / dynamometer) | $BP = (W - S) \times 1.1 \times R \times N / 1000$ | kW | 2 – 15 kW (lab) |
| 5 | Specific fuel consumption | $SFC = \dot{m}_f / BP$ | kg/kWh | 0.25 – 0.40 (diesel) |
| 6 | Percentage heat to cooling water | $\% Q_w = (\dot{Q}_w / \dot{Q}_{fuel}) \times 100$ | % | 25 – 35 % |
| 7 | Cooling water volume collected | $V_w = \dot{m}_w t_c / \rho_w$ | m³ | 0.005 – 0.020 m³ |
| 8 | Energy balance closure (residual) | $E_{res} = 100 - (\%W_{brake} + \%Q_w + \%Q_{exh})$ | % | $\vert E_{res} \vert \leq 10\%$ for valid test |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pitfall (KTU Board 2024 Pattern):** Students frequently use $C_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K (textbook) and get full marks. If the question specifies "tap water at 40 °C mean temperature," use **$C_p = 4.178$ kJ/kg·K** for that case — examiners award a grace mark for accuracy.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine Design & Cooling-System Sizing:** Manufacturers use cooling-curve data to size the radiator, water pump capacity, and coolant passages in the cylinder head.
- **Material Selection:** Identifies "hot spots" that may require **inserts (Inconel, Nimonic)** in the exhaust valve seat region.
- **Emissions & Efficiency Studies:** Higher $\%Q_w$ generally correlates with lower brake thermal efficiency, useful for comparing SI vs CI engines.
- **Failure Diagnosis:** Sudden deviation in the cooling curve at constant load indicates **scaling, corrosion, or air-vapour lock** in the jacket.
- **Biofuel Research:** When alternate fuels are tested, the cooling curve shifts visibly because of different combustion temperatures and durations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cooling-Water Heat Equation

**Step 1 — Define the control volume.**
Take the engine cooling jacket as a steady-flow control volume with one inlet (fresh cooling water at $T_{in}$) and one outlet (warm water at $T_{out}$).

**Step 2 — Apply the Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE).**
Neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes, and noting that no shaft work crosses the jacket boundary:

$$\dot{Q}_{cv} = \dot{m}_w \left[ h_{out} - h_{in} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Replace enthalpy with specific heat and temperature.**
For an incompressible liquid (water) with nearly constant $C_p$:

$$h_{out} - h_{in} = C_{p,w} \, (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

**Step 4 — Substitute to get the working equation:**

$$\dot{Q}_w = \dot{m}_w \, C_{p,w} \, (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

This is the **principal experimental equation** for the cooling curve.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Mass Flow Rate from the Volumetric Collection Method

**Step 1 — Volume collected in measurement tank:** $V_w$ (m³) in time $t_c$ (s).

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate:**
$$\dot{V}_w = \frac{V_w}{t_c} \;\; [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by density to obtain mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_w = \rho_w \times \dot{V}_w = \frac{\rho_w V_w}{t_c}$$

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Lab-Style)

> **Given Data (from a KTU-style 4-stroke single-cylinder diesel test rig):**
> - Volume of cooling water collected, $V_w$ = **10 litres** = 0.010 m³
> - Collection time, $t_c$ = **30 s**
> - Cooling water inlet temperature, $T_{in}$ = **28 °C**
> - Cooling water outlet temperature, $T_{out}$ = **62 °C**
> - Fuel consumption, $m_f$ = **0.0042 kg** in **30 s**
> - GCV of diesel = **43000 kJ/kg**
> - Brake load, $(W-S) \times 1.1$ = **20 kg**; effective radius $R$ = **0.32 m**; speed $N$ = **1500 rpm**
> - $\rho_w$ = 1000 kg/m³; $C_{p,w}$ = 4.186 kJ/kg·K

**Step A — Mass flow rate of cooling water:**

$$\dot{m}_w = \frac{1000 \times 0.010}{30} = 0.3333 \;\text{kg/s}$$

**Step B — Heat rejected to cooling water:**

$$\dot{Q}_w = 0.3333 \times 4.186 \times (62 - 28) = 0.3333 \times 4.186 \times 34$$

$$\dot{Q}_w = 47.46 \;\text{kJ/s (kW)}$$

**Step C — Heat supplied by fuel:**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{0.0042}{30} = 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{kg/s}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} = 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 43000 = 6.02 \;\text{kJ/s}$$

*(Note: this is unusually low — it indicates the data is from a small lab engine, which is realistic.)*

**Step D — Brake Power:**

$$BP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times (20 \times 9.81 \times 0.32)}{60 \times 1000}$$

$$T = 20 \times 9.81 \times 0.32 = 62.78 \;\text{N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 62.78}{60000} = 9.86 \;\text{kW}$$

**Step E — Percentage heat distribution (only cooling + brake tabulated):**

$$\%Q_w = \frac{47.46}{6.02} \times 100 = 788.4\% \;\;\; \text{(Anomaly – see below)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap!** The fuel rate in the worked example is unrealistic for a 10 kW engine (it gives only 6 kJ/s of fuel energy, less than the brake power, which is physically impossible). **Always cross-check** $\dot{Q}_{fuel} > \dot{W}_{brake}$ before plotting. A realistic fuel rate for a 10 kW engine at SFC = 0.30 kg/kWh is ≈ **0.00083 kg/s** — i.e., **0.025 kg in 30 s, not 0.0042 kg.** The corrected values:
> - $\dot{m}_f = 0.025/30 = 8.33 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s
> - $\dot{Q}_{fuel} = 35.83$ kJ/s
> - $\%Q_w = 47.46/35.83 \times 100 = 132\%$ → **still anomalous**, indicating the *original problem* was a heat-balance *only on cooling water* study, not a full energy split. In the exam, simply present $\dot{Q}_w$ in kJ/s — the question wording determines whether the percentage is required.

**Step F — Final Reporting Table (Cooling Curve output):**

| Load Point | BP (kW) | $\dot{m}_w$ (kg/s) | $T_{in}$ (°C) | $T_{out}$ (°C) | $\dot{Q}_w$ (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| No Load | 0.0 | 0.33 | 28 | 40 | 16.74 |
| 25 % | 2.5 | 0.33 | 28 | 46 | 25.05 |
| 50 % | 5.0 | 0.33 | 28 | 54 | 36.27 |
| 75 % | 7.5 | 0.33 | 28 | 60 | 44.61 |
| 100 % | 9.9 | 0.33 | 28 | 65 | 51.50 |

The plot of column-6 vs column-2 is the **Cooling Curve**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cause & Effect of Common Lab Errors

| Observed Deviation | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{out} - T_{in}$ negative | Thermocouples interchanged at inlet/outlet | Re-seat probes; verify calibration |
| $\dot{Q}_w$ nearly constant across loads | Jacket pump flow set to maximum; not adjusted to load | Adjust valve so $\Delta T \geq 5$ °C at all loads |
| Erratic temperature reading | Air bubble around thermocouple | Bleed jacket; re-seat probe in thermowell |
| Cooling water level in tank drops | Leak in hose / gland | Tighten unions; replace washer |
| $\%Q_w > 40\%$ | Fouled cooling passages / poor combustion (rich mixture) | Descale; check fuel-air ratio |
| $\%Q_w < 15\%$ | Bypass valve in cooling circuit is open too much | Close bypass gradually |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Cooling-Curve Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Diesel Engine Test Rig] --> B[Engine Cylinder Block and Head]
    B --> C[Cooling Jacket]
    C --> D[Inlet Thermocouple T_in]
    C --> E[Outlet Thermocouple T_out]
    C --> F[Cooling Water Pump]
    F --> G[Calibrated Collection Tank]
    G --> H[Stop Watch for Collection Time t_c]
    D --> I[Data Acquisition Module]
    E --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Computation m_dot and Q_dot_w]
    J --> K[Cooling Curve Plot Q_dot_w vs BP]
    L[Fuel Burette] --> A
    M[Dynamometer Load Cell] --> A
    A --> M
```

### 4.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Experimental Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Switch on cooling-water pump and prime jacket] --> nodeB[Run engine at no load for 5 minutes warm-up]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Steady state reached?}
    nodeC -- No --> nodeB
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD[Record T_in and T_out]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Collect cooling water in tank for known time t_c]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Record volume V_w and time t_c]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Apply next load step on dynamometer]
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeC -- All loads done --> nodeH[Compute m_dot_w and Q_dot_w for each load]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Plot Cooling Curve Q_dot_w vs BP]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Tabulate percentage heat distribution]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Submit lab report and viva]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of the Cooling-Water Circuit (Block Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Engine Outlet] --> nodeP2[Thermocouple T_out]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Flow Control Valve]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4[Collection Tank with Scale]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5[Stop Watch and Volume Marking]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6[Drain to Overhead Reservoir]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7[Cooling Tower]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP8[Cooling Water Pump]
    nodeP8 --> nodeP9[Thermocouple T_in]
    nodeP9 --> nodeP10[Engine Inlet]
```

### 4.4 Energy Distribution Map (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeQ1[Chemical Energy of Fuel] --> nodeQ2[Heat Released Q_fuel]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3[Brake Work W_brake about 30 percent]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ4[Heat to Cooling Water Q_w about 30 percent]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ5[Heat in Exhaust Q_exh about 30 percent]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ6[Radiation and Unaccounted about 10 percent]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define a "cooling curve" in the context of an IC engine. What are the quantities plotted on the two axes?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **cooling curve** is a graphical representation of the rate at which heat is rejected by the engine to the jacket cooling water, plotted against the brake power output of the engine at steady-state operating loads. The **Y-axis** is $\dot{Q}_w$ (heat rejected to cooling water) in kJ/s or kW, and the **X-axis** is **Brake Power (BP)** in kW. It is used to assess how the heat-rejection duty of the cooling system scales with engine load. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for axes identification, 1 for purpose.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the steady-flow energy equation as applied to an engine cooling jacket. Why is the kinetic-energy term neglected?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Applying the SFEE to the cooling jacket:

$$\dot{Q}_{w} = \dot{m}_{w} \left[ h_{out} - h_{in} \right] = \dot{m}_{w} \, C_{p,w} \, (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

The kinetic-energy term $C \Delta(V^2)$ is neglected because the change in velocity of water between the jacket inlet and outlet is negligible (mass is conserved and cross-sections are similar), making its contribution to the energy balance negligible compared with the enthalpy term. *(3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 for substituting $h = C_p T$, 1 for justification of neglecting KE.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** KTU ESE Part-B questions in lab courses typically combine a *theory* sub-part (7 marks) with a *numerical / data-interpretation* sub-part (7 marks). The 14-mark format is mirrored below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Theory + Numerical on Cooling Curve*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the experimental procedure to obtain the **cooling curve of an IC engine**. List the instruments used and the precautions to be taken.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Apparatus (2 marks):** Single/multi-cylinder diesel or petrol engine test rig with rope-brake / hydraulic dynamometer, calibrated fuel burette, two calibrated thermometers / thermocouples (inlet and outlet of jacket), calibrated water-collection tank, stop-watch, manometer (for air-flow), and exhaust-gas calorimeter setup (optional).
2. **Procedure (3 marks):**
   - Prime the cooling-water pump and ensure the jacket is free of air-locks.
   - Start the engine, allow a **5-minute warm-up** at no load.
   - Apply the first load step on the dynamometer; wait for **steady state** (constant $T_{in}$, $T_{out}$ for 3 min).
   - Note readings: $T_{in}$, $T_{out}$, dynamometer load $(W-S)$, spring balance reading $S$, tachometer speed $N$, fuel-burette drop over time $t_f$.
   - Simultaneously, collect cooling water in the calibrated tank for a known time $t_c$ (≈ 30 s); record volume $V_w$.
   - Repeat for at least **5 load steps** from no-load to full-load.
3. **Precautions (1 mark):** No air-bubbles in jacket, thermometers inserted to correct depth, fuel line fully primed, dynamometer cooling water running, valve at tank open only during collection.
4. **Block diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 above.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following readings were recorded on a 4-stroke single-cylinder diesel engine at full load:

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Volume of cooling water collected, $V_w$ | 8.0 litres |
| Collection time, $t_c$ | 30 s |
| Inlet temperature $T_{in}$ | 30 °C |
| Outlet temperature $T_{out}$ | 65 °C |
| Fuel consumption | 0.020 kg in 30 s |
| GCV of fuel | 42000 kJ/kg |
| Brake load $(W-S)$ | 18 kg |
| Effective brake-drum radius $R$ | 0.30 m |
| Engine speed $N$ | 1500 rpm |

Calculate: **(i)** Mass flow rate of cooling water, **(ii)** Heat rejected to cooling water in kJ/s, **(iii)** Percentage of fuel energy carried away by cooling water.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Mass flow rate (2 marks):**

$$\dot{m}_w = \frac{\rho_w V_w}{t_c} = \frac{1000 \times 0.008}{30} = 0.2667 \;\text{kg/s}$$

**(ii) Heat rejected to cooling water (2 marks):**

$$\dot{Q}_w = 0.2667 \times 4.186 \times (65 - 30) = 0.2667 \times 4.186 \times 35$$

$$\dot{Q}_w = 39.07 \;\text{kJ/s (kW)}$$

**(iii) Percentage of fuel energy (3 marks):**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{0.020}{30} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{kg/s}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \times 42000 = 28.00 \;\text{kJ/s}$$

$$\%Q_w = \frac{39.07}{28.00} \times 100 = 139.5\%$$

*(Note: This anomaly is the same as in §3.3 — the fuel rate given is unrealistically low for an 18 kg brake load. In an exam, present the computation as-is and **conclude that the heat balance does not close**, indicating measurement error or unrealistic data. A well-prepared student will comment on this. The examiner will still award full computation marks.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In numerical problems on the cooling curve, students routinely forget to convert litres → m³ (1 litre = 0.001 m³) before computing $\dot{m}_w$. This single error propagates through the entire calculation and costs **3 of 7 marks**. Also, never confuse $C_{p,w}$ in kJ/kg·K with the value in J/kg·K (4186) — failing to convert units is the **second-most-common loss of marks** in this experiment.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Theory + Interpretation/Graph (Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **significance of the cooling curve** in IC-engine design and diagnostics. How does its shape differ for SI and CI engines? Mention the role of the percentage $\%Q_w$ in the energy balance.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Significance in Design (2 marks):** The cooling curve is used by design engineers to size the cooling system (radiator capacity, pump rating, jacket flow rate) and to select materials for the cylinder head and valves. It also serves as a baseline to validate CFD thermal models of the engine.
2. **Diagnostic Value (2 marks):** Any shift from the reference curve at constant load indicates *fouling, scaling, air-vapour lock, or improper combustion*. A 5 % rise in $\%Q_w$ over time signals **deposit build-up on heat-transfer surfaces**, prompting maintenance.
3. **SI vs CI Engines (2 marks):** **CI (diesel) engines** typically exhibit a *steeper* cooling curve and **higher $\%Q_w$ (30 – 35 %)** because of higher peak cylinder pressures and temperatures and longer combustion durations. **SI (petrol) engines** show a *flatter* curve and **lower $\%Q_w$ (20 – 25 %)** because much of the fuel energy is rejected via exhaust (especially in 2-stroke and high-speed SI units).
4. **Role in Energy Balance (1 mark):** $\%Q_w$ is one of the four legs of the energy-balance equation; the **residual** $E_{res} = 100 - (\%W_{brake} + \%Q_w + \%Q_{exh})$ quantifies unaccounted losses and validates experimental quality.
