**(b) [7 Marks]** A 5-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine develops **30 kW** of brake power at **3000 rpm**. The fuel consumption is **9 kg/h**, and the GCV is **44000 kJ/kg**. The cooling water enters the jacket at **35 °C** and leaves at **75 °C** with a flow rate of **0.55 kg/s**. **$C_{p,w}$ = 4.18 kJ/kg·K**. Calculate **(i)** Heat supplied per second, **(ii)** Heat rejected to cooling water per second, **(iii)** Percentage heat in cooling water, and **(iv)** Energy-balance residual assuming the brake efficiency is **28 %** and exhaust losses are **35 %**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Heat supplied per second (2 marks):**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{9}{3600} = 0.0025 \;\text{kg/s}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} = 0.0025 \times 44000 = 110 \;\text{kJ/s (kW)}$$

**(ii) Heat rejected to cooling water (2 marks):**

$$\dot{Q}_w = 0.55 \times 4.18 \times (75 - 35) = 0.55 \times 4.18 \times 40 = 91.96 \;\text{kW}$$

**(iii) Percentage of fuel energy to cooling water (1 mark):**

$$\%Q_w = \frac{91.96}{110} \times 100 = 83.6\%$$

*(This is again anomalously high — but the question gives all data, so the computation is the learning objective.)*

**(iv) Energy-balance residual (2 marks):**

$$E_{res} = 100 - (28 + 83.6 + 35) = 100 - 146.6 = -46.6\%$$

*Negative residual* confirms data inconsistency. In an exam, conclude: *"The given data does not satisfy the energy-balance equation; the cooling-water mass flow rate is too high. A physically realistic $\%Q_w$ for a petrol engine is 20 – 25 %."*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (KTU 2024):** When a problem is built around an inconsistent data set, the KTU examiner **wants** the student to *spot and comment* on the imbalance. A student who silently returns a value greater than 100 % without any remark loses **1 of the 2 reasoning marks**. Always include a 1-line conclusion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Cooling curve =** $\dot{Q}_w$ vs **BP** plot, the *heat-rejection* leg of the energy balance. *(high-yield, 1-mark definition)*
- **Core equation:** $\dot{Q}_w = \dot{m}_w \, C_{p,w} \, (T_{out} - T_{in})$ — memorize the order of multiplication. *(formula-derivation question, 2 marks)*
- **Mass flow rate** comes from **tank collection**, not from the pump nameplate. *(frequently asked as 3-mark short question)*
- **Standard values:** $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³; $C_{p,w} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K. Use **kJ** consistently with **kW** to avoid 1000× errors. *(unit-conversion trap)*
- **Energy-balance closure:** $100\% = \%W_b + \%Q_w + \%Q_{exh} + \%Q_{rad}$; residual should be **< ±10 %** for a valid test. *(3-mark question)*
- **SI vs CI:** CI has higher $\%Q_w$ (30 – 35 %); SI has lower (20 – 25 %) and higher exhaust losses. *(comparison question, 3 marks)*
- **Steady state** must be confirmed before every reading — *constant $T_{in}$, $T_{out}$ for 3 minutes* — this is the KTU examiner's **favourite viva question**. *(1 mark viva)*
- **Precautions** to list: bleed jacket, calibrate thermometers, run dynamometer cooling water, avoid air-locks, use calibrated burette. *(lab-record checklist, 2 marks)*
- **Diagnostic use:** upward drift of cooling curve at constant load ⇒ **scaling or fouling** of jacket. *(application question, 3 marks)*
- **Cooling curve shape:** **almost linear** with BP for a well-tuned engine; **non-linear** (concave-down) indicates **heat-flux saturation** at high loads. *(graphical question, 3 marks)*
- **Air-vapour lock** is the most common practical problem in KTU labs — if your temperature readings oscillate wildly, **bleed the jacket first** before any other action. *(practical tip)*
- **Always convert litres to m³** by multiplying by $10^{-3}$; **always convert minutes to seconds** by multiplying by 60 — these are the two most common arithmetic errors flagged in KTU valuation. *(exam pitfall)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valve timing diagram of IC engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL609 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-2 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Valve timing diagram of IC engines # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Valve Timing Diagram:** A graphical representation plotted on a **Crank Angle (θ)** versus **Piston Displacement** coordinate system that precisely depicts the instants (in degrees of crank rotation) at which the **Intake Valve** and **Exhaust Valve** of an Internal Combustion (IC) engine open and close, relative to the piston reaching the **Top Dead Center (TDC)** and **Bottom Dead Center (BDC)** positions. In the KTU 2024 scheme laboratory context, the valve timing diagram of a 4-stroke IC engine is one of the most fundamental **experimental demonstrations** in Thermal Engineering Lab-2. The diagram is constructed by physically rotating the crankshaft of a cut-section model engine through 720° (two complete revolutions for a 4-stroke engine) while observing the exact crank angle at which the poppet valves begin to lift and the moment they seat back onto their seats. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Breathing" Analogy:** Imagine you are running a marathon and need to breathe in (intake) and breathe out (exhaust) most efficiently. If you only started inhaling exactly when your mouth was at its widest opening, you would have already wasted precious time. So instead, you start inhaling a little **before** the mouth fully opens (advance) and keep exhaling a little **after** it has technically closed (retard). The IC engine does the exact same thing with its intake and exhaust valves to maximize the filling of fresh charge and the scavenging of burnt gases. In mechanical terms: - The **intake valve opens slightly before TDC** (during the exhaust stroke) so that by the time the piston starts moving down on the suction stroke, the valve is already partly open — this prevents the loss of fresh charge. - The **intake valve closes after BDC** so that the momentum of the incoming charge continues to pack the cylinder (ram effect). - The **exhaust valve opens before BDC** so that the high-pressure burnt gases start escaping while the piston is still moving up on the power stroke, reducing the work required to push them out. - The **exhaust valve closes after TDC** so that the residual exhaust gases are pushed out by the rising fresh charge. ## 1.3 The Four Cardinal Events of a 4-Stroke Cycle | Event | Stroke | Crank Angle Span | |---|---|---| | Suction / Induction | 1st stroke (Down) | 0° → 180° | | Compression | 2nd stroke (Up) | 180° → 360° | | Power / Expansion | 3rd stroke (Down) | 360° → 540° | | Exhaust | 4th stroke (Up) | 540° → 720° | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Convention in KTU Lab Manuals:** The **suction stroke is taken as the reference** and begins at **0° crank angle (TDC at start of suction stroke)**. The exhaust stroke ends at **720° crank angle**, returning to the original TDC. All valve opening and closing angles are referenced to this 0°–720° base. ## 1.4 Key Terminology — Must Know for KTU Viva - **TDC** = Top Dead Center (piston at its highest point, crank at 0° or 360°) - **BDC** = Bottom Dead Center (piston at its lowest point, crank at 180° or 540°) - **IVO** = Intake Valve Opens - **IVC** = Intake Valve Closes - **EVO** = Exhaust Valve Opens - **EVC** = Exhaust Valve Closes - **Valve Overlap** = Period during which BOTH intake and exhaust valves are open simultaneously (occurs near TDC at the end of exhaust / start of suction) - **Valve Lead** = Angle by which a valve opens BEFORE the dead center - **Valve Lag (Retard)** = Angle by which a valve closes AFTER the dead center - **Effective Stroke** = Actual crank angle during which the valve is open # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Advance and Retard are Needed (The "Why") ### 2.1.1 Why Intake Valve Opens *Before* TDC - The poppet valve has **finite mass and inertia**. - It cannot open instantaneously — it takes a finite crank angle to lift the valve from its seat. - If IVO were exactly at TDC, the cylinder would be sealed during the initial part of the suction stroke, leading to **loss of fresh charge** and poor volumetric efficiency. - **Solution:** Open the intake valve **10°–20° before TDC** (this is called **intake advance** or **IVO lead**). ### 2.1.2 Why Intake Valve Closes *After* BDC - The incoming air-fuel mixture has significant **kinetic energy (ram effect)**. - The inertia of the flowing gas column continues to push charge into the cylinder even as the piston has reached BDC and started moving up. - Closing the valve at BDC would **waste this momentum-driven filling**. - **Solution:** Close the intake valve **40°–70° after BDC** (this is **intake lag/retard**), improving **volumetric efficiency**. ### 2.1.3 Why Exhaust Valve Opens *Before* BDC - Near the end of the power stroke, the cylinder pressure has dropped significantly (often below 4–5 bar for petrol engines). - If we waited until BDC to open the exhaust valve, the piston would have to push out burnt gases during the entire exhaust stroke, wasting **pumping work**. - **Solution:** Open the exhaust valve **40°–60° before BDC**, allowing the high-pressure burnt gases to **blow down** spontaneously, reducing the work the piston must do during the exhaust stroke. ### 2.1.4 Why Exhaust Valve Closes *After* TDC - Due to valve inertia, it cannot close instantly. - Some exhaust gases continue to escape from the clearance volume even after the piston has started moving down. - **Solution:** Close the exhaust valve **10°–20° after TDC** to ensure complete scavenging of burnt gases. ## 2.2 Valve Overlap — The Critical Phenomenon > [!IMPORTANT] > **Valve Overlap (θ_OL)** is the angle in degrees of crank rotation during which BOTH intake and exhaust valves are open simultaneously. It occurs near the TDC at the end of the exhaust stroke and the beginning of the suction stroke. $$ \theta_{OL} = \theta_{EVC} + \theta_{IVO} $$ where both angles are measured from the **same TDC reference**. For a typical petrol engine, overlap is **15°–25°**. **Why overlap is needed (KTU Viva Favorite):** - The overlap period allows the **scavenging** of residual burnt gases by the incoming fresh charge. - For **diesel engines**, the overlap is **smaller** (10°–15°) because the diesel cycle does not rely on throttling for load control. - For **high-speed petrol engines**, the overlap is **larger** to take advantage of ram effect and improve volumetric efficiency at high RPM. ## 2.3 Typical Valve Timing Values (KTU Reference Data) | Parameter | Petrol (SI) Engine | Diesel (CI) Engine | |---|---|---| | IVO (before TDC) | 10° – 20° | 10° – 20° | | IVC (after BDC) | 40° – 70° | 25° – 40° | | EVO (before BDC) | 40° – 60° | 35° – 50° | | EVC (after TDC) | 10° – 20° | 5° – 15° | | Valve Overlap | 15° – 25° | 10° – 15° | | Effective Intake Duration | 220° – 270° | 215° – 240° | | Effective Exhaust Duration | 230° – 260° | 220° – 245° | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following formulas are the **most frequently tested equations** for valve timing problems in the KTU 2024 scheme. Memorize these for guaranteed marks. | # | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\theta_{OL} = \theta_{EVC} + \theta_{IVO}$ | Valve overlap angle (both measured from same TDC) | | 2 | $\theta_{intake\,open} = 180° + \theta_{IVC\,(lag)}$ | Total crank angle intake valve is open | | 3 | $\theta_{intake\,open} = 180° + \theta_{IVC} - \theta_{IVO\,(lead)}$ | Alternative form using both lead and lag | | 4 | $\theta_{exhaust\,open} = 180° + \theta_{EVC} - \theta_{EVO\,(lead)}$ | Total crank angle exhaust valve is open | | 5 | $t = \dfrac{\theta}{6N}$ seconds | Time for crank to rotate by θ degrees at N rpm | | 6 | $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{120}{N}$ seconds | Time for one complete 4-stroke cycle at N rpm | | 7 | $\eta_{vol} \approx f(\text{intake duration, overlap})$ | Volumetric efficiency (qualitative) | | 8 | $\% \text{ Overlap} = \dfrac{\theta_{OL}}{180°} \times 100$ | Overlap as a percentage (rarely asked) | ## 2.5 Two-Stroke Engine — Port Timing (Brief) In a two-stroke engine, there are **no poppet valves** in the conventional sense. Instead, the cylinder uses **ports** (openings in the cylinder wall) that are uncovered and covered by the piston itself. The port timing values are: - **Inlet Port (IP) Opens:** 5°–15° before BDC - **Inlet Port Closes:** 5°–15° after TDC (transfer complete) - **Exhaust Port (EP) Opens:** 30°–60° before BDC - **Exhaust Port Closes:** 5°–15° after TDC > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse two-stroke and four-stroke port/valve timings. The KTU examiner will deduct marks if you state "intake valve timing" for a two-stroke engine — it should be **port timing**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Crank Angle to Time Conversion This is the **most common calculation** in valve timing problems at KTU. **Given:** Engine speed = $N$ rpm (revolutions per minute) **To find:** Time required for the crank to rotate by $\theta$ degrees. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1:** The engine crankshaft makes $N$ revolutions in $60$ seconds. $$ \text{Time for 1 full revolution} = \frac{60}{N} \text{ seconds} $$ **Step 2:** One full revolution = 360° of crank rotation. $$ \text{Time for 360° rotation} = \frac{60}{N} \text{ seconds} $$ **Step 3:** Time per degree of crank rotation: $$ \text{Time per degree} = \frac{60}{N \times 360} = \frac{1}{6N} \text{ seconds/degree} $$ **Step 4:** Time for $\theta$ degrees of crank rotation: $$ t = \theta \times \frac{1}{6N} = \frac{\theta}{6N} \text{ seconds} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion sanity check:** If $N = 600$ rpm, then time for $1° = \frac{1}{3600}$ s $\approx 0.278$ ms. This is the correct order of magnitude for engine events. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Valve Overlap from Given Timing Data **Given Standard Petrol Engine Timing (KTU Reference):** - IVO = 15° before TDC (i.e., 720° − 15° = 705° on the standard 0°–720° base, or equivalently 15° before the TDC that ends the exhaust stroke) - EVC = 15° after TDC ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1:** Identify the reference TDC. Both IVO and EVC are referenced to the **same TDC** (the TDC at the end of exhaust / start of suction stroke). **Step 2:** Express both angles as positive numbers measured from this TDC: - EVC is $15°$ **after** TDC - IVO is $15°$ **before** TDC **Step 3:** Since both are measured from the same TDC but on opposite sides, the overlap is the **sum**: $$ \theta_{OL} = \theta_{EVC} + \theta_{IVO} = 15° + 15° = 30° $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Intake Valve Duration (Sum of Lead + Lag + 180°) **Given:** IVO = 12° before TDC, IVC = 48° after BDC. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1:** The base opening of the intake valve is from TDC (start of suction) to BDC (end of suction) = 180°. **Step 2:** Add the **lead angle** (valve opens 12° early): $$ \text{Start of intake opening} = 180° - 12° = 168° \text{ before BDC (or 12° before TDC)} $$ **Step 3:** Add the **lag angle** (valve closes 48° late): $$ \text{End of intake opening} = 180° + 48° = 228° \text{ after TDC} $$ **Step 4:** Total effective intake duration: $$ \theta_{intake} = 180° + \theta_{lag} + \theta_{lead} = 180° + 48° + 12° = 240° $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Standard Problem Templates | Problem Type | Given Data | Required Output | Key Formula | |---|---|---|---| | Type 1: Overlap Calculation | IVO & EVC angles | Overlap angle | $\theta_{OL} = \theta_{EVC} + \theta_{IVO}$ | | Type 2: Effective Duration | IVO lead & IVC lag | Total intake open angle | $\theta = 180° + \text{lead} + \text{lag}$ | | Type 3: Time Conversion | Crank angle θ, Speed N | Time in seconds | $t = \theta / (6N)$ | | Type 4: Crank Angle from Time | Time t, Speed N | Crank angle in degrees | $\theta = 6Nt$ | | Type 5: Cycle Time | Speed N | Time for 720° | $T = 120/N$ seconds | ## 3.5 Solved Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A 4-stroke petrol engine runs at **3000 rpm**. The valve timing data is as follows: > - Intake Valve Opens (IVO): 12° before TDC > - Intake Valve Closes (IVC): 45° after BDC > - Exhaust Valve Opens (EVO): 50° before BDC > - Exhaust Valve Closes (EVC): 10° after TDC > > **Find:** > (a) Valve overlap angle > (b) Effective intake valve duration > (c) Time during which the intake valve remains open > (d) Total time for one complete cycle ### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Valve Overlap** The overlap is the sum of IVO and EVC angles (both measured from the same TDC at the end of exhaust): $$ \theta_{OL} = \theta_{EVC} + \theta_{IVO} = 10° + 12° = \boxed{22°} $$ **Part (b) — Effective Intake Valve Duration** Total crank angle during which the intake valve is open = 180° (base suction stroke) + lead + lag: $$ \theta_{intake} = 180° + \theta_{IVC\,(lag)} + \theta_{IVO\,(lead)} = 180° + 45° + 12° = \boxed{237°} $$ **Part (c) — Time Intake Valve is Open** Using $t = \dfrac{\theta}{6N}$: $$ t_{intake} = \frac{237°}{6 \times 3000} = \frac{237}{18000} = 0.01317 \text{ s} \approx \boxed{13.17 \text{ ms}} $$ **Part (d) — Total Time for One Complete 4-Stroke Cycle** A complete 4-stroke cycle = 720° of crank rotation: $$ T_{cycle} = \frac{120}{N} = \frac{120}{3000} = 0.04 \text{ s} = \boxed{40 \text{ ms}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification Check:** The intake valve stays open for 13.17 ms out of 40 ms total cycle time = 32.9% of the cycle. This is reasonable for a typical petrol engine (intake is open roughly 30%–40% of the cycle). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Valve Timing Diagram > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **logical structure** of a complete 4-stroke valve timing diagram, mapping the relationships between TDC, BDC, valve events, and the four strokes. This is the most commonly drawn schematic in KTU lab records. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start 720 deg Cycle] --> B[TDC 0 deg
Suction stroke begins] B --> C[IVO Intake Valve Opens
12 deg BEFORE TDC
End of exhaust overlap starts] C --> D[Piston moves DOWN
Suction stroke 0 to 180 deg] D --> E[BDC 180 deg
Suction stroke ends] E --> F[IVC Intake Valve Closes
45 deg AFTER BDC
Intake lag period begins] F --> G[Piston moves UP
Compression stroke 180 to 360 deg] G --> H[TDC 360 deg
Compression ends / Power begins] H --> I[Piston moves DOWN
Power stroke 360 to 540 deg] I --> J[BDC 540 deg
Power stroke ends] J --> K[EVO Exhaust Valve Opens
50 deg BEFORE BDC
Blowdown begins] K --> L[Piston moves UP
Exhaust stroke 540 to 720 deg] L --> M[TDC 720 deg
Cycle complete] M --> N[EVC Exhaust Valve Closes
10 deg AFTER TDC
Overlap ends] N --> O[VALVE OVERLAP REGION
Both valves open for 22 deg] O --> P[End of complete cycle
Return to 0 deg reference] style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style H fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style M fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style P fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff style O fill:#991b1b,color:#ffffff style C fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff style F fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff style K fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff style N fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Valve Timing Diagram (Conceptual Skeleton Plot) The following ASCII representation shows the **standard layout** of a valve timing diagram that students must produce during the lab exam: ``` Piston Position TDC |‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾ | EVC IVO EVC IVO | ←10°→←12°→ ←10°→←12°→ | ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ | │EV│ │IV│ │EV│ │IV│ ( | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ←────│ │ │ │ │←──EXHAUST──→ │ │ │ │←SUCTION→ Pis- │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ton) | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ BDC | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ TDC | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ | │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ | └──┘ └──┘ └──┘ └──┘ | ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑ | 720° 540° 360° 180° 0° Reference | └────────────────────────────────────────→ Crank Angle Legend: IV = Intake Valve Open Period EV = Exhaust Valve Open Period Overlap = EVC + IVO (near TDC at 0° / 720°) ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — How the Lab Experiment is Conducted ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA[Step 1: Identify engine
on test bed] --> stepB[Step 2: Mark TDC
using reference pointer] stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Set dial gauge
to 0 deg at TDC] stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Rotate crank
in normal direction] stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Observe IVO
Note crank angle] stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Continue rotation
Note IVC angle] stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Note EVO
and EVC angles] stepG --> stepH[Step 8: Plot diagram
on graph sheet] stepH --> stepI[Step 9: Calculate
overlap and duration] stepI --> stepJ[Step 10: Tabulate results
and conclude] style stepA fill:#0369a1,color:#ffffff style stepE fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff stepF --> stepG style stepJ fill:#047857,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **In the actual KTU lab exam**, the engine model is hand-cranked, and students use a **protractor-style degree disc** mounted on the crankshaft pulley to read the angles. The values are then plotted on graph paper to construct the diagram. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Valve Timing Diagram. Why is it necessary for an IC engine? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Exam 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A valve timing diagram is a **graphical representation** of the opening and closing instants of the intake and exhaust valves of an IC engine, plotted against the crank angle (on the horizontal axis) and piston displacement (on the vertical axis), over a complete cycle (720° for 4-stroke). It is necessary because the valves cannot be opened and closed instantaneously due to their mechanical inertia, and the **ram effect** of incoming charge and **blowdown** of exhaust gases require the valves to open slightly before and close slightly after the theoretical dead centers to maximize **volumetric efficiency** and engine performance. **(3 marks — Definition: 1 mark, Necessity: 2 marks)** ### Question 2: What is Valve Overlap? Why is it provided in a 4-stroke petrol engine? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series Exam 1]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Valve overlap is the **angle in degrees of crank rotation** during which both the intake and exhaust valves remain open simultaneously, occurring near the TDC at the end of the exhaust stroke and the beginning of the suction stroke. Mathematically, it is the sum of the **exhaust valve closing angle (after TDC)** and the **intake valve opening angle (before TDC)**, i.e., $\theta_{OL} = \theta_{EVC} + \theta_{IVO}$. It is provided in petrol engines to enable **scavenging of residual burnt gases** by the incoming fresh charge, improving volumetric efficiency at high engine speeds. **(3 marks — Definition: 1.5 marks, Reason: 1.5 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, End Semester Evaluation - Module 2]` **Question:** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **valve timing diagram of a 4-stroke petrol engine**. Clearly indicate the following in your diagram: **(a)** Intake valve opening and closing instants, **(b)** Exhaust valve opening and closing instants, **(c)** Valve overlap, and **(d)** Discuss with justification why the intake valve closes after BDC and the exhaust valve opens before BDC. **(7 + 7 = 14 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Diagram Construction (7 marks):** The valve timing diagram is constructed with **crank angle on the horizontal axis (0° to 720°)** and **piston position on the vertical axis (TDC at top, BDC at bottom)**. The four strokes are represented as sinusoidal curves of the piston motion. The standard valve timing values for a 4-stroke petrol engine are: | Event | Angle | Reference | |---|---|---| | IVO | 10°–20° | before TDC | | IVC | 40°–70° | after BDC | | EVO | 40°–60° | before BDC | | EVC | 10°–20° | after TDC | The diagram is drawn showing two TDCs and two BDCs. The **intake valve opens** 10°–20° before the TDC at the end of exhaust, remains open through the entire suction stroke, and **closes** 40°–70° after the BDC of the suction stroke. The **exhaust valve opens** 40°–60° before the BDC of the power stroke and **closes** 10°–20° after the TDC at the start of suction. The two regions of valve activity overlap near the TDC at the start/end of the cycle, forming the **valve overlap zone** of typically 15°–25°. **(b) Justifications (7 marks):** **Why intake valve closes after BDC (3.5 marks):** When the piston reaches BDC during the suction stroke, the **incoming air-fuel mixture has considerable kinetic energy and momentum** (ram effect). If the valve were to close exactly at BDC, this momentum would be **wasted**. Therefore, the intake valve is allowed to close **40°–70° after BDC**, during which the piston is moving upward on the compression stroke. This uses the inertia of the gas column to **pack more charge** into the cylinder, improving the **volumetric efficiency** and increasing the power output of the engine. **Why exhaust valve opens before BDC (3.5 marks):** Near the end of the power stroke, the cylinder pressure has dropped to about **4–5 bar** (in petrol engines). The remaining useful work in the expansion stroke is small. If the exhaust valve were to open exactly at BDC, the piston would have to **push out** all the burnt gases during the entire exhaust stroke, wasting significant **pumping work**. By opening the exhaust valve **40°–60° before BDC**, the high-pressure burnt gases undergo **spontaneous blowdown** to the atmosphere, releasing energy as kinetic energy of the exhaust jet. This **reduces the work** required from the piston during the exhaust stroke and improves the **thermal efficiency** of the engine. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, End Semester Evaluation - Module 2]` **Question:** A 4-stroke petrol engine runs at **2400 rpm**. The valve timing data is given below: - IVO = 18° before TDC - IVC = 50° after BDC - EVO = 55° before BDC - EVC = 12° after TDC Calculate: **(a)** The valve overlap angle and the effective duration (in crank degrees) for which each valve remains open. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The time (in milliseconds) for which each valve is open and the total time for one complete cycle. Verify whether the total time is consistent. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Part (a) — Overlap and Effective Duration (7 marks):** **Valve Overlap Calculation (2 marks):** The valve overlap is the sum of IVO and EVC angles (both referenced to the same TDC at the end of exhaust): $$ \theta_{OL} = \theta_{EVC} + \theta_{IVO} = 12° + 18° = \boxed{30°} $$ **Effective Intake Valve Duration (2.5 marks):** The intake valve remains open from 18° before TDC (start of suction) to 50° after BDC (end of suction): $$ \theta_{intake} = 180° + \theta_{lag} + \theta_{lead} = 180° + 50° + 18° = \boxed{248°} $$ **Effective Exhaust Valve Duration (2.5 marks):** The exhaust valve remains open from 55° before BDC (end of power) to 12° after TDC (end of exhaust): $$ \theta_{exhaust} = 180° + \theta_{EVC\,(lag)} + \theta_{EVO\,(lead)} = 180° + 12° + 55° = \boxed{247°} $$ **Part (b) — Time Calculations (7 marks):** **Time for which intake valve is open (2.5 marks):** $$ t_{intake} = \frac{\theta_{intake}}{6N} = \frac{248°}{6 \times 2400} = \frac{248}{14400} = 0.01722 \text{ s} = \boxed{17.22 \text{ ms}} $$ **Time for which exhaust valve is open (2.5 marks):** $$ t_{exhaust} = \frac{248°}{6 \times 2400} = \boxed{17.22 \text{ ms}} $$ **Total time for one complete cycle (2 marks):** $$ T_{cycle} = \frac{120}{N} = \frac{120}{2400} = 0.05 \text{ s} = \boxed{50 \text{ ms}} $$ **Consistency Verification:** The intake valve is open for 17.22 ms out of 50 ms total cycle time = **34.4% of the cycle**. This is within the expected range of 30%–40% for a typical petrol engine. **Therefore, the result is consistent.** ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Students often add IVC lag angle incorrectly, forgetting to add the **180° base stroke**. The correct formula is always: $180° + \text{lead} + \text{lag}$. Missing this loses **2 marks** immediately. > 2. **Reference TDC Confusion:** Many students calculate overlap using IVC and EVO instead of IVO and EVC. Always remember: overlap uses **EVC (after TDC) + IVO (before TDC)**, both referenced to the **same TDC**. > 3. **Unit Mismatch:** Crank angle must be in **degrees**, speed in **rpm**, and time will be in **seconds**. Forgetting the 6N factor in $t = \theta/(6N)$ is a common slip. > 4. **No Diagram in Theory Questions:** For 7-mark questions on valve timing, a **neat labeled sketch is mandatory**. The KTU examiner typically allocates **3–4 marks** for the diagram alone. Writing only text will cap your marks at 4/7. > 5. **Petrol vs Diesel Confusion:** Students state the same timing values for diesel and petrol engines. Remember: **diesel has shorter overlap and lower IVC lag** because of different combustion characteristics. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Notes for KTU 2024:** - **Definition:** Valve timing diagram is a plot of **piston position vs. crank angle** showing the instants of valve opening and closing. - **4-Stroke Reference:** A complete cycle is **720° (2 crank revolutions)**. - **2-Stroke Reference:** A complete cycle is **360° (1 crank revolution)** — but uses **ports**, not valves. - **IVO** = 10°–20° before TDC (intake lead / advance). - **IVC** = 40°–70° after BDC for petrol; 25°–40° for diesel (intake lag / retard). - **EVO** = 40°–60° before BDC (exhaust lead / advance). - **EVC** = 10°–20° after TDC (exhaust lag / retard). - **Valve Overlap Formula:** $\theta_{OL} = \theta_{EVC} + \theta_{IVO}$ (both from same TDC). - **Effective Duration Formula:** $\theta = 180° + \text{lead} + \text{lag}$. - **Time Conversion:** $t = \theta / (6N)$ seconds; $T_{cycle} = 120/N$ seconds for 4-stroke. - **Why IVO is early:** To overcome valve inertia and avoid loss of fresh charge. - **Why IVC is late:** To utilize ram effect of incoming charge for better volumetric efficiency. - **Why EVO is early:** To allow spontaneous blowdown of burnt gases and reduce pumping work. - **Why EVC is late:** To scavenge residual exhaust gases completely. - **Overlap Purpose:** Scavenging of burnt gases using incoming fresh charge. - **Diesel vs Petrol:** Diesel has **smaller overlap** and **shorter intake duration** due to no throttling for load control. - **Viva Favorites:** Definitions of TDC, BDC, overlap, lead, lag, port timing (for 2-stroke), and numerical on time conversion.

topics.title: Economic speed test on IC engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL609 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-2
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Economic speed test on IC engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Economic Speed Test** on an Internal Combustion (IC) engine is a standardized performance evaluation procedure conducted to determine the **rotational speed at which the engine consumes the minimum amount of fuel per unit of brake power output**. The performance metric used for this evaluation is the **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**.

Mathematically, the economic speed is defined as the engine speed (in RPM) corresponding to the **minimum point on the BSFC versus engine speed characteristic curve**, evaluated under a constant load condition (typically full load or a specified fractional load).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCMEL609):**
> The Economic Speed Test is listed as a core experiment under Module 2. The student is expected to (a) conduct the test by varying engine speed at a fixed load, (b) tabulate fuel consumption, (c) compute BSFC at each speed, and (d) plot the BSFC versus Speed curve to identify the economic speed of operation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a motorcycle from your home (Calicut) to your college (Thrissur). You have three gear options — 2nd gear, 3rd gear, and 4th gear — at the same road gradient.

- In **2nd gear**, the engine revs very high, you cover the distance quickly, but the engine "drinks" petrol very fast (high fuel consumption).
- In **4th gear**, the engine revs low, you save fuel, but the engine struggles on the slope and may even stall (low power output).
- In **3rd gear** (a sweet spot), the engine revs moderately, delivers reasonable power, AND uses the least petrol per kilometer travelled.

That "**3rd gear speed**" is exactly analogous to the **economic speed** of the IC engine — the speed where fuel consumption per unit of useful work delivered is the absolute minimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used:**
> - **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC):** typically **0.20 to 0.40 kg/kWh** for modern diesel engines; **0.30 to 0.50 kg/kWh** for petrol engines.
> - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of diesel:** approximately **42,000 kJ/kg**.
> - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of petrol:** approximately **44,000 kJ/kg**.
> - **Brake Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{bth}$):** typically **25% – 38%** for diesel; **20% – 30%** for petrol.

## 1.3 Practical Relevance

| Application Domain | Relevance of Economic Speed |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (Cars, Trucks, Buses)** | Highway cruising RPM is tuned close to BSFC minimum to maximize mileage |
| **Marine Propulsion** | Ship engine order telegraph RPM set near economic speed for long voyages |
| **Gensets (Diesel Generators)** | Rated RPM is chosen near minimum BSFC to reduce operational fuel cost |
| **Tractors & Off-road Vehicles** | PTO speed matched to engine economic speed for sustained field operations |
| **Aircraft Piston Engines** | Cruise power setting aligned to minimum BSFC for range optimization |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Performance Parameter

In any fuel-consuming engine, the **figure of merit** is not just "how much fuel is burnt" but "**how much fuel is burnt per unit of useful mechanical work delivered**". This is precisely what BSFC quantifies.

### Step 2: Define BSFC Mathematically

The Brake Specific Fuel Consumption is defined as the **mass flow rate of fuel supplied** divided by the **brake power (BP)** produced by the engine.

### Step 3: Conduct the Test

The engine is coupled to a **dynamometer** (eddy current, hydraulic, or rope brake). The load on the dynamometer is held **constant** at a pre-decided value (commonly full load or 75% of full load). The engine throttle/fuel pump setting is kept fixed, and only the **engine speed is varied** in discrete steps by adjusting the dynamometer loading characteristic (in eddy current dynamometers) or by adjusting the fuel injection quantity in coordination with load.

### Step 4: Measure Fuel Consumption

At each speed setting, the **fuel consumption rate ($\dot{m}_f$)** is measured using either:
- A **gravimetric method** (measuring mass of fuel consumed per unit time using a precision weighing balance and stopwatch), or
- A **volumetric method** (measuring volume of fuel consumed using a burette/burette and converting to mass using density).

### Step 5: Record Speed and Torque

The engine speed **N** is measured using a **tachometer** (digital or analog), and the brake load (spring balance reading) on the dynamometer is noted.

### Step 6: Calculate Brake Power

BP is calculated from the net brake load (W − S, where W is the gross load and S is the spring balance tare reading) and the engine speed.

### Step 7: Calculate BSFC

For each speed point, BSFC is calculated using the standard formula.

### Step 8: Plot the Curve

A graph of **BSFC (y-axis) versus Engine Speed N (x-axis)** is plotted. The **minimum point** on the curve corresponds to the **economic speed**.

### Step 9: Why Does BSFC Have a Minimum?

At very low speeds:
- Mechanical friction is proportionally high relative to useful power output.
- Combustion is poor due to low turbulence and inadequate mixing time.
- Heat losses to the cylinder walls are proportionally large.

At very high speeds:
- Combustion time becomes insufficient, leading to incomplete combustion.
- Pumping losses increase substantially.
- Gas exchange (valve overlap) losses become significant.

In between, there exists an **optimum speed** where the combined effects produce the best fuel economy.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Brake Power (BP) | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) R}{60000}$ | kW | R = effective radius of brake drum in metres; (W − S) in kgf; N in RPM |
| 2 | Brake Power (alternate) | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60000}$ | kW | T = torque in N·m; N in RPM |
| 3 | Torque (T) | $T = (W - S) \times g \times R$ | N·m | (W − S) in kgf, g = 9.81 m/s², R in metres |
| 4 | Fuel mass flow rate (gravimetric) | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{t}$ | kg/s | $m_{fuel}$ in kg, t in seconds |
| 5 | Fuel volume flow rate | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{V_{fuel} \times \rho_{fuel}}{t}$ | kg/s | V in m³, $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 6 | BSFC | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | Hourly basis; multiplying $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/s) by 3600 s/h gives kg/h |
| 7 | BSFC (alternative) | $BSFC = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{bth} \times LHV}$ | kg/kWh | Where $\eta_{bth}$ is brake thermal efficiency |
| 8 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times LHV}$ | dimensionless | LHV in kJ/kg |
| 9 | Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) | $SFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600 \times 1000}{BP}$ | g/kWh | Same as BSFC multiplied by 1000 |
| 10 | Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) | $AFR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\dot{m}_f}$ | dimensionless | Required to compute calorific value |
| 11 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{IP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times LHV}$ | dimensionless | Where IP = BP + FP (friction power) |
| 12 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of BP to IP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Reminders (commonly tested in KTU viva):**
> - 1 kW = 1.341 HP (mechanical horsepower)
> - 1 kgf = 9.81 N
> - 1 HP = 745.7 W
> - 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3600 kJ

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The economic speed test result is the **single most important data point used by automotive OEMs** when calibrating the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** of fuel-injected engines. Modern ECUs use a **3D BSFC map** (BSFC as a function of both speed AND load) to determine fueling quantity and injection timing for the best fuel economy in **each driving mode** (idle, part-throttle, wide-open throttle, cruise). Hybrid vehicles go a step further — the internal combustion engine is deliberately **clutched in only when operating near its economic speed**, while the electric motor handles low-speed and high-torque demands.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From First Principles to the BSFC Equation

### 3.1.1 Starting Definition

By definition, BSFC is the **mass of fuel consumed per unit time** divided by the **brake power delivered in that same unit of time**.

$$BSFC = \frac{\text{Mass of fuel consumed per second}}{\text{Brake power output in kW}}$$

Let:
- $\dot{m}_f$ = mass flow rate of fuel in **kg/s**
- $BP$ = brake power in **kW**

Then the fuel consumed in **one hour** is:

$$\text{Fuel per hour} = \dot{m}_f \times 3600 \quad [\text{kg/h}]$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Conversion

Since BSFC is conventionally expressed in **kg/kWh** (kilograms of fuel per kilowatt-hour of brake work), we divide the hourly fuel consumption by the brake power:

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}}\right]$$

This is the **fundamental BSFC equation**. Every subsequent variant is derived from this single relationship.

### 3.1.3 Expressing BP in Terms of Speed, Load, and Radius

The brake power developed by the engine is transmitted to the dynamometer, where it is absorbed as a frictional or electromagnetic load. The torque generated by the engine is:

$$T = (W - S) \times g \times R \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

Where:
- $W$ = gross spring balance reading in kgf
- $S$ = spring balance tare (dead weight) reading in kgf
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $R$ = effective radius of the brake drum in metres

The brake power (work done per second) is:

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

Dividing by 1000 to convert to kW:

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60000} \quad [\text{kW}]$$

Substituting $T = (W - S) g R$:

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N (W - S) g R}{60000} \quad [\text{kW}]$$

### 3.1.4 Combining the Equations

Substituting the BP expression into the BSFC equation:

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600 \times 60000}{2 \pi N (W - S) g R} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}}\right]$$

This is the **most general form** used in KTU lab calculations. In practice, students compute BP first using the simpler form, then compute BSFC separately for tabulation clarity.

### 3.1.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard Format)

**Given data from a typical economic speed test on a single-cylinder diesel engine:**

| Trial No. | Engine Speed N (RPM) | Net Load (W − S) in kgf | Time for 10 mL fuel (s) | Fuel density (kg/m³) | Drum radius R (m) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1200 | 5.0 | 28 | 850 | 0.15 |
| 2 | 1500 | 5.0 | 24 | 850 | 0.15 |
| 3 | 1800 | 5.0 | 21 | 850 | 0.15 |
| 4 | 2100 | 5.0 | 19 | 850 | 0.15 |
| 5 | 2400 | 5.0 | 17 | 850 | 0.15 |

**Step 1 — Calculate fuel mass flow rate for Trial 1:**

Volume of fuel consumed = 10 mL = $10 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ = $1 \times 10^{-5}$ m³

Mass of fuel consumed = Volume × Density:

$$m_f = V \times \rho = 1 \times 10^{-5} \times 850 = 8.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}$$

Mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{m_f}{t} = \frac{8.5 \times 10^{-3}}{28} = 3.036 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Torque for Trial 1:**

$$T = (W - S) \times g \times R = 5.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.15 = 7.358 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Brake Power for Trial 1:**

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60000} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1200 \times 7.358}{60000}$$

$$BP = \frac{55{,}471.04}{60000} = 0.9245 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate BSFC for Trial 1:**

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP} = \frac{3.036 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600}{0.9245}$$

$$BSFC = \frac{1.0929}{0.9245} = 1.182 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for all trials and tabulate the consolidated results:**

| Trial | N (RPM) | $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/s) | BP (kW) | BSFC (kg/kWh) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1200 | 3.036 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.9245 | **1.182** |
| 2 | 1500 | 3.542 × 10⁻⁴ | 1.156 | **1.103** |
| 3 | 1800 | 4.048 × 10⁻⁴ | 1.387 | **1.050** |
| 4 | 2100 | 4.474 × 10⁻⁴ | 1.618 | **0.995** |
| 5 | 2400 | 5.000 × 10⁻⁴ | 1.849 | **0.974** |

**Step 6 — Plot BSFC vs N curve:**

On the x-axis, plot engine speed N (RPM) from 1200 to 2400. On the y-axis, plot BSFC (kg/kWh) from 0.97 to 1.20. The curve descends continuously in this range — meaning in this particular example, **the economic speed would be at the upper end of the tested range (2400 RPM)**. Had we extended trials to 2700 and 3000 RPM, we would expect to see BSFC begin to rise again, and the minimum would then be the true economic speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a complete economic speed test, the engine speed range MUST be broad enough to clearly capture the U-shape of the BSFC curve.** Testing at only 3 to 4 speeds without observing the rising branch on the right side is considered an **incomplete experiment** and will attract examiner deductions in KTU practical records.

### 3.1.6 Deriving the Economic Speed from the Curve

Mathematically, the economic speed $N_{econ}$ satisfies:

$$\left.\frac{d(BSFC)}{dN}\right|_{N = N_{econ}} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left.\frac{d^2(BSFC)}{dN^2}\right|_{N = N_{econ}} > 0$$

In practice, $N_{econ}$ is read off graphically from the lowest point of the plotted BSFC-N curve.

### 3.1.7 Conversion to Brake Thermal Efficiency at Economic Speed

At the economic speed, the brake thermal efficiency is correspondingly **maximum**:

$$\eta_{bth, \max} = \frac{3600}{BSFC_{\min} \times LHV}$$

For diesel with $LHV = 42{,}000$ kJ/kg and $BSFC_{\min} = 0.30$ kg/kWh:

$$\eta_{bth,\max} = \frac{3600}{0.30 \times 42000} = \frac{3600}{12600} = 0.2857 = 28.57\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow of the economic speed test setup**, including the engine, dynamometer, fuel measurement system, and instrumentation chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IC Engine Under Test] --> B[Crankshaft Coupling]
    B --> C[Dynamometer Rotor]
    C --> D[Eddy Current Field Coil]
    D --> E[Cooling Water Supply]
    D --> F[Swing Arm Assembly]
    F --> G[Spring Balance W1]
    F --> H[Tare Weight W2]
    F --> I[Brake Drum Pulley]
    F --> J[Effective Radius R]
    A --> K[Fuel Tank Reservoir]
    K --> L[Burette or Weighing Scale]
    L --> M[Stopwatch or Flow Timer]
    A --> N[Tachometer Sensor]
    A --> O[Thermocouple for Exhaust]
    A --> P[Manometer for Air Intake]
    G --> Q[Data Logging Sheet]
    H --> Q
    J --> Q
    L --> Q
    M --> Q
    N --> Q
    Q --> R[Calculation of BP and BSFC]
    R --> S[Plot of BSFC versus N Curve]
    S --> T[Identification of Economic Speed]
    T --> U[Report Submission to KTU Examiner]
```

## 4.2 Test Procedure Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Engine Warmup at Rated RPM for 10 minutes] --> B[Set Dynamometer to Fixed Load]
    B --> C[Stabilize Engine at Trial 1 Speed]
    C --> D[Record N in RPM]
    D --> E[Record Spring Balance Readings W and S]
    E --> F[Measure Fuel Consumption for Fixed Volume]
    F --> G[Record Time t in seconds]
    G --> H[Repeat for 5 to 7 Different Speeds]
    H --> I[Calculate BP for Each Speed]
    I --> J[Calculate BSFC for Each Speed]
    J --> K[Plot BSFC versus N Graph]
    K --> L[Identify Minimum BSFC Point]
    L --> M[Read Corresponding Speed as Economic Speed]
    M --> N[End: Tabulate Results and Write Conclusion]
```

## 4.3 Expected BSFC vs Engine Speed Curve — Shape Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Speed Region] -->|Friction High, Combustion Poor| B[BSFC High]
    B -->|Speed Increases, Mixing Improves| C[BSFC Decreases]
    C -->|Reaches Optimum Speed| D[Economic Speed N econ]
    D -->|BSFC at Minimum| E[Minimum BSFC Point]
    E -->|Further Speed Increase| F[Incomplete Combustion, Pumping Losses Rise]
    F -->|BSFC Rises Again| G[High Speed Region]
```

## 4.4 Instrumentation & Safety Summary Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Personal Protective Equipment: Safety Goggles and Gloves] --> B[Pre-test Inspection]
    B --> C[Check Oil Level, Coolant Level, Fuel Level]
    C --> D[Verify Tachometer Calibration]
    D --> E[Verify Spring Balance Zero Reading]
    E --> F[Start Engine and Warmup]
    F --> G[Commence Trials with Constant Vigilance on Exhaust Temperature]
    G --> H[Emergency Shutdown Procedure in Case of Overheating or Backfire]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Viva Point:** Students are expected to identify and label the **dynamometer type** used in their specific lab (eddy current dynamometer, hydraulic dynamometer, or rope brake dynamometer). The economic speed test is most commonly performed using the **eddy current dynamometer** because it offers easy, stepless load control via field current variation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) and explain its significance in engine performance evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
BSFC is defined as the **mass flow rate of fuel consumed per unit of brake power output** by the engine, expressed in **kg/kWh**. Mathematically:

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}}\right]$$

**Significance (2 Marks):**
- It directly indicates the **fuel economy** of the engine — lower BSFC means better fuel economy.
- It is used to determine the **economic speed** of the engine, which is the speed at which BSFC is minimum.
- It is the primary metric used by automotive OEMs to calibrate fuel injection systems for optimal mileage.
- BSFC values typically range from **0.20 to 0.40 kg/kWh** for diesel engines and **0.30 to 0.50 kg/kWh** for petrol engines.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**What is meant by "economic speed" of an IC engine? How is it determined experimentally?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The economic speed of an IC engine is defined as the **rotational speed at which the brake specific fuel consumption is minimum** for a given constant load condition.

**Experimental Determination (2 Marks):**
1. Couple the engine to a **dynamometer** and apply a **fixed load** (full load or specified fraction).
2. Vary the engine speed in **5 to 7 discrete steps** across the operating range.
3. At each speed, measure the **fuel consumption rate** and the **net brake load**.
4. Compute BSFC at each speed using $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP}$.
5. Plot **BSFC vs Engine Speed** curve.
6. The speed corresponding to the **minimum point** of the curve is the economic speed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice between two full questions**. Students must answer **either Question A or Question B in full**. Each question typically has two sub-parts **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Set A]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**With the help of a neat sketch, describe the experimental setup and procedure to conduct an economic speed test on a multi-cylinder diesel engine. List all the instruments required.**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**The following observations were recorded during an economic speed test on a 4-stroke, 4-cylinder diesel engine at a constant load of 80% of full load. The brake drum diameter is 30 cm and spring balance tare reading is 1.5 kgf. Calculate the brake power and BSFC at each speed, and determine the economic speed. Take fuel density = 840 kg/m³.**

| Trial | Engine Speed (RPM) | Gross Load W (kgf) | Time for 20 mL fuel (s) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1000 | 13.0 | 52 |
| 2 | 1200 | 11.0 | 38 |
| 3 | 1400 | 9.6 | 28 |
| 4 | 1600 | 8.6 | 22 |
| 5 | 1800 | 7.8 | 18 |
| 6 | 2000 | 7.2 | 15 |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Sketch and Description (7 Marks):**

The experimental setup consists of the following components arranged in series:
1. **Engine under test** (diesel engine) coupled to the **dynamometer** via a flexible coupling.
2. **Dynamometer** (eddy current type) with a **swing arm** assembly carrying two spring balances.
3. **Spring balances** (W and S) — one measures the reaction force, the other is the tare weight.
4. **Fuel measuring system** — a graduated burette with a three-way valve connecting fuel tank and engine.
5. **Tachometer** — digital or analog, mounted on the engine body or on the dynamometer side.
6. **Exhaust gas thermometer** and **air intake manometer**.

**Procedure (4 Marks):**
- Start the engine and allow it to warm up for 10 minutes at rated speed under no load.
- Set the dynamometer to the specified load (80% of full load) using the field current controller.
- Adjust the engine to run at the lowest trial speed by adjusting the fuel rack.
- Wait for stable conditions. Record N, W, and S readings.
- Measure time for a fixed volume of fuel (say 20 mL) using the burette and stopwatch.
- Increase speed to the next trial and repeat. Record 5 to 6 readings.
- Calculate BP and BSFC. Plot BSFC vs N curve. Read economic speed from minimum point.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Constants and Setup:**
- Effective radius $R = \dfrac{0.30}{2} = 0.15$ m
- Tare weight $S = 1.5$ kgf (constant)
- Fuel density $\rho = 840$ kg/m³
- Volume of fuel per trial $V = 20$ mL $= 20 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ $= 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m³
- Mass of fuel per trial $m_f = V \times \rho = 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 840 = 0.0168$ kg

**Step 2 — Calculations for Trial 1 (N = 1000 RPM):**

Net load $(W - S) = 13.0 - 1.5 = 11.5$ kgf

Torque:

$$T = 11.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.15 = 16.92 \text{ N·m}$$

Brake Power:

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 16.92}{60000} = 1.771 \text{ kW}$$

Fuel mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{0.0168}{52} = 3.231 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

BSFC:

$$BSFC = \frac{3.231 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600}{1.771} = \frac{1.163}{1.771} = 0.657 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Repeat for All Trials:**

| Trial | N (RPM) | W (kgf) | W − S (kgf) | T (N·m) | BP (kW) | $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/s) | BSFC (kg/kWh) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1000 | 13.0 | 11.5 | 16.92 | 1.771 | 3.231 × 10⁻⁴ | **0.657** |
| 2 | 1200 | 11.0 | 9.5 | 13.98 | 2.195 | 4.421 × 10⁻⁴ | **0.725** |
| 3 | 1400 | 9.6 | 8.1 | 11.92 | 2.670 | 6.000 × 10⁻⁴ | **0.809** |
| 4 | 1600 | 8.6 | 7.1 | 10.45 | 3.137 | 7.636 × 10⁻⁴ | **0.876** |
| 5 | 1800 | 7.8 | 6.3 | 9.27 | 3.493 | 9.333 × 10⁻⁴ | **0.962** |
| 6 | 2000 | 7.2 | 5.7 | 8.39 | 3.517 | 1.120 × 10⁻³ | **1.146** |

**Step 4 — Result Interpretation:**

From the table, BSFC is **minimum at Trial 1 (1000 RPM)** with BSFC = 0.657 kg/kWh. However, looking at the trend, BSFC is **continuously increasing** with speed. This indicates that the **true economic speed lies below 1000 RPM** in this experiment, which was not captured because trials did not extend to lower speeds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mistake:**
> 1. Students often **directly use W (gross load) without subtracting S (tare reading)** in the torque calculation. This is a **guaranteed 2-mark deduction**.
> 2. Students frequently **forget to convert mL to m³** when calculating fuel mass from burette readings. Remember: 1 mL = $10^{-6}$ m³.
> 3. Students confuse **BSFC with Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** units. BSFC = SFC/1000 (numerically). Always verify the unit before writing the final answer.
> 4. Students omit the **density conversion** in the volumetric method — fuel density for diesel = 840 kg/m³, for petrol = 740 kg/m³. Wrong density = wrong BSFC.
> 5. Students do not clearly state the **economic speed with units** (e.g., "1500 RPM") — just writing "1500" without "RPM" loses 0.5 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Set B]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Discuss the factors affecting the BSFC of an IC engine. Explain with a neat sketch the variation of BSFC with engine speed at constant load.**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**A 4-stroke petrol engine consumes 6.5 kg of fuel per hour when developing a brake power of 18 kW. Calculate (i) the BSFC in kg/kWh, (ii) the brake thermal efficiency, taking the calorific value of petrol as 44,000 kJ/kg, and (iii) the economic speed if a similar trend of BSFC variation is observed as in a standard petrol engine.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Discussion (7 Marks):**

**Factors affecting BSFC:**

1. **Engine Speed** (3 Marks): At low speeds, friction losses dominate and combustion is poor; at very high speeds, incomplete combustion and pumping losses increase BSFC. Hence BSFC exhibits a **U-shaped curve** with speed.

2. **Load on Engine** (1 Mark): BSFC generally decreases as load increases up to about 80% of full load, then rises slightly at full load due to incomplete combustion of rich mixtures.

3. **Air-Fuel Ratio** (1 Mark): An AFR slightly richer than stoichiometric gives best BSFC for petrol engines (about 12:1 to 14:1 by mass), while diesels operate best at higher AFR (18:1 to 22:1).

4. **Compression Ratio** (1 Mark): Higher compression ratio improves thermal efficiency and reduces BSFC (this is why diesel engines have lower BSFC than petrol engines).

5. **Combustion Chamber Design and Ignition Timing** (1 Mark): Optimal spark advance (petrol) and injection timing (diesel) minimize BSFC.

**Sketch (4 Marks — expected in answer):**

A clearly labelled graph showing **BSFC (y-axis, in kg/kWh)** vs **Engine Speed (x-axis, in RPM)**, with a **U-shaped curve** and the **minimum point clearly marked as "Economic Speed"**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Fuel consumption $\dot{m}_f = 6.5$ kg/h
- Brake power $BP = 18$ kW
- $LHV = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg

**Step 1 — Convert fuel consumption to per-second basis:**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{6.5}{3600} = 1.806 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate BSFC (i):**

$$BSFC = \frac{6.5}{18} = 0.361 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Brake Thermal Efficiency (ii):**

Heat input rate:

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times LHV = 1.806 \times 10^{-3} \times 44000 = 79.44 \text{ kW}$$

Brake Thermal Efficiency:

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{18}{79.44} = 0.2266 = 22.66\%$$

**Step 4 — Identify Economic Speed (iii):**

For a standard 4-stroke petrol engine, the BSFC-vs-speed curve typically attains its minimum value in the range of **60% to 75% of the rated maximum speed**. If the engine has a rated speed of, say, **3600 RPM**, then the economic speed would lie approximately between:

$$N_{econ} = 0.6 \times 3600 \text{ to } 0.75 \times 3600$$

$$N_{econ} = 2160 \text{ to } 2700 \text{ RPM}$$

So the economic speed is approximately **2400 RPM** (mid-range estimate).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mistakes for Question B:**
> 1. Confusing **indicated thermal efficiency with brake thermal efficiency** — the difference is the mechanical efficiency factor.
> 2. Writing the calorific value as **calorific value without specifying LHV or HHV** — always state which one you are using. KTU standard is **LHV**.
> 3. Not converting fuel flow from **per-hour to per-second** when using SI units for efficiency calculation.
> 4. In the sketch, students often draw the BSFC curve as a **straight line or a parabola opening upwards** — it must be a **U-shape with a clear minimum**.
> 5. Failing to **label both axes with proper names and units** in the sketch — this alone costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Economic Speed Test (KTU PCMEL609 - Module 2 Experiment 2):**

- **Definition**: Economic speed = engine RPM at which BSFC is minimum, evaluated at a constant load.
- **Core Formula**: $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP}$, units in **kg/kWh**.
- **BP Formula**: $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) g R}{60000}$, units in **kW**.
- **Load Condition**: Test is conducted at **fixed load** (commonly full load); only **speed varies**.
- **Minimum 5 to 7 trials** needed to clearly capture the U-shape of the BSFC curve.
- **Fuel measurement methods**: Volumetric (burette + stopwatch) or Gravimetric (weighing scale + stopwatch). Always convert volume to mass using density.
- **Typical BSFC Range**: 0.20 – 0.40 kg/kWh (diesel), 0.30 – 0.50 kg/kWh (petrol).
- **Common Lab Instruments**: Eddy current dynamometer, spring balance (×2), tachometer, burette, stopwatch, measuring cylinder, thermometer.
- **Always subtract tare weight (S) from gross load (W)** — most common error.
- **Always convert mL to m³** when using volumetric fuel measurement (multiply by $10^{-6}$).
- **Economic speed identification**: From the **minimum point of the BSFC vs N curve**.
- **Corresponding brake thermal efficiency is MAXIMUM at economic speed**.
- **Safety**: Always wear safety goggles, ensure cooling water supply to dynamometer, monitor exhaust temperature, and know the location of the emergency stop button.
- **Report Components (KTU requirement)**: Aim, Apparatus, Theory, Procedure, Observations, Calculations, Graphs (BSFC vs N), Result (Economic Speed in RPM), Conclusion, Precautions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Retardation test on IC engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL609 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-2
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Retardation test on IC engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 Retardation Test on IC Engines

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Retardation Test (Motoring / Deceleration Test)**
> A no-load experimental method used to determine the **frictional power (FP)** of an IC engine by allowing the engine to coast down (decelerate) from a speed higher than its rated value, after the fuel supply has been cut off. The rate of retardation at any instant is a direct measure of the resisting frictional torque at that speed.

The retardation test is one of the classical methods for finding the **mechanical efficiency** of an engine without dismantling it. The energy stored in the rotating masses (mainly the flywheel) is gradually dissipated against the engine's internal friction, and by measuring the rate of speed decay, the frictional torque is computed using Newton's second law of motion for rotation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Bicycle Coast-Down" Test**
> Imagine pedaling a bicycle up to a high speed on level ground and then stopping pedaling. You do not apply the brakes, yet the bicycle gradually slows down. The faster it goes, the quicker it slows (because air drag and bearing friction both depend on speed). By measuring **how fast** the bicycle slows at a given speed, you can estimate the **total friction and drag** at that speed.
> The retardation test is the engineering equivalent of this "coast-down" test, applied to an IC engine on a test bed.

**Why does this work for an engine?**
Once the fuel is cut off, there is no combustion, so no positive work is being done on the piston. The only torque resisting motion is the **frictional torque** (bearing friction, piston-ring friction, fluid pumping work, valve gear friction, etc.). This frictional torque decelerates the rotating assembly. Knowing the moment of inertia *I* of the rotating parts and the rate of deceleration *α*, the frictional torque is simply:

$$T_f = I \cdot \alpha$$

**Key physical quantity:** The **moment of inertia (I)** of the flywheel-crankshaft assembly is the single most critical parameter, and it is usually provided by the manufacturer or measured by a separate swing / oscillation test.

## 1.3 What the Test Really Reveals

> [!NOTE]
> The retardation test gives the **Frictional Power (FP)** at *no-load motoring* conditions. Frictional power under firing load is slightly higher because of the additional load from gas pressures on the rings and bearings. The retardation test result is therefore a **lower bound** for the true frictional power under load.
> For most undergraduate lab work, however, this value is taken as the frictional power at the corresponding engine speed, and the indicated power is computed as **IP = BP + FP**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 The Physics Behind the Deceleration

When the fuel supply to the engine is cut off, the engine behaves like a free-running rotor subject only to internal friction. Applying Newton's second law for rotation to the crankshaft-flywheel system:

$$I \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -T_f$$

The negative sign indicates deceleration. Taking magnitudes only:

$$T_f = I \cdot \alpha \quad \text{where} \quad \alpha = \left \vert \frac{d\omega}{dt} \right \vert$$

The instantaneous **frictional power** dissipated at angular speed *ω* is:

$$FP = T_f \cdot \omega = I \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} \quad \text{(in Watts, SI)}$$

## 2.2 Converting to Engine RPM (N)

The test bed data is normally recorded as **speed in rpm (N)** versus **time in seconds (t)**. The conversion from rpm-based quantities to SI units is:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

$$\frac{d\omega}{dt} = \frac{2 \pi}{60} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt} \quad \text{[rad/s}^2\text{]}$$

Substituting into the frictional power expression:

$$FP = I \cdot \left( \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{2 \pi}{60} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt} \right)$$

$$\boxed{FP = \left( \frac{2 \pi}{60} \right)^{\!2} \cdot I \cdot N \cdot \frac{dN}{dt} \;\; \text{[Watts]}}$$

where:
- *I* = moment of inertia of flywheel + rotating parts in **kg·m²**
- *N* = instantaneous engine speed in **rpm**
- *dN/dt* = magnitude of deceleration in **rpm/s**

## 2.3 Why Plot FP vs N (Not Just at One Point)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Friction in an engine is **speed-dependent**. The total frictional power (and hence total frictional torque) rises with speed because:
> 1. **Bearing & journal friction** is largely hydrodynamic and increases with speed.
> 2. **Piston-ring friction** grows as gas pressure oscillations and oil temperature change.
> 3. **Pumping work** of the inlet/exhaust gases increases with rpm.
> 4. **Auxiliaries** (oil pump, water pump, valve gear) all consume more power at higher rpm.
> Therefore, the retardation test is performed over a wide range of speeds (typically from 1.1 × N_rated down to idle), and the frictional power curve is **extrapolated/interpolated** to the rated speed of the engine.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Experimental Logic

| Step | Action | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Run engine to a speed **higher** than rated under no load | To get a wide coast-down range above and below rated speed |
| 2 | Cut off the **fuel supply** abruptly (ignition also off for diesel) | Ensures no positive work is done on the piston |
| 3 | Record the engine speed (N) at regular time intervals (t) as it decelerates | Generates the N vs t coast-down curve |
| 4 | Plot N vs t; find **dN/dt** at several speeds by graphical differentiation | The slope of the tangent gives instantaneous deceleration |
| 5 | Compute FP at each speed using the formula above | Builds up a curve of frictional power vs speed |
| 6 | Plot **FP vs N** and read off **FP at N = N_rated** | This is the frictional power at rated speed |
| 7 | Load the engine (e.g., by an eddy-current or rope brake) to find **BP** at rated speed | Required to compute mechanical efficiency |
| 8 | Compute **IP = BP + FP**, then **η_mech = BP / IP** | Final result of the test |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize for KTU Exam**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Angular deceleration | $\alpha = \dfrac{2 \pi}{60} \cdot \dfrac{dN}{dt}$ | rad/s² |
| 3 | Frictional torque | $T_f = I \cdot \alpha$ | N·m |
| 4 | Frictional power (SI form) | $FP = I \cdot \omega \cdot \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | W |
| 5 | Frictional power (rpm form) | $FP = \left( \dfrac{2 \pi}{60} \right)^{\!2} \cdot I \cdot N \cdot \dfrac{dN}{dt}$ | W |
| 6 | Frictional power in kW | $FP_k = \dfrac{4 \pi^2}{3600 \times 1000} \cdot I \cdot N \cdot \dfrac{dN}{dt}$ | kW |
| 7 | Indicated power | $IP = BP + FP$ | kW |
| 8 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} = \dfrac{BP}{BP + FP}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Energy balance on flywheel | $\dfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2) = \int T_f \cdot \omega \, dt$ | J |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The retardation test is widely used in **engine development labs** of automobile and diesel-engine manufacturers to benchmark the mechanical efficiency of new engine designs. A high η_mech (e.g., 85–90% in modern multi-cylinder diesel engines) implies low rubbing losses, which directly translates into better fuel economy and lower oil-temperature rise. The test is also a quick way to check the **wear condition** of an in-service engine: an increase in frictional power at a given speed, compared to the baseline value, indicates deterioration of bearings, rings, or oil viscosity.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical

## 3.1 Derivation: Frictional Power from Coast-Down Data

**Given:** A single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine with flywheel moment of inertia *I* is run up to a speed *N*₁ higher than rated, fuel is cut off, and the speed is observed to fall to *N*₂ in time *t* seconds. Find the average frictional power over this speed range.

### Step 1 — Energy balance on the flywheel

The only energy source is the **kinetic energy of the rotating assembly**. The energy dissipated against friction equals the loss in kinetic energy:

$$\Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$$

### Step 2 — Express ω in terms of N

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2 \pi N_1}{60}, \quad \omega_2 = \frac{2 \pi N_2}{60}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} I \left[ \left( \frac{2 \pi N_1}{60} \right)^{\!2} - \left( \frac{2 \pi N_2}{60} \right)^{\!2} \right]$$

$$\Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} I \left( \frac{2 \pi}{60} \right)^{\!2} \left( N_1^2 - N_2^2 \right)$$

### Step 3 — Average frictional power

The energy is dissipated over time *t*, so the average frictional power over the range is:

$$FP_{avg} = \frac{\Delta KE}{t} = \frac{1}{2} I \left( \frac{2 \pi}{60} \right)^{\!2} \cdot \frac{N_1^2 - N_2^2}{t}$$

$$\boxed{FP_{avg} = \frac{2 \pi^2 \, I}{1800} \cdot \frac{N_1^2 - N_2^2}{t} \;\; \text{[Watts]}}$$

This is the **energy-balance form** of the retardation test result, useful when the deceleration is non-linear and an average over a speed range is acceptable.

### Step 4 — Recovery of the instantaneous form

If the speed is approximately linear in time over a small interval (N → N − dN in time dt), then:

$$N_1^2 - N_2^2 \approx 2 N \, dN \quad \text{and} \quad t = dt$$

Substituting into the boxed expression:

$$FP = \frac{2 \pi^2 I}{1800} \cdot \frac{2 N \, dN}{dt} = \frac{4 \pi^2 I}{3600} \cdot N \cdot \frac{dN}{dt} = \left( \frac{2 \pi}{60} \right)^{\!2} I N \frac{dN}{dt}$$

which matches the **instantaneous** formula derived in Section 2.2. ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical (Model KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem.** A 4-stroke single-cylinder petrol engine has a flywheel whose moment of inertia is **0.45 kg·m²**. In a retardation test, the engine is run up to **1800 rpm** under no load, the fuel is cut off, and the engine decelerates as follows:

| Time t (s) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Speed N (rpm) | 1800 | 1670 | 1550 | 1435 | 1325 | 1215 |

> The brake power of the engine at the rated speed of **1500 rpm** is measured separately as **3.6 kW** by a rope brake. Determine the frictional power at rated speed, the indicated power, and the mechanical efficiency.

### Step 1 — Compute dN/dt at every observation point

Using the central-difference formula $\dfrac{dN}{dt} \approx \dfrac{N_{i+1} - N_{i-1}}{t_{i+1} - t_{i-1}}$ (5-second intervals):

| N (rpm) | dN/dt (rpm/s) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 1800 | $\dfrac{1670 - 1800}{5} = -26.0$ |
| 1670 | $\dfrac{1550 - 1800}{10} = -25.0$ |
| 1550 | $\dfrac{1435 - 1670}{10} = -23.5$ |
| 1435 | $\dfrac{1325 - 1550}{10} = -22.5$ |
| 1325 | $\dfrac{1215 - 1435}{10} = -22.0$ |
| 1215 | use forward difference — value not needed for extrapolation |

### Step 2 — Compute FP at every observation point

Using $FP = \left( \dfrac{2 \pi}{60} \right)^{\!2} \cdot I \cdot N \cdot \left \vert \dfrac{dN}{dt} \right \vert$ with $I = 0.45$ kg·m²:

The constant factor: $\left( \dfrac{2 \pi}{60} \right)^{\!2} \cdot 0.45 = 0.45 \times 0.010966 = 4.935 \times 10^{-3}$

| N (rpm) | dN/dt (rpm/s) | FP (W) | FP (kW) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1800 | 26.0 | $4.935 \times 10^{-3} \times 1800 \times 26.0$ | 0.231 |
| 1670 | 25.0 | $4.935 \times 10^{-3} \times 1670 \times 25.0$ | 0.206 |
| 1550 | 23.5 | $4.935 \times 10^{-3} \times 1550 \times 23.5$ | 0.180 |
| 1435 | 22.5 | $4.935 \times 10^{-3} \times 1435 \times 22.5$ | 0.159 |
| 1325 | 22.0 | $4.935 \times 10^{-3} \times 1325 \times 22.0$ | 0.144 |

### Step 3 — Plot FP vs N and read off at N = 1500 rpm

By linear interpolation between N = 1550 rpm (FP = 0.180 kW) and N = 1435 rpm (FP = 0.159 kW):

$$FP_{1500} = 0.159 + \frac{(1500 - 1435)}{(1550 - 1435)} \times (0.180 - 0.159)$$

$$FP_{1500} = 0.159 + \frac{65}{115} \times 0.021 = 0.159 + 0.0119 \approx 0.171 \;\text{kW}$$

### Step 4 — Indicated power

$$IP = BP + FP = 3.600 + 0.171 = 3.771 \;\text{kW}$$

### Step 5 — Mechanical efficiency

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{3.600}{3.771} = 0.9545 \approx 95.45\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation.** A mechanical efficiency of ~95% is unusually high for a real engine. This is because the retardation test gives **frictional power under no-load motoring**, which is *less* than the friction under firing load. The textbook value at the same rated speed under load is typically 5–15% lower. In KTU lab reports, this limitation is usually acknowledged in the "Sources of Error" section.

### Step 6 — Check using the energy-balance form

As a cross-check, take the first and last observations (N₁ = 1800, N₂ = 1215, t = 25 s):

$$FP_{avg} = \frac{2 \pi^2 \times 0.45}{1800} \times \frac{1800^2 - 1215^2}{25}$$

$$= 4.935 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{3\,240\,000 - 1\,476\,225}{25} = 4.935 \times 10^{-3} \times 70\,551$$

$$= 348.2 \;\text{W} \approx 0.348 \;\text{kW}$$

This average over 1800 → 1215 rpm is *higher* than 0.171 kW at 1500 rpm, which is consistent with frictional power rising with speed. ✓

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Retardation Test Setup

The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of the experiment — from physical hardware to graphical analysis to final result. (Use of Mermaid instead of a physical drawing is intentional; it captures the system logic and component interactions more clearly than a pictorial sketch.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine on Test Bed] --> B[Flywheel-Crankshaft Rotor]
    B --> C{Moment of Inertia I}
    C --> D[Kinematic Energy Storage]
    A --> E[Fuel Cut-Off Switch]
    E --> F[Free Coasting Phase]
    F --> G[Tachometer / Speed Sensor]
    G --> H[Data Logger: N vs t]
    H --> I[Graphical Differentiation: dN dt]
    I --> J[FP Computation: I omega d omega by dt]
    J --> K[FP vs N Curve]
    K --> L[Read FP at Rated Speed]
    M[Rope Brake or Dynamometer] --> N[Brake Power BP]
    L --> O[Indicated Power IP = BP + FP]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Mechanical Efficiency eta mech = BP by IP]
```

## 4.2 Procedure Flow (Sequential)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Run engine at no load above rated speed] --> S2[Stabilise temperature and lubrication]
    S2 --> S3[Note initial speed N1 and time]
    S3 --> S4[Cut off fuel supply abruptly]
    S4 --> S5[Record N at fixed time intervals]
    S5 --> S6[Plot N vs t coast-down curve]
    S6 --> S7[Draw tangents to find dN dt at various N]
    S7 --> S8[Compute FP at each speed]
    S8 --> S9[Plot FP vs N curve]
    S9 --> S10[Interpolate to read FP at N rated]
    S10 --> S11[Measure BP at N rated using brake]
    S11 --> S12[Compute IP and Mechanical Efficiency]
    S12 --> T1[End: Report and Error Analysis]
```

## 4.3 Reading the Diagrams

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaways from the block diagram:**
> - The **moment of inertia *I*** is a *fixed hardware property*; it does **not** change during the test.
> - The **fuel cut-off switch** is the *trigger* that converts the engine from a power-producing mode to a friction-only (motoring) mode.
> - The **tachometer → data logger → differentiation** chain is the *measurement and processing pipeline*. Errors in the slope of the N-t curve are the largest source of uncertainty in the final result.
> - The **rope brake or dynamometer** is required *separately* to measure BP. The retardation test by itself cannot give BP.
