The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow of energy and instrumentation** in a typical KTU-style rotary blower performance test rig. Note: all node IDs are alphanumeric (letter-prefixed), and labels contain no markdown bold/italic markers, in compliance with the Mermaid safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Motor Three Phase AC] --> S1[Coupling Flexible]
    S1 --> R1[Rotary Blower Impeller Assembly]
    S1 --> D1[Dynamometer Prony Brake]
    D1 --> LB1[Load Cell and Lever Arm]
    LB1 --> WT1[Weighing Scale Slotted Weights]
    R1 --> CV1[Volute Casing]
    CV1 --> TV1[Throttle Valve Delivery Side]
    TV1 --> PT1[Pressure Gauge Delivery P2]
    TV1 --> TT1[Thermometer Discharge T2]
    CV1 --> OR1[Orifice Plate Suction Side]
    OR1 --> MH1[U Tube Manometer H reading]
    OR1 --> T1S[Thermometer Suction T1]
    OR1 --> GP1[Atmospheric Air Inlet Filter]
    D1 --> NC1[Tachometer Speed N rpm]
    WT1 --> CALC[Data Acquisition and Calculation]
    PT1 --> CALC
    TT1 --> CALC
    MH1 --> CALC
    T1S --> CALC
    NC1 --> CALC
    CALC --> RSLT[Output Q P ratio eta isen eta mech shaft power]
```

### 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([Start of Test]) --> S2[Switch on motor allow warm up 5 min]
    S2 --> S3[Record no load speed N0 and no load P2]
    S3 --> S4[Set throttle to first position]
    S4 --> S5[Stabilize readings wait 1 minute]
    S5 --> S6[Record P2 T2 T1 H N and load W]
    S6 --> S7{More throttle positions to test}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Reduce throttle opening next setting]
    S8 --> S5
    S7 -- No --> S9[Compute Q eta isen P shaft for each setting]
    S9 --> S10[Plot performance curves P2 vs Q P shaft vs Q eta vs Q]
    S10 --> S11[Switch off motor release load gradually]
    S11 --> S12([End of Test])
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Text-Based) of a Centrifugal Blower

```
                ─── Suction (P1, T1)
                     │
                     ▼
        ┌─────────────────────────┐
        │       INLET EYE         │
        │            ┌─────┐      │
        │            │ I  │       │
        │            │ M  │       │   ← Impeller (I/M) rotates
        │            │ P  │       │     at speed N
        │            │ E  │       │
        │            │ L  │       │
        │            │ L  │       │
        │            │ E  │       │
        │            │ R  │       │
        │            └─────┘      │
        │              │          │
        │       ┌──────┴──────┐   │
        │       │   VOLUTE    │   │   ← Volute (V) converts
        │       │   CASING    │   │     kinetic energy → pressure
        │       └──────┬──────┘   │
        │              │          │
        └──────────────┼──────────┘
                       ▼
                ─── Delivery (P2, T2)
```

### 4.4 Instrumentation Layout Summary

| Location | Instrument | Quantity Measured | Symbol |
|----------|------------|-------------------|--------|
| Suction | Thermometer | Inlet temperature | $T_1$ |
| Suction | Barometer (assumed) | Atmospheric $P_1$ | $P_1$ |
| Suction (orifice) | U-tube manometer | Orifice head $H$ | $H$ |
| Discharge | Pressure gauge | Discharge pressure | $P_2$ |
| Discharge | Thermometer | Discharge temperature | $T_2$ |
| Shaft | Tachometer | Rotational speed | $N$ |
| Brake | Spring balance + lever | Torque $T = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot R$ | $T$ |
| Orifice | Plate (D, d) | Flow measurement | $Q$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]**
> **Q:** Define isentropic efficiency of a rotary compressor and state its formula.
>
> **Model Answer:** Isentropic efficiency is the ratio of the *minimum* (isentropic) work required to compress a given mass of gas between two specified pressures, to the *actual* work supplied to the compressor shaft. Mathematically,
> $$\eta_{isen} = \frac{W_{isen}}{W_{act}} = \frac{c_p (T_2' - T_1)}{c_p (T_2 - T_1)} = \frac{T_2' - T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$
> where $T_2' = T_1 (r_p)^{\frac{\kappa-1}{\kappa}}$ is the isentropic discharge temperature and $T_2$ is the actual discharge temperature. *(3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]**
> **Q:** Distinguish between a blower and a compressor based on the pressure ratio.
>
> **Model Answer:** A **blower** is a machine that compresses gas to a pressure ratio typically up to **1.2** (i.e., pressure rise up to ~20% of inlet pressure); a **compressor** delivers pressure ratios above **1.2** and can go up to several atmospheres. Blowers often approximate isothermal compression due to slow running, while compressors approach adiabatic behaviour. *(3 marks)*

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat line diagram, describe the experimental setup used to conduct a performance test on a centrifugal blower in the lab. List the instruments used and the readings recorded. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** During a test on a rotary blower, the following observations were noted:
- Suction pressure $P_1 = 1.02$ bar, $T_1 = 305$ K
- Discharge pressure $P_2 = 1.25$ bar, $T_2 = 340$ K
- Orifice diameter $d = 25$ mm, pipe diameter $D = 50$ mm
- Manometer reading $H = 0.15$ m of water, $C_d = 0.61$
- Speed $N = 3000$ rpm, brake load torque $T = 22$ N·m
- $\rho_{air} = 1.20$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K), $\kappa = 1.4$

Calculate the **(i)** pressure ratio, **(ii)** volumetric flow rate, **(iii)** shaft power, and **(iv)** isentropic efficiency. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Experimental Setup (7 marks):**

```
Components (4 marks):
  - Centrifugal blower coupled to AC motor via flexible coupling
  - Suction side: orifice plate (D, d) with U-tube manometer
  - Discharge side: pressure gauge, thermometer, throttle valve
  - Brake side: prony brake with rope and spring balance (or
    electrical dynamometer with load cell)
  - Tachometer (digital/optical) for speed N

Procedure (2 marks):
  1. Start motor, allow 5 min warm-up, record no-load N and P2
  2. Apply throttle, wait 1 min for stabilization
  3. Record P1, T1, P2, T2, H, N, spring-balance readings
  4. Repeat for 5-6 throttle settings covering wide Q range
  5. Plot P2 vs Q, Pshaft vs Q, eta_isen vs Q

Instruments (1 mark): list as in Section 4.4 above.
```

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Pressure ratio:**

$$
r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{1.25}{1.02} = 1.2255
$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate:**

Orifice area: $A_o = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4}$ m²

Effective head:

$$
h = H \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{d}{D} \right)^4 \right] = 0.15 \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{25}{50} \right)^4 \right] = 0.15 \cdot 0.9375 = 0.1406 \text{ m}
$$

Velocity at orifice:

$$
V_o = \sqrt{2 g h} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.1406} = 1.661 \text{ m/s}
$$

Volumetric flow:

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot V_o = 0.61 \cdot 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1.661 = 4.972 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(iii) Shaft power:**

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 3000 \cdot 22}{60} = 6911.5 \text{ W} = 6.91 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iv) Isentropic efficiency:**

Isentropic exit temperature:

$$
T_2' = T_1 (r_p)^{\frac{\kappa-1}{\kappa}} = 305 \cdot (1.2255)^{0.2857} = 305 \cdot 1.0612 = 323.66 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\eta_{isen} = \frac{T_2' - T_1}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{323.66 - 305}{340 - 305} = \frac{18.66}{35} = 0.5331 = 53.31\%
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working principle of a **Roots blower** and a **centrifugal blower**. Compare their constructional and operational features. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A two-lobe Roots blower delivers $Q = 0.10$ m³/s of air at suction conditions of $P_1 = 1.0$ bar and $T_1 = 300$ K. The discharge pressure is $P_2 = 1.20$ bar. The mechanical efficiency of the blower is 80% and electrical input to the motor is 18 kW. Calculate the **(i)** mass flow rate, **(ii)** isentropic and isothermal work per kg, **(iii)** overall efficiency, and **(iv)** shaft power. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Working Principle & Comparison (7 marks):**

*Roots Blower (3 marks):*
- Two figure-8-shaped rotors (lobes) rotate in opposite directions inside a casing.
- Air trapped between the lobes and casing is *carried* from inlet to outlet **without internal compression** during transit.
- Compression happens only at the moment the trapped pocket meets the higher-pressure discharge side.
- Flow is **pulsation-free** at steady speed, **fixed displacement** per revolution.
- Typical application: pneumatic conveying, sewage aeration, gas boosting.

*Centrifugal Blower (2 marks):*
- Impeller rotates at high speed; air enters axially at the eye.
- Centrifugal action flings air radially outward through the impeller blades.
- Velocity head is converted to pressure head in the volute casing.
- Flow and pressure vary with speed (centrifugal action is dynamic).
- Typical application: HVAC, combustion air, boiler FD/ID.

*Comparison Table (2 marks):*

| Feature | Roots Blower | Centrifugal Blower |
|---------|--------------|---------------------|
| Type | Positive displacement | Dynamic (turbo) |
| Pressure ratio | 1.1–1.5 typical | 1.2–4 typical |
| Flow vs pressure | Independent (PD) | Inter-dependent |
| Compression | During discharge only | Continuous in impeller |
| Maintenance | Higher (timing gears) | Lower (single impeller) |
| Pulsation | Low at constant speed | Virtually zero |
| Sensitivity to dust | Lower (lobes are robust) | Higher (blade fouling) |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \frac{P_1}{R T_1} \cdot Q = \frac{1.0 \times 10^5}{287 \cdot 300} \cdot 0.10 = 1.1614 \cdot 0.10 = 0.1161 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**(ii) Isentropic and isothermal work:**

$$
r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{1.20}{1.00} = 1.20
$$

Isentropic work:

$$
w_{isen} = c_p T_1 \left[ (r_p)^{\frac{\kappa-1}{\kappa}} - 1 \right] = 1005 \cdot 300 \left[ (1.20)^{0.2857} - 1 \right] = 301500 \left[ 1.0547 - 1 \right] = 301500 \cdot 0.0547 = 16492 \text{ J/kg}
$$

Isothermal work:

$$
w_{isoth} = R T_1 \ln(r_p) = 287 \cdot 300 \cdot \ln(1.20) = 86100 \cdot 0.18232 = 15698 \text{ J/kg}
$$

**(iii) Overall efficiency (using isentropic reference):**

Shaft power input = motor input × $\eta_{motor}$ assumed ≈ 100% (not given); using $P_{shaft}$ as electrical input × mechanical efficiency:

$$
P_{shaft} = P_{elec} \cdot \eta_{motor} \approx 18 \text{ kW (assuming direct drive, }\eta_{motor} = 1\text{)}
$$

Air power (isentropic basis):

$$
P_{air,isen} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{isen} = 0.1161 \cdot 16492 = 1914.7 \text{ W} = 1.915 \text{ kW}
$$

Overall efficiency:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{air,isen}}{P_{shaft}} = \frac{1.915}{18.0} = 0.1064 = 10.64\%
$$

**(iv) Shaft power (using mechanical efficiency):**

$$
\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_{air,act}}{P_{shaft}} \Rightarrow P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{air,act}}{\eta_{mech}}
$$

But here $P_{air,act}$ is the actual compression work input, not isentropic. From given motor power (18 kW) and assuming $\eta_{mech} = 80\%$ refers to the blower unit (i.e., mechanical losses between shaft and compression process):

$$
P_{shaft} = P_{elec} = 18 \text{ kW (motor-side input)}
$$

If the question expects the *shaft power* to be derived as the *air power at shaft*, then with $\eta_{mech} = 0.80$:

$$
P_{shaft,blower} = \frac{P_{air,act}}{0.80}
$$

where $P_{air,act} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{act}$, and $w_{act}$ may be found from the isentropic efficiency if known, or directly:

$$
w_{act} = \frac{w_{isen}}{\eta_{isen}}
$$

Taking $\eta_{isen} = 60\%$ (typical Roots blower):

$$
w_{act} = \frac{16492}{0.60} = 27487 \text{ J/kg}
$$

$$
P_{air,act} = 0.1161 \cdot 27487 = 3191 \text{ W} = 3.191 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_{shaft,blower} = \frac{3.191}{0.80} = 3.989 \text{ kW} \approx 4.0 \text{ kW}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the correction factor in orifice flow** $[1 - (d/D)^4]$ — instant 1-mark penalty.
> 2. **Using gauge pressure instead of absolute** for $P_1$, $P_2$ in efficiency formulas — 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Mixing $\eta_{isen}$ and $\eta_{isoth}$** — examiners specifically ask for one; substituting the other formula fetches **zero** marks even if arithmetic is correct.
> 4. **Neglecting the units in shaft power** ($N$ in rpm, not rad/s) — the $\dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ formula MUST be used as-is.
> 5. **Forgetting to record atmospheric pressure** — examiners give it as data; students who use $P_1 = 1.0$ bar without confirming lose 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Not converting mm of water to m** — 0.5-mark penalty.
> 7. **Plotting curves with Q on the y-axis** — convention is $P_2$ on y-axis vs $Q$ on x-axis. Reverse → 1-mark deduction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- [x] A **rotary blower** is a low-pressure-ratio ($r_p \le 1.2$) continuous-flow machine; **compressor** implies higher ratios.
- [x] The **two main classes** are **Positive Displacement** (Roots, screw, vane) and **Dynamic/Turbo** (centrifugal, axial).
- [x] **Volumetric flow** $Q$ is measured using an **orifice meter** with formula $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gh}$, where $h = H[1 - (d/D)^4]$.
- [x] **Shaft power** is from a **prony brake / dynamometer**: $P_{shaft} = 2\pi N T / 60$.
- [x] **Isentropic efficiency** is the most common KPI: $\eta_{isen} = (T_2' - T_1) / (T_2 - T_1)$ with $T_2' = T_1 (r_p)^{(\kappa-1)/\kappa}$.
- [x] **Isothermal efficiency** uses $w_{isoth} = R T_1 \ln(r_p)$ and is preferred for low-speed blowers.
- [x] **Mechanical efficiency** $\eta_{mech} = P_{air,act} / P_{shaft}$ separates aerodynamic losses from bearing/friction losses.
- [x] **Polytropic index** $n$ lies between 1 (isothermal) and $\kappa$ (isentropic); for real machines, $1.2 \le n \le 1.35$.
- [x] **Slip factor** (centrifugal) reduces Euler head: actual head $\phi \cdot H_{Euler}$.
- [x] **Three mandatory plots**: $P_2$ vs $Q$, $P_{shaft}$ vs $Q$, $\eta_{isen}$ vs $Q$.
- [x] **Always use absolute pressures** in $r_p$ and efficiency formulas.
- [x] **Constants to memorize**: $c_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K), $R = 287$ J/(kg·K), $\kappa = 1.4$, $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- [x] **Examiner's hot topics**: orifice flow correction, shaft power formula with rpm, isentropic vs isothermal distinction, performance curve shape.
- [x] **Safety**: gradually release brake load, never suddenly — to avoid motor burnout and personal injury.
- [x] **Repeatability**: 5–6 readings at distinct throttle openings; plot a smooth curve, not a zigzag.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and performance test on refrigeration (Refrigeration Test rig)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL609 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-2 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study and performance test on refrigeration (Refrigeration Test rig) # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: STUDY AND PERFORMANCE TEST ON REFRIGERATION ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Refrigeration Test Rig:** A laboratory apparatus designed to evaluate the steady-state performance of a **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** by measuring the thermodynamic state properties at the four cardinal points of the cycle (compressor inlet/outlet, condenser outlet, and evaporator inlet) under controlled load conditions, and subsequently computing the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, **Refrigerating Effect (RE)**, and **Compressor Work** to assess conformity with design specifications. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMEL609)** syllabus, this experiment belongs to the *Thermal Engineering Lab-II* and tests the student's ability to: - Identify the major components of a refrigeration test rig. - Record the pressure, temperature, mass flow rate, and electrical inputs. - Calculate the **actual COP**, **theoretical COP**, **relative COP**, and **compressor efficiency**. - Plot the **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram** and the **Temperature-Entropy (T-s) diagram**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Simple Analogy — The "Sweat-Cooling" Refrigerator:** > Imagine you pour cold water from a clay pot (evaporator) on a hot summer day. The water absorbs heat from the surrounding air and evaporates, leaving the pot's surface cool. A refrigeration system does **exactly the same thing** — it uses a special "water" called **refrigerant** (R-134a, R-22) that *boils at a very low temperature*. The refrigerant absorbs heat inside the fridge (evaporator), turns into vapour, gets **squeezed** by a compressor to high pressure, **rejects** that heat to the room through the condenser coils (the hot backside of your fridge), and returns as a liquid — ready to absorb heat again. > **The test rig is simply a "transparent window" into this cycle**, where we put measuring instruments (thermometers, pressure gauges, wattmeters) at every critical point to find out *how well* the fridge is performing. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics The performance of any refrigeration system is benchmarked against these fundamental dimensionless/rated quantities: - **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** — ratio of desired cooling to work input (ideal Carnot COP for refrigeration is **COP_carnot = T_L / (T_H − T_L)**, where temperatures are in Kelvin). - **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** — **1 TR = 3.517 kW** (≈ 210 kJ/min ≈ 50.4 MJ/day), the heat extraction rate required to freeze 1 ton (2000 lb) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours. - **Specific Enthalpy (h)** — measured in **kJ/kg**, read directly from refrigerant property tables. - **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** — measured in **kJ/kg**. - **Standard Test Condition** — typically **evaporator temperature: −5 °C to 5 °C**, **condenser temperature: 40 °C to 55 °C**, **sub-cooling: 5 °C**, **superheat: 5 °C** as per IS: 666 and ASHRAE standards. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The expected outcome of this experiment is the determination of the **COP of the refrigeration test rig** and comparison with the **theoretical (Carnot) COP** to compute the **Second-Law (or 2nd Law) efficiency** of the system. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRS) — Operational Breakdown A **single-stage, single-acting VCRS** consists of four primary components connected in a closed loop: 1. **Compressor** (Reciprocating / Rotary / Scroll) — Low-pressure vapour is compressed to high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour. 2. **Condenser** (Air-cooled / Water-cooled) — High-pressure vapour rejects heat to the surroundings and condenses to a saturated/sub-cooled liquid. 3. **Expansion Device** (Capillary tube / Thermostatic Expansion Valve / TXV) — High-pressure liquid is throttled to a low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture (**h_3 = h_4**, isenthalpic process). 4. **Evaporator** (Finned coil / Plate type) — Low-pressure liquid absorbs heat (Refrigerating Effect) and evaporates to saturated/slightly superheated vapour. ### 2.2 Thermodynamic State Points (The "Four Cardinal Points") | State Point | Location in Cycle | Typical Property | |-------------|-------------------|------------------| | **Point 1** | Compressor Inlet | Saturated / Superheated Vapour, Low P, Low T | | **Point 2** | Compressor Inlet of Condenser | Superheated Vapour, High P, High T | | **Point 3** | Condenser Outlet / TXV Inlet | Saturated / Sub-cooled Liquid, High P | | **Point 4** | Evaporator Inlet | Saturated Liquid-Vapour Mix, Low P, Low T | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** State point **3 → 4** is an **isenthalpic throttling** process (no work, no heat transfer, negligible KE/PE change). The energy equation reduces to: > $$h_3 = h_4$$ > This is the *only* process in the ideal VCRS that is **irreversible** (entropy increases: $s_4 > s_3$). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All tables below use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of literal `|` to preserve Markdown table integrity. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|-----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed per kg of refrigerant in evaporator | | 2 | **Compressor Work (W_c)** | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Work input per kg of refrigerant | | 3 | **Heat Rejected in Condenser** | $Q_{cond} = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | Energy balance: RE + W_c = Q_cond | | 4 | **Actual COP** | $COP_{actual} = \dfrac{RE}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of desired to work input | | 5 | **Carnot COP** | $COP_{carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{T_{evap}}{T_{cond} - T_{evap}}$ | dimensionless | Upper theoretical limit; T in **Kelvin** | | 6 | **Relative COP / 2nd-Law Efficiency** | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{carnot}} \times 100$ | % | Indicates how close actual cycle is to ideal | | 7 | **Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cooling}}{RE}$ | kg/s | Where $\dot{Q}_{cooling}$ is the cooling load (kW) | | 8 | **Refrigeration Tonnage** | $TR = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cooling}}{3.517}$ | TR | $1 \, TR = 3.517 \, kW$ | | 9 | **Compressor Isentropic Efficiency** | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | $h_{2s}$ = enthalpy at compressor exit for isentropic compression | | 10 | **Volumetric Refrigerating Capacity** | $q_v = \rho_1 \times RE$ | kJ/m³ | Used for compressor sizing | | 11 | **Heat Balance (Condenser Side)** | $\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_3)$ | kW | Heat rejected per second | | 12 | **Heat Balance (Evaporator Side)** | $\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m} \times (h_1 - h_4)$ | kW | Heat absorbed per second | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Conversion — The Three to Memorize:** > - **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min** > - **1 kW = 0.2843 TR** > - **1 kcal/h = 1.163 W = 3.968 × 10⁻⁴ TR** (used in older Indian problems) ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The refrigeration test rig is **not** an academic curiosity — it is the bedrock of every commercial cold-chain, HVAC, and industrial cooling system: - **Domestic Refrigerators & ACs** — Capacity rating, energy efficiency (EER, ISEER) are all derived from test-rig measurements. - **Cold Storage Warehouses** — Potato, dairy, pharma industries need certified TR ratings. - **Cascade & Multi-Stage Systems** — Lab-scale VCRS rigs validate the design of LNG liquefaction plants. - **Heat Pumps** — Identical cycle, opposite direction; the rig data is used to size domestic water heaters. - **Automotive AC Loops** — R-134a and R-1234yf transitions in vehicles are validated using similar rigs. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the COP Expression for an Ideal VCRS The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is, by definition, the ratio of the *useful* (refrigerating) effect to the *work* (or energy) that must be supplied to obtain that effect: $$COP = \frac{\text{Desired Output (Refrigerating Effect)}}{\text{Required Input (Compressor Work)}}$$ ### 3.1.1 Step 1: Energy Balance Across the Evaporator (Control Volume) Apply the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to the evaporator. Neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes, and assuming **adiabatic** (well-insulated) and no work transfer: $$\dot{m} \cdot h_1 = \dot{Q}_{evap} + \dot{m} \cdot h_4$$ > **Logical Step:** Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ is constant throughout a *single-loop* VCRS. Heat absorbed in evaporator = $\dot{Q}_{evap}$. Rearranging: $$\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$$ Therefore, **Refrigerating Effect per kg:** $$RE = \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{\dot{m}} = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ ### 3.1.2 Step 2: Energy Balance Across the Compressor Apply SFEE to the compressor (adiabatic, work input = $\dot{W}_c$): $$\dot{m} \cdot h_1 + \dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \cdot h_2$$ > **Logical Step:** No heat transfer, but work is *added* to the refrigerant. Rearranging: $$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$$ Therefore, **Specific Compressor Work:** $$w_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ ### 3.1.3 Step 3: The COP Ratio Substitute the per-unit-mass expressions: $$COP_{actual} = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ > **Logical Step:** Since $\dot{m}$ cancels out, COP is an *intensive* property — independent of system size. ### 3.1.4 Step 4: Carnot COP (Theoretical Maximum) For a **reversible (Carnot) cycle** operating between two thermal reservoirs at $T_L$ (evaporator, in Kelvin) and $T_H$ (condenser, in Kelvin): $$COP_{carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{T_{evap}}{T_{cond} - T_{evap}}$$ > **Logical Step:** This is the *upper limit* — no real cycle can exceed this. Temperatures **must** be in Kelvin to be thermodynamically valid. ## 3.2 Second-Law (Relative) Efficiency $$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{carnot}}$$ If the value is closer to 1, the cycle is more reversible. Typical VCRS systems achieve **$\eta_{II} \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$** (40–60 % of theoretical). ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Complete KTU Board-Style Solution > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example — KTU University Exam Pattern (Dec 2023-type):** > > A refrigeration test rig uses **R-134a** as the refrigerant. The following steady-state readings are recorded: > - Evaporator pressure: **2.21 bar** (saturation temperature ≈ −10 °C) > - Condenser pressure: **12 bar** (saturation temperature ≈ 46.3 °C) > - Compressor inlet temperature: **−5 °C** (superheated by 5 K) > - Condenser exit temperature: **40 °C** (sub-cooled by 6.3 K) > - Mass flow rate: **2.5 kg/min** > - Compressor power input: **1.2 kW** > > **Required:** (a) Plot the cycle on a **P-h** diagram. (b) Calculate the **Refrigerating Effect (RE)**, **COP_actual**, **Carnot COP**, **Second-Law efficiency**, and **Refrigeration Tonnage (TR)**. ### 3.3.1 Step (a): P-h Diagram Description The cycle is plotted on a P-h chart of R-134a with the following state points: - **Point 1** (Compressor Inlet): P₁ = 2.21 bar, T₁ = −5 °C → Superheated region. From R-134a superheat tables: $h_1 = 247.32 \, kJ/kg$, $s_1 = 0.9390 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$. - **Point 2** (Compressor Outlet / Condenser Inlet): P₂ = 12 bar, $s_2 = s_1 = 0.9390 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$ (assuming ideal isentropic compression). From superheat tables at 12 bar and s = 0.9390: $h_2 = 281.78 \, kJ/kg$. - **Point 3** (Condenser Exit): P₃ = 12 bar, T₃ = 40 °C → Sub-cooled region. From sub-cooled liquid tables: $h_3 \approx 111.26 \, kJ/kg$. - **Point 4** (Evaporator Inlet): P₄ = 2.21 bar, $h_4 = h_3 = 111.26 \, kJ/kg$ (isenthalpic throttling). ### 3.3.2 Step (b): Calculations **Refrigerating Effect (RE):** $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 247.32 - 111.26 = 136.06 \, kJ/kg$$ **Specific Compressor Work (w_c):** $$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 281.78 - 247.32 = 34.46 \, kJ/kg$$ **Actual COP:** $$COP_{actual} = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{136.06}{34.46} = 3.948$$ **Carnot COP:** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin. $$T_L = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \, K$$ $$T_H = 46.3 + 273.15 = 319.45 \, K$$ $$COP_{carnot} = \frac{263.15}{319.45 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{56.30} = 4.674$$ **Second-Law Efficiency:** $$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{carnot}} = \frac{3.948}{4.674} = 0.8446 = 84.46\%$$ **Refrigeration Tonnage:** Total cooling capacity: $$\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m} \cdot RE = \frac{2.5}{60} \times 136.06 = 5.669 \, kW$$ $$TR = \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{3.517} = \frac{5.669}{3.517} = 1.612 \, TR$$ **Cross-check using compressor input:** $$\eta_{motor-coupling} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot w_c}{P_{input}} = \frac{2.5/60 \times 34.46}{1.2} = 1.196$$ > **Logical Step:** The cross-check $> 1$ implies either the compressor has not been idealized (real friction/electrical losses) or the readings include *combined* power. For KTU board exams, present both ideal and actual results clearly. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU 14-Mark Question Variations | Variation Type | Given | Required | Difficulty | |----------------|-------|----------|------------| | **Type 1** | h₁, h₂, h₃ (=h₄) | RE, w_c, COP | Easy | | **Type 2** | P-s at compressor, saturated states | All enthalpies via tables, COP, η_II | Medium | | **Type 3** | Power input, mass flow, condenser & evaporator pressures | TR, COP, COP_carnot, η_II | Hard | | **Type 4** | Effect of sub-cooling & superheat on COP | Comparison of two scenarios | Conceptual | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Refrigeration Test Rig (Functional Architecture) The test rig is essentially the **VCRS hardware plus instrumentation**. The Mermaid block diagram below captures the **functional flow** of the refrigerant and measurement points: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Compressor
Electric Motor Driven] -->|Discharge
State 2| B[Condenser
Air Cooled] B -->|Liquid Out
State 3| C[Dryer Filter] C --> D[Sight Glass
Moisture Indicator] D -->|Liquid In| E[Expansion Valve
TXV or Capillary] E -->|Low P Liquid
State 4| F[Evaporator
Finned Coil] F -->|Vapour Out
State 1| A G[Pressure Gauge P1
Compressor Inlet] -.-> A H[Pressure Gauge P2
Compressor Outlet] -.-> B I[Thermocouple T1
Compressor Inlet] -.-> A J[Thermocouple T2
Compressor Outlet] -.-> B K[Thermocouple T3
Condenser Exit] -.-> D L[Thermocouple T4
Evaporator Inlet] -.-> F M[Wattmeter
Power Input] -.-> A N[Flow Meter
Refrigerant m_dot] -.-> E subgraph Measurement_Loop["Instrumentation and Data Acquisition"] G H I J K L M N end ``` ## 4.2 Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) Diagram of the Ideal VCRS A **P-h diagram** is the most important graphical tool used in KTU board exams for this experiment. The cycle traces a *clockwise* path on the P-h chart: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PH_Diagram["PRESSURE ENTHALPY DIAGRAM OF R-134a"] direction TB A1["Point 4
Evaporator Inlet
Low P, Two Phase
h4 = h3"] -->|Process 4 to 1
Evaporation
Constant Low P| A2["Point 1
Compressor Inlet
Low P, Saturated Vapour
h1"] A2 -->|Process 1 to 2
Isentropic Compression
Entropy Constant| A3["Point 2
Compressor Outlet
High P, Superheated
h2"] A3 -->|Process 2 to 3
Condensation
Constant High P| A4["Point 3
Condenser Exit
High P, Saturated Liquid
h3"] A4 -->|Process 3 to 4
Isenthalpic Throttling
h3 equals h4| A1 end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Areas in the P-h Diagram for KTU Marks:** > - **Area under process 4→1** (constant low pressure) = **Refrigerating Effect = $h_1 - h_4$** > - **Vertical distance 1→2** = **Compressor Work = $h_2 - h_1$** > - **Area under 2→3** = **Heat Rejected in Condenser = $h_2 - h_3$** > - **Process 3→4** is a **vertical line** (constant h) — the only irreversible step. ## 4.3 Physical Test Rig Layout (Lab-Setup Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PhysicalRig["REFRIGERATION TEST RIG - PHYSICAL LAYOUT"] direction TB COMP["Reciprocating Hermetic Compressor
Mounted on rubber isolators"] COND["Finned Tube Condenser
With Axial Fan"] EVAP["Finned Tube Evaporator
Inside Test Chamber"] TXV["Capillary Tube / TXV
Throttling Device"] DRIER["Filter Drier"] SG["Sight Glass"] ACC["Liquid Receiver"] COMP -- Hot Discharge Line --> COND COND -- Liquid Line --> DRIER DRIER --> SG SG --> ACC ACC --> TXV TXV -- Low P Two Phase --> EVAP EVAP -- Suction Line --> COMP end subgraph Instrumentation["INSTRUMENTATION PANEL"] direction TB PG1["Pressure Gauge 1
LP Side"] PG2["Pressure Gauge 2
HP Side"] TH1["Digital Thermometer T1"] TH2["Digital Thermometer T2"] TH3["Digital Thermometer T3"] TH4["Digital Thermometer T4"] WM["Wattmeter 0 to 2 kW"] VFD["Variac / Voltage Stabilizer"] HMD["Humidity and Dry Bulb Sensors
In Test Chamber"] end COMP -.-> PG1 COND -.-> PG2 EVAP -.-> TH1 COMP -.-> TH2 COND -.-> TH3 TXV -.-> TH4 COMP -.-> WM VFD -.-> COMP EVAP -.-> HMD ``` ## 4.4 Temperature-Entropy (T-s) Diagram of the Cycle For board exam completeness, the T-s diagram is also drawn: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TS_Diagram["TEMPERATURE ENTROPY DIAGRAM OF IDEAL VCRS"] direction TB B1["Point 4
Two Phase Mix
s4 greater than s3"] -->|Evaporation
Constant T evap| B2["Point 1
Saturated Vapour
s1"] B2 -->|Isentropic Compression
Vertical Line| B3["Point 2
Superheated Vapour
s2 equals s1"] B3 -->|Condensation
Constant T cond| B4["Point 3
Saturated Liquid
s3"] B4 -->|Throttling
Irreversible s rises| B1 end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Difference Between P-h and T-s:** > - In **P-h diagram**, process 1→2 and 3→4 are **not** vertical (since enthalpy changes). > - In **T-s diagram**, process 1→2 (isentropic) is **vertical**, and 3→4 (irreversible) is a **horizontal-ish** line where entropy *increases*. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Short-answer questions requiring precise, textbook definitions. ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: Define the term "Coefficient of Performance" (COP) of a refrigerator. Why is the COP of a refrigerator always greater than 1?** **Model Answer:** The Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigerating effect (heat removed from the cold space) to the work input required to achieve it. $$COP = \frac{\text{Refrigerating Effect (RE)}}{\text{Work Input } (W_c)} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ > The COP of a refrigerator is **always greater than 1** because the *desired output* (heat extracted from the cold space) is **not** equal to the work input. In fact, a refrigerator can extract **3 to 5 times** more heat from the cold space than the work supplied. This is a fundamental thermodynamic advantage — refrigeration is *more efficient* than direct heat-to-work conversion (which is limited to 100 % by the First Law). [3 marks] ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: What is the significance of sub-cooling and superheat in a Vapour Compression Refrigeration System? State typical values used in test rig experiments.** **Model Answer:** **Sub-cooling** is the process of cooling the condensed refrigerant **below its saturation temperature** at the condenser pressure. It increases the refrigerating effect (since $h_4 = h_3$ decreases) and ensures that **only liquid** enters the expansion device, preventing flash gas that reduces capacity. **Superheat** is the process of heating the refrigerant vapour **above its saturation temperature** at the evaporator pressure. It ensures that **only vapour** enters the compressor, preventing liquid slugging that can damage the compressor. > **Typical Test-Rig Values:** Sub-cooling = **5 K**, Superheat = **5 K**. These are standard reference values per **IS:666** and **ASHRAE 23** test conditions. [3 marks] ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern (ESE Module — Internal Choice):** Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives. ### ⭐ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023 + Dec 2023 Pattern] **Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a vapour compression refrigeration test rig. List the instruments used and derive the expression for the COP.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Construction, Working & Sketch **Working of VCRS Test Rig (5 marks):** 1. **Compressor** (Hermetically sealed reciprocating type): The low-pressure, low-temperature refrigerant vapour from the evaporator is drawn in via the suction line and compressed to a high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour. The compressor is driven by a single-phase or three-phase electric motor through a belt-drive coupling. 2. **Condenser** (Air-cooled finned tube with forced draft fan): The hot, high-pressure vapour from the compressor rejects heat to the ambient air (sensible + latent heat). It first desuperheats, then condenses to a saturated liquid, and finally sub-cools slightly below the saturation temperature. 3. **Expansion Device** (Capillary tube / Thermostatic Expansion Valve): The high-pressure sub-cooled liquid passes through the throttling device, where its pressure drops abruptly to the evaporator pressure. This is an **isenthalpic** process ($h_3 = h_4$). The refrigerant emerges as a low-quality two-phase mixture. 4. **Evaporator** (Finned coil inside a test chamber): The low-pressure liquid-vapour mixture absorbs heat from the test chamber (which simulates the refrigerated space), evaporates completely, and leaves as a (slightly) superheated vapour to be sucked by the compressor. **Instruments Used (2 marks):** - Two **Bourdon-type pressure gauges** — at compressor inlet (LP) and outlet (HP). - Four **K-type thermocouples** (or digital thermometers) — T₁ at compressor inlet, T₂ at compressor outlet, T₃ at condenser exit, T₄ at evaporator inlet. - One **Wattmeter** (0–2 kW) — measures compressor electrical input. - One **Variac** — regulates compressor voltage to vary load. - Optional: **Flow meter** for refrigerant mass flow rate, **Humidity sensors** in test chamber. > **Sketch (refer to Section 4.3 above for full diagram)** — The expected board diagram shows the four components in a closed loop with all instruments labelled at their respective state points. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Derivation of COP The derivation is **identical to Section 3.1** above: **Step 1 — SFEE on Evaporator** (steady-state, adiabatic, no work): $$\dot{m} \cdot h_4 + \dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m} \cdot h_1$$ $$\Rightarrow RE = h_1 - h_4 \, kJ/kg$$ **Step 2 — SFEE on Compressor** (adiabatic, work input $\dot{W}_c$): $$\dot{m} \cdot h_1 + \dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \cdot h_2$$ $$\Rightarrow w_c = h_2 - h_1 \, kJ/kg$$ **Step 3 — COP Ratio:** $$COP = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ > **Key Assumption for Ideal VCRS:** Compression is **isentropic** ($s_1 = s_2$). The isentropic efficiency of the compressor is given by: > $$\eta_c = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$$ > where $h_{2s}$ is the enthalpy at compressor exit for an *ideal* isentropic compression. [7 marks] --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 + June 2024 Pattern] **Q: In an experimental investigation of a refrigeration test rig using R-134a, the following readings were recorded at steady state:** | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Evaporator pressure, P₁ | **2.21 bar** (T_sat = −10 °C) | | Condenser pressure, P₂ | **9.61 bar** (T_sat = 40 °C) | | Compressor inlet temperature, T₁ | **−5 °C** (superheat = 5 K) | | Condenser outlet temperature, T₃ | **35 °C** (sub-cooling = 5 K) | | Mass flow rate of refrigerant | **3.0 kg/h** | | Compressor electrical input | **0.18 kW** | **From the R-134a property tables:** - $h_1 = 247.32 \, kJ/kg$, $h_2 = 277.40 \, kJ/kg$ (isentropic), $h_3 = h_f \text{ at } 40°C \approx 108.26 \, kJ/kg$ (sub-cooled correction: $h_3 \approx 105.29 \, kJ/kg$) **Required:** - **(a)** Calculate the **Refrigerating Effect (RE)**, **Compressor Work (w_c)**, **Actual COP**, and **Carnot COP**. - **(b)** Calculate the **Second-Law Efficiency**, **Refrigeration Tonnage (TR)**, and **compressor isentropic efficiency** if the actual $h_2 = 290.5 \, kJ/kg$. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Primary Performance Calculations **Step 1: Refrigerating Effect** $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3 = 247.32 - 105.29 = 142.03 \, kJ/kg$$ **Step 2: Compressor Specific Work (Ideal, Isentropic)** $$w_{c,isen} = h_{2s} - h_1 = 277.40 - 247.32 = 30.08 \, kJ/kg$$ **Step 3: Actual COP** $$COP_{actual} = \frac{RE}{w_{c,isen}} = \frac{142.03}{30.08} = 4.722$$ **Step 4: Carnot COP** Converting evaporator and condenser saturation temperatures to Kelvin: $$T_L = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \, K$$ $$T_H = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \, K$$ $$COP_{carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{50.00} = 5.263$$ [7 marks awarded for: correct identification of state points + 4 correctly solved equations + units] #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Advanced Performance Indices **Step 1: Second-Law Efficiency** $$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{carnot}} = \frac{4.722}{5.263} = 0.8972 = 89.72\%$$ > **Interpretation:** The actual cycle is achieving **~90 %** of the theoretical Carnot performance — an *excellent* VCRS design. **Step 2: Refrigeration Tonnage** Cooling capacity: $$\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m} \cdot RE = \frac{3.0}{3600} \times 142.03 = 0.1184 \, kW$$ Converting to TR: $$TR = \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{3.517} = \frac{0.1184}{3.517} = 0.03366 \, TR$$ > **Equivalent in kcal/h:** $0.1184 \, kW = 101.8 \, kcal/h$ — typical of a *small* lab-scale test rig. **Step 3: Compressor Isentropic Efficiency** Using the actual compressor outlet enthalpy $h_2 = 290.5 \, kJ/kg$: $$\eta_{c,isen} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{277.40 - 247.32}{290.50 - 247.32} = \frac{30.08}{43.18} = 0.6967 = 69.67\%$$ > **Interpretation:** The compressor is **~70 %** isentropically efficient — typical of a small reciprocating compressor (real-world range: 60–75 %). [7 marks for: correctly applying the three formulas, providing physical interpretation, and consistent units] ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks — A KTU Examiner's Perspective:** > > 1. **Temperature in Celsius, not Kelvin — Instant 2-Mark Loss.** When computing $COP_{carnot}$, students often write $T_L = -10$ instead of $T_L = 263.15 \, K$. *Always convert* — this is the **#1 most common mistake** flagged in KTU valuation reports. > > 2. **Confusing State Point 3 and 4.** Many students incorrectly assume $h_4 = h_f \text{ at evaporator}$ instead of $h_4 = h_3$ (since throttling is isenthalpic). This is a **3-mark penalty** in most valuation sheets. > > 3. **Mixing Up RE and W_c.** RE is heat *absorbed in the evaporator* ($h_1 - h_4$), while $W_c$ is compressor work ($h_2 - h_1$). Reversing them gives COP $< 1$ — physically meaningless and an automatic **2-mark deduction**. > > 4. **Ignoring Sub-cooling Correction.** When T₃ is below saturation, the student must use the *sub-cooled liquid enthalpy*, not $h_f$. A typical oversight costs **1 mark**. > > 5. **No Units in Final Answer.** KTU board examiners are instructed to deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units (kJ/kg, kW, TR, °C, K). *Always box your final answer with units.* > > 6. **Forgetting to Mention "Adiabatic" and "Negligible KE/PE"** assumptions in the SFEE derivation. Worth **1 mark** in the derivation step. > > 7. **Skipping the P-h Diagram.** A 14-mark question without a labelled P-h diagram typically loses **2–3 marks** because the diagram is a *compulsory* visualization expected by KTU examiners. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Flash-Card for KTU ESE — Refrigeration Test Rig** - [x] **VCRS = 4 components:** Compressor, Condenser, Expansion device, Evaporator. - [x] **COP formula (memorize verbatim):** $COP = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$. - [x] **Carnot COP (always Kelvin):** $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$. - [x] **Throttling is isenthalpic:** $h_3 = h_4$ — but entropy *increases* (irreversible). - [x] **1 Ton of Refrigeration = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min.** - [x] **Isentropic compression:** $s_1 = s_2$ (vertical line in T-s diagram, not in P-h). - [x] **Sub-cooling increases RE; superheat protects compressor.** - [x] **2nd Law efficiency:** $\eta_{II} = COP_{actual} / COP_{carnot} \times 100$. - [x] **Compressor isentropic efficiency:** $\eta_{c} = (h_{2s} - h_1) / (h_2 - h_1)$. - [x] **Typical test conditions:** $T_{evap} = -10°C$, $T_{cond} = 40°C$, sub-cool = 5 K, superheat = 5 K. - [x] **Realistic COP of small VCRS:** 2.5 to 4.5; 2nd-law efficiency 40–60 % for full systems, 80–90 % for *idealized* lab calculations. - [x] **State point locations (memorize):** 1 = compressor inlet, 2 = compressor outlet, 3 = condenser exit, 4 = evaporator inlet. - [x] **Diagrams required in exams:** P-h diagram (always), T-s diagram (often), Test rig layout (frequently). - [x] **Common refrigerants in KTU labs:** R-134a (most common in modern rigs), R-22 (older rigs). - [x] **Most-marked questions:** COP calculation, Carnot comparison, P-h plotting, and instrument identification. - [x] **Avoid:** Mixing Celsius with Kelvin, using $h_f$ at evaporator instead of $h_3 = h_4$, and forgetting units.

topics.title: Study and performance test on air conditioning equipment (Air Conditioning test rig)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL609 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-2
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and performance test on air conditioning equipment (Air Conditioning test rig)

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

An **Air Conditioning Test Rig** is a laboratory-scale, fully instrumented, closed-loop ducted apparatus designed to experimentally investigate, evaluate, and quantify the steady-state thermodynamic performance of various human-comfort air-conditioning processes — namely **cooling, sensible heating, humidification, and dehumidification** — by routing a controlled air stream successively through a chilled-water cooling coil, a finned electric heater, a water-spray humidifier, and a dehumidifying cooling coil, while continuously measuring the inlet and outlet **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)**, **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)**, air velocity, and electrical power consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCMEL609):** *A standard test rig consists of a duct fitted with a fan, cooling coil, heating coil, humidifier, dehumidifier, and air measuring devices to evaluate the performance of an air conditioning system by determining Sensible Heat Factor (SHF), Apparatus Dew Point (ADP), By-pass Factor (BPF), and Coefficient of Performance (COP).*

The performance is rated against the following standard metrics:

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensible Heat | SH | kW | Temperature reduction capacity |
| Latent Heat | LH | kW | Moisture removal capacity |
| Total Heat | TH | kW | Overall cooling/heating load |
| Sensible Heat Factor | SHF | — | Ratio of SH to TH |
| By-pass Factor | BPF | — | Coil inefficiency indicator |
| Apparatus Dew Point | ADP | °C | Effective surface temperature of coil |
| Coefficient of Performance | COP | — | Energy efficiency ratio (desired effect / work input) |
| Energy Efficiency Ratio | EER | — | Cooling capacity in BTU/hr per watt input |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Climate Customizer"

Imagine your classroom has a **"Climate Customizer Box"** mounted on the ceiling. You set the desired temperature and humidity on a small panel. Inside this box:

- **Cold Water Snake (Cooling Coil):** Chilled water (like ice-cold lemonade) flows through pipes. When hot room air blows across these pipes, the air **cools down** — similar to blowing on a hot spoonful of soup.
- **Heater Rod (Heating Coil):** An electric element (like a room heater coil) warms the air when the room is too cold.
- **Water Fountain (Humidifier):** A fine mist of water droplets is sprayed to add moisture to dry winter air — like the steam from a pressure cooker.
- **Super Cold Coils (Dehumidifier Coil):** When air passes over extra-cold coils, water vapor condenses (like droplets forming on a cold glass of water in summer), removing humidity.

The test rig is essentially this **"Climate Customizer Box"** in a controlled laboratory duct, with sensors, gauges, and an energy meter to scientifically measure *how well* each subsystem performs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this experiment matters:** In real-world HVAC design (hospitals, data centers, malls, server rooms), engineers must predict cooling/heating loads accurately. Under-sized systems cause discomfort; over-sized systems waste capital. This experiment teaches students to **quantify** the actual capacity of an AC plant from measurable parameters — the very skill expected of a B.Tech Mechanical Engineer in the HVAC industry.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following reference values are used throughout the experiment:

- **Specific heat of air:** $C_{p,a} = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$
- **Specific heat of water vapour:** $C_{p,v} = 1.88\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$
- **Latent heat of vaporization at 0 °C:** $h_{fg} = 2501\ \text{kJ/kg}$
- **Density of standard air:** $\rho_a = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Psychrometric relation:** $W = 0.622 \dfrac{p_v}{p_t - p_v}$ where $p_t = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$
- **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR):** $3.516\ \text{kW}$ of cooling effect

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-Wise Functional Breakdown

An Air Conditioning test rig typically consists of the following components arranged in a single rectangular duct through which air is forced by a centrifugal blower:

1. **Blower / Centrifugal Fan** — Provides a steady, measurable mass flow of air.
2. **Orifice Meter (with U-tube manometer)** — Used to calculate the velocity and mass flow rate of air using Bernoulli's principle.
3. **Cooling Coil (Chilled Water Type)** — Uses refrigerant-cooled or chilled-water tubes with fins to cool and dehumidify the air.
4. **Heating Coil (Finned Electrical Heater)** — An electric resistance heater used for sensible heating (no moisture change).
5. **Humidifier (Water Spray / Steam Injection)** — Atomizes water into fine droplets, which evaporate into the air stream, raising humidity.
6. **Dehumidifier Coil (Re-cooling coil)** — A secondary cooling coil used to remove moisture by condensing water vapor.
7. **Bypass Damper** — Allows a portion of air to bypass the coil to study the **By-pass Factor (BPF)**.
8. **Measuring Section** — Contains Dry Bulb and Wet Bulb thermometers at inlet and outlet of each component.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality:** Industrial chillers use chilled water from a chiller plant (a separate refrigeration cycle). The test rig typically has its own small chiller unit, making it a self-contained **"miniature central AC plant"** — perfect for lab study.

## 2.2 Air-Conditioning Processes on the Psychrometric Chart

| Process | What Changes | What Stays Constant | Psychrometric Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensible Cooling** | DBT ↓ | W (humidity) | Horizontal line to the left |
| **Cooling & Dehumidification** | DBT ↓, W ↓ | — | Diagonal line to lower-left |
| **Sensible Heating** | DBT ↑ | W | Horizontal line to the right |
| **Humidification (with heating)** | DBT ↑, W ↑ | — | Diagonal line to upper-right |
| **Adiabatic Humidification** | DBT ↓, W ↑ | h (approx.) | Line along constant wet-bulb / enthalpy |
| **Heating & Dehumidification** | DBT ↑, W ↓ | — | Diagonal line to lower-right |
| **Mixing of Two Air Streams** | DBT and W new values | — | Straight line on chart |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — at least 70% of KTU ESE numerical problems are solved using these equations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Humidity | $W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_v}{p_t - p_v}$ | kg water/kg dry air |
| 2 | Saturation pressure (from steam table) | $p_s$ as a function of DBT | kPa |
| 3 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{p_v}{p_s}$ | fraction or % |
| 4 | Air mass flow rate (from orifice) | $\dot{m}_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \Delta h}$ | kg/s |
| 5 | Orifice discharge equation | $\dot{m}_a = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_o}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \Delta h}$ | kg/s |
| 6 | Sensible Heat | $SH = \dot{m}_a \cdot C_{p,a} \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$ | kW |
| 7 | Latent Heat | $LH = \dot{m}_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_1 - W_2)$ | kW |
| 8 | Total Heat (Enthalpy change) | $TH = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$ | kW |
| 9 | Sensible Heat Factor | $SHF = \dfrac{SH}{TH}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Apparatus Dew Point | $ADP = T_{coil} - \dfrac{DBT_2 - ADP}{1 - BPF}$ (from chart) | °C |
| 11 | By-pass Factor | $BPF = \dfrac{DBT_2 - ADP}{DBT_1 - ADP}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Contact Factor | $CF = 1 - BPF$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Cooling Load in TR | $Q_{TR} = \dfrac{Q_{kW}}{3.516}$ | TR |
| 14 | COP (Cooling) | $COP = \dfrac{\text{Refrigeration Effect (kW)}}{\text{Work Input (kW)}}$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | COP (Heat Pump) | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{\text{Heating Effect (kW)}}{\text{Work Input (kW)}}$ | dimensionless |
| 16 | EER | $EER = \dfrac{\text{Cooling Effect (BTU/hr)}}{\text{Input Power (W)}}$ | BTU/hr·W |
| 17 | Enthalpy of moist air | $h = C_{p,a} \cdot T + W \cdot (h_{fg} + C_{p,v} \cdot T)$ | kJ/kg dry air |
| 18 | Effectiveness (heating/cooling coil) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{out} - T_{in}}{T_{source} - T_{in}}$ | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mind the signs!** For **cooling**, $T_1 > T_2$ and $W_1 > W_2$ → use $(T_1 - T_2)$ and $(W_1 - W_2)$ to get a *positive* heat removal. For **heating**, $T_2 > T_1$ — use $(T_2 - T_1)$ to keep heat addition positive.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Building Design (ISHRAE standards):** SHF determines the required cooling coil size and the position of chilled water valves.
- **Cleanroom Design (Pharma, Semiconductors):** ADP and BPF dictate the precision of temperature and humidity control — typically BPF < 0.05 is desired.
- **Data Center Cooling:** ASHRAE TC 9.9 recommends tightly controlled inlet conditions → test rig data validates coil selection.
- **Energy Auditing:** COP and EER quantify the operating cost savings of a VAV (Variable Air Volume) system vs. a CAV (Constant Air Volume) system.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Flow Rate of Air Through an Orifice Meter

A venturi/orifice is placed in the duct to measure air velocity. Consider an elemental horizontal section of duct with area $A_1$ (upstream) and $A_2$ (at the throat/orifice). Applying **Bernoulli's Equation** between points 1 and 2 (assuming incompressible, inviscid flow) and **Continuity Equation**:

**Step 1: Bernoulli's equation** (no pump, no elevation change):

$$
\frac{p_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = \frac{p_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2: Continuity equation:**

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $V_1$ in Bernoulli's equation:**

$$
\frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho} = \frac{V_2^2}{2} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \right]
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $V_2$ — the theoretical velocity at the throat:**

$$
V_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_1 - p_2)}{\rho \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \right]}}
$$

**Step 5: Introduce the coefficient of discharge $C_d$ to account for real flow losses:**

$$
V_{2,\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_1 - p_2)}{\rho \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \right]}}
$$

**Step 6: Express as mass flow rate. Let $A_o$ = throat area, $\Delta h$ = manometer head (in metres of air):**

$$
\dot{m}_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \Delta h} \quad \left[ \text{kg/s, when } \beta = A_o / A_1 \ll 1 \right]
$$

**Step 7: Convert to volumetric flow at standard conditions:**

$$
\dot{V}_a = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the manometer?** $p_1 - p_2 = (\rho_{man} - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot h$. For an inclined manometer with light oil, $\Delta h$ is read directly. For a U-tube water manometer, $\Delta h$ is the differential water column.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) from First Principles

The total cooling load on an air stream is the **rate of enthalpy removal**:

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_1 - h_2)
$$

Expand the moist-air enthalpy:

$$
h = C_{p,a} \cdot T + W \cdot (h_{fg} + C_{p,v} \cdot T)
$$

Therefore:

$$
h_1 - h_2 = C_{p,a}(T_1 - T_2) + W_1 \cdot h_{fg} + W_1 \cdot C_{p,v} \cdot T_1 - W_2 \cdot h_{fg} - W_2 \cdot C_{p,v} \cdot T_2
$$

Group the **sensible terms** and the **latent terms**:

$$
h_1 - h_2 \approx C_{p,a}(T_1 - T_2) + (W_1 - W_2) \cdot h_{fg} + (\text{small term})
$$

Define:

- **Sensible Heat:** $\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot C_{p,a} \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$
- **Latent Heat:** $\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_1 - W_2)$

Hence:

$$
SHF = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_s}{\dot{Q}_s + \dot{Q}_L}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model ESE Problem)

**Problem:** In an air-conditioning test rig, air enters the cooling coil at DBT = 35 °C, WBT = 24 °C and leaves at DBT = 18 °C, WBT = 15 °C. The orifice manometer reading is 12 mm of water and $C_d A_o = 0.0025$ m². Take $C_d A_o$ constant. Density of air = 1.2 kg/m³. Determine: (a) mass flow rate of air, (b) sensible and latent heat removed, (c) SHF, (d) cooling load in TR, (e) COP if compressor power input is 1.2 kW.

**Step (a): Mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m}_a = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \rho_a \Delta h} = 0.0025 \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.012}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a = 0.0025 \cdot \sqrt{0.0288} = 0.0025 \cdot 0.1697 = 4.24 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg/s} \cdot (\text{recheck units})
$$

> Correction — use manometer head in metres of water (12 mm = 0.012 m of water). Convert to head of *air* using $\Delta h_{air} = \Delta h_w \cdot (\rho_w/\rho_a) = 0.012 \cdot (1000/1.2) = 10$ m of air.

$$
\dot{m}_a = 0.0025 \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 10} = 0.0025 \cdot \sqrt{24} = 0.0025 \cdot 4.899 = 0.01225\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step (b): Sensible heat**

$$
\dot{Q}_s = 0.01225 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (35 - 18) = 0.01225 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 17 = 0.2093\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step (c): Latent heat** — need humidities from psychrometric chart or formulae:
- At 35 °C DBT, 24 °C WBT → $W_1 \approx 0.0145$ kg/kg (chart)
- At 18 °C DBT, 15 °C WBT → $W_2 \approx 0.0107$ kg/kg (chart)

$$
\dot{Q}_L = 0.01225 \cdot 2501 \cdot (0.0145 - 0.0107) = 0.01225 \cdot 2501 \cdot 0.0038 = 0.1164\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step (d): Total cooling load**

$$
\dot{Q}_T = 0.2093 + 0.1164 = 0.3257\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
Q_{TR} = \dfrac{0.3257}{3.516} = 0.0926\ \text{TR} \approx 0.093\ \text{TR}
$$

**Step (e): SHF and COP**

$$
SHF = \dfrac{0.2093}{0.3257} = 0.6427 \approx 0.64
$$

$$
COP = \dfrac{0.3257}{1.2} = 0.2714
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why is COP < 1 here?** In a *test rig* the cooling effect measured at the coil is small (a few hundred watts), but the *compressor* drives the entire chiller — losses in the chiller plant mean more work is consumed than the "useful" coil effect extracted. Compare with the COP of the **chiller unit** itself (typically 2.5–3.5).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Numerical Outcomes to Check

| Observation | Expected Trend | Possible Error |
|---|---|---|
| $\dot{m}_a$ computed from orifice | Should lie between **0.01 and 0.05 kg/s** for typical lab duct | Wrong manometer fluid; forgot to convert mm of water to m of air |
| DBT after cooling | Lower than inlet DBT | If not lower, thermocouple may be on the wrong side |
| WBT after cooling | Lower than inlet WBT | If higher, humidifier is leaking into the cooling section |
| SHF for human comfort (ASHRAE) | 0.6 – 0.8 | SHF > 0.95 → dry cooling, no latent load |
| BPF | 0.05 – 0.30 for a well-designed coil | BPF > 0.4 → coil fouled or undersized |

```mermaid

    E1 --> F1[Cooling Coil Chilled Water]
    F1 --> G1[Measuring Section 2 DBT2 WBT2]

    H1 --> I1[Measuring Section 3 DBT3 WBT3]
    I1 --> J1[Humidifier Water Spray]
    J1 --> K1[Measuring Section 4 DBT4 WBT4]
    K1 --> L1[Dehumidifier Cooling Coil]
    L1 --> M1[Measuring Section 5 DBT5 WBT5]
    M1 --> N1[Outlet Duct to Atmosphere]
    F1 -.Chilled water.-> O1[Chiller Plant Compressor Condenser Expansion Valve]
    O1 -.Return.-> F1
    H1 -.Power.-> P1[Energy Meter Electrical Supply]
    P1 -.Power.-> H1
    P1 -.Power.-> O1
```

```mermaid
    P3 -->|Adiabatic Humidification| P4[State 4: Humid Air DBT4 WBT4]

    P1 -.By pass Factor BPF.-> P2
```


## 4.4 State-Point Process Diagram (Mapping of DBT, WBT, W, h)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 1: DBT1, WBT1, W1, h1] -->|Sensible Heat SH1| B[State 2: DBT2, WBT2, W2, h2]
    B -->|Sensible Heat SH2 Heat Addition| C[State 3: DBT3, WBT3, W3, h3]
    C -->|Latent Heat LH1 Adiabatic| D[State 4: DBT4, WBT4, W4, h4]
    D -->|Latent Heat LH2 Dehumidification| E[State 5: DBT5, WBT5, W5, h5]
    A -.-|BPF Calculation| B
    B -.-|ADP Determination| E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Note:** These Mermaid diagrams represent the **functional architecture** of the test rig. The actual physical rig is a single horizontal duct of cross-section typically **30 cm × 30 cm** with each section roughly **0.5 m** in length, instrumented at five stations. The chilled water flow rate is separately measured by a rotameter, and the heater coil wattage is read directly from a digital wattmeter or computed from voltage × current readings.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) and state its typical range for human comfort applications.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Sensible Heat Factor is defined as the ratio of sensible heat to the total heat (sensible + latent) removed by the cooling coil:

$$
SHF = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_L}
$$

For human comfort air-conditioning, the recommended SHF lies in the range **0.6 to 0.8**, as per ASHRAE Standard 55. A value close to 1 indicates purely sensible cooling (no dehumidification), whereas a value below 0.5 indicates high latent load (very humid climate or high internal moisture generation, e.g., crowded auditoriums).

---

### **Q2. What is By-pass Factor (BPF) of a cooling coil? How is it related to Coil Contact Factor?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The **By-pass Factor (BPF)** of a cooling coil is the ratio of the difference between the leaving air DBT and the Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) to the difference between the entering air DBT and the ADP:

$$
BPF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}
$$

It represents the fraction of the entering air that effectively **escapes** the cooling process — i.e., passes through the coil without coming into proper thermal contact with the coil surface. The **Contact Factor (CF)** is the complementary fraction:

$$
CF = 1 - BPF
$$

A well-designed coil has a **BPF between 0.05 and 0.30**. A higher BPF indicates poor coil design, fouled fins, or insufficient face area. BPF is purely a function of the coil geometry and air velocity — it is **independent of the entering air condition**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Draw a neat labelled schematic diagram of an air-conditioning test rig and explain the function of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Air at DBT 38 °C and WBT 26 °C enters a cooling coil and leaves at DBT 20 °C and WBT 18 °C. The mass flow rate of air is 0.08 kg/s. Calculate: (i) Sensible Heat removed, (ii) Latent Heat removed, (iii) Total cooling load in kW and TR, (iv) Sensible Heat Factor, (v) Apparatus Dew Point if the By-pass Factor is 0.2. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

From psychrometric chart / steam table look-up at given conditions:
- $W_1 = 0.0185$ kg water/kg dry air (at 38 °C DBT, 26 °C WBT)
- $W_2 = 0.0131$ kg water/kg dry air (at 20 °C DBT, 18 °C WBT)

**(i) Sensible Heat:**

$$
Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot C_{p,a} \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 0.08 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (38 - 20)
$$

$$
Q_s = 0.08 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 18 = 1.4472\ \text{kW}
$$

**(ii) Latent Heat:**

$$
Q_L = \dot{m}_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_1 - W_2) = 0.08 \cdot 2501 \cdot (0.0185 - 0.0131)
$$

$$
Q_L = 0.08 \cdot 2501 \cdot 0.0054 = 1.0804\ \text{kW}
$$

**(iii) Total cooling load:**

$$
Q_T = Q_s + Q_L = 1.4472 + 1.0804 = 2.5276\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
Q_{TR} = \dfrac{2.5276}{3.516} = 0.7189\ \text{TR} \approx 0.72\ \text{TR}
$$

**(iv) Sensible Heat Factor:**

$$
SHF = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_T} = \dfrac{1.4472}{2.5276} = 0.5726 \approx 0.57
$$

**(v) Apparatus Dew Point:**

$$
BPF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{ADP} = T_2 - BPF \cdot (T_1 - T_2) + BPF \cdot 0
$$

Solving for $T_{ADP}$:

$$
0.2 = \dfrac{20 - T_{ADP}}{38 - T_{ADP}}
$$

$$
0.2 \cdot (38 - T_{ADP}) = 20 - T_{ADP}
$$

$$
7.6 - 0.2 \cdot T_{ADP} = 20 - T_{ADP}
$$

$$
0.8 \cdot T_{ADP} = 12.4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{ADP} = 15.5\ \text{°C}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a psychrometric chart, explain the air-conditioning processes of (i) sensible heating, (ii) cooling and dehumidification, and (iii) adiabatic humidification. Show the line of constant SHF and explain its significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a cooling and dehumidification test, the following data were observed:
- Inlet DBT = 40 °C, WBT = 28 °C
- Outlet DBT = 22 °C, WBT = 21 °C
- Air velocity at duct = 6 m/s
- Duct cross-section = 0.3 m × 0.3 m
- Compressor power input = 2.5 kW

Calculate: (i) mass flow rate of air, (ii) SH, LH and total cooling load, (iii) SHF, (iv) COP of the system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Mass flow rate:**

Area of duct: $A = 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.09\ \text{m}^2$

Volumetric flow: $\dot{V} = A \cdot V = 0.09 \cdot 6 = 0.54\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$

Mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V} = 1.2 \cdot 0.54 = 0.648\ \text{kg/s}$

**(ii) Heat calculations:**
From psychrometric chart: $W_1 = 0.0208$ kg/kg; $W_2 = 0.0160$ kg/kg

$$
Q_s = 0.648 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (40 - 22) = 0.648 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 18 = 11.72\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
Q_L = 0.648 \cdot 2501 \cdot (0.0208 - 0.0160) = 0.648 \cdot 2501 \cdot 0.0048 = 7.778\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
Q_T = 11.72 + 7.778 = 19.50\ \text{kW}
$$

**(iii) SHF:**

$$
SHF = \dfrac{11.72}{19.50} = 0.601
$$

**(iv) COP:**

$$
COP = \dfrac{19.50}{2.5} = 7.80
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common mistakes that cost marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm of water to m of air** in orifice calculations (use $\Delta h_{air} = \Delta h_w \cdot 1000/1.2$).
> 2. **Sign convention reversal:** Students write $(T_2 - T_1)$ for cooling, getting negative heat. Always define state 1 = inlet, state 2 = outlet and use *positive* $(T_1 - T_2)$ for cooling.
> 3. **Confusing SHF with BPF:** SHF depends on *entering air condition* and *coil ADP*; BPF depends only on *coil design*.
> 4. **Wrong $h_{fg}$ value:** Use 2501 kJ/kg at 0 °C; at higher temperatures it's slightly lower (e.g., 2406 kJ/kg at 30 °C). For B.Tech lab calculations, 2501 kJ/kg is acceptable.
> 5. **Unit of TR:** 1 TR = 3.516 kW (NOT 3.5, NOT 4.18). The "3.516" comes from 1 ton of ice melting per 24 hours × latent heat of ice.
> 6. **Forgetting $C_{p,v} \cdot T$** in enthalpy calculation → small error, but examiner may deduct 0.5 marks.
> 7. **Stating "BPF = 1 − SHF"** — this is **WRONG**. They are independent quantities.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Test Rig Order:** Blower → Cooling → Heating → Humidification → Dehumidification (the standard sequence; verify with your lab manual).
- 📌 **Five measuring stations** are used to log DBT, WBT at: (1) Inlet, (2) After cooling, (3) After heating, (4) After humidifier, (5) After dehumidifier.
- 📌 **Mass flow rate** is found from orifice + manometer using $C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \rho_a \Delta h}$.
- 📌 **SHF range** for human comfort: **0.6 to 0.8**; BPF for a good coil: **0.05 to 0.30**.
- 📌 **ADP is found** either from the psychrometric chart (intersection of the line through states 1 and 2 with the saturation curve) or algebraically from the BPF equation.
- 📌 **COP for cooling < COP for heat pump** for the same refrigeration cycle; relationship: $COP_{HP} = COP_{cooling} + 1$.
- 📌 **1 TR = 3.516 kW = 12000 BTU/hr** — keep this conversion on your fingertip.
- 📌 **$h_{fg}$ at 0 °C = 2501 kJ/kg**; at room temperature ≈ 2450 kJ/kg (use 2501 unless otherwise specified).
- 📌 **Enthalpy of moist air** $h = 1.005 T + W(2501 + 1.88 T)$ kJ/kg dry air — be sure to use **$T$ in °C** (since the term $1.88T$ is the steam-side sensible heat above 0 °C).
- 📌 **Why manometer fluid matters:** Water manometer gives head in mm of water → multiply by $1000/\rho_a$ to get equivalent air head.
- 📌 **Energy meter reading** in kWh divided by experiment time (h) gives average compressor power in kW.
- 📌 **Checklist before submission:** (i) All five sets of DBT/WBT readings, (ii) manometer reading, (iii) voltmeter and ammeter (or kWh + time), (iv) duct area, (v) atmospheric pressure if humidity is to be calculated from first principles.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Revision Tip:** Draw the **psychrometric process diagram** for any numerical problem *before* substituting values. Plot states 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 with clear arrows. KTU examiners give **partial credit** even when arithmetic goes wrong, provided the **process logic** is correctly shown on the chart.
